$\forall\mathcal{C}_1,\mathcal{C}_2\subseteq\mathcal{P}(X)$:
$\mathcal{C}_1\subseteq\mathcal{C}_2 \Rightarrow
\sigma(\mathcal{C}_1)\subseteq\sigma(\mathcal{C}_2)$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$\forall\mathcal{C}_1,\mathcal{C}_2\subseteq\mathcal{P}(X)$:
$\mathcal{C}_1\subseteq\mathcal{C}_2 \Rightarrow
\sigma(\mathcal{C}_1)\subseteq\sigma(\mathcal{C}_2)$.
nodes.proof_sketch: $\sigma(\mathcal{C}_2)$ is a $\sigma$-algebra and contains $\mathcal{C}_2\supseteq\mathcal{C}_1$. So $\sigma(\mathcal{C}_2)$ contains $\mathcal{C}_1$. By minimality of $\sigma(\mathcal{C}_1)$: $\sigma(\mathcal{C}_1)\subseteq\sigma(\mathcal{C}_2)$.

nodes.name:
%
    Singletons Generate the Full Power Set on Countable Sets
nodes.question:
Why does the $\sigma$-algebra generated by singletons equal
  $\mathcal{P}(X)$ when $X$ is countable?
nodes.statement_display:
($\subseteq$): $\sigma(\{\{x\}:x\in X\})\subseteq\mathcal{P}(X)$
  trivially.
  ($\supseteq$): let $A\subseteq X$; since $X$ is countable, $A$ is countable,
  so $A = \bigcup_{x\in A}\{x\}$ is a countable union of singletons, each in
  $\sigma(\{\{x\}:x\in X\})$, hence $A\in\sigma(\{\{x\}:x\in X\})$.
nodes.statement_tex:
($\subseteq$): $\sigma(\{\{x\}:x\in X\})\subseteq\mathcal{P}(X)$
  trivially.
  ($\supseteq$): let $A\subseteq X$; since $X$ is countable, $A$ is countable,
  so $A = \bigcup_{x\in A}\{x\}$ is a countable union of singletons, each in
  $\sigma(\{\{x\}:x\in X\})$, hence $A\in\sigma(\{\{x\}:x\in X\})$.
nodes.hint:
Countability of $A$ is essential — on uncountable sets singletons
  generate only the countable/co-countable $\sigma$-algebra.
nodes.formal:
$\forall$ countable non-empty set $X$:
$\sigma(\{\{x\}:x\in X\}) = \mathcal{P}(X)$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$\forall$ countable non-empty set $X$:
$\sigma(\{\{x\}:x\in X\}) = \mathcal{P}(X)$.
nodes.proof_sketch: Let $\mathcal{S} = \sigma(\{\{x\}:x\in X\})$. ($\subseteq$): $\mathcal{S}\subseteq\mathcal{P}(X)$ trivially. ($\supseteq$): Let $A\subseteq X$. Since $X$ is countable, $A$ is countable, so $A = \bigcup_{x\in A}\{x\}$ is a countable union of singletons. Each $\{x\}\in\mathcal{S}$ by construction, so $A\in\mathcal{S}$ by ($\sigma$3).

nodes.name: $\varepsilon$-Approximation
nodes.question: State the $\varepsilon$-Approximation Corollary.
nodes.statement_display: If $s = \sup A$, then $\forall \varepsilon > 0\; \exists a \in A$: $s - \varepsilon < a \leq s$.
nodes.statement_tex: If $s = \sup A$, then $\forall \varepsilon > 0\; \exists a \in A$: $s - \varepsilon < a \leq s$.
nodes.hint: Direct restatement of the $\varepsilon$-characterization with the upper bound condition made explicit.
nodes.formal: For every nonempty set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and every real number $s$, if $s=\sup A$, then for every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists $a\in A$ such that $s-\varepsilon<a\le s$.
nodes.negation_formal: There exist a nonempty set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, a real number $s$, and $\varepsilon>0$ such that $s=\sup A$ but no element $a\in A$ satisfies $s-\varepsilon<a\le s$.
nodes.contrapositive: If there exists $\varepsilon>0$ such that no $a\in A$ satisfies $s-\varepsilon<a\le s$, then $s \ne \sup A$.
aliases: approximation from below to the supremum

nodes.name: $\varepsilon$-Characterization of Supremum and Infimum
nodes.question: State the $\varepsilon$-characterisation of supremum.
nodes.statement_display: $s=\sup A$ iff: (i) $s$ is an upper bound of $A$; and (ii) $\forall\varepsilon>0\,\exists a\in A: a>s-\varepsilon$.
nodes.statement_tex: $s=\sup A$ iff: (i) $s$ is an upper bound of $A$; and (ii) $\forall\varepsilon>0\,\exists a\in A: a>s-\varepsilon$.
nodes.hint: The $\varepsilon$-test replaces abstract least-ness with a constructive approximation condition.
nodes.formal: For every nonempty set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and every real number $s$, $s=\sup A$ if and only if $s$ is an upper bound of $A$ and for every $\varepsilon>0$ there exists $a\in A$ such that $a>s-\varepsilon$. The dual statement for infimum is obtained by reversing the inequalities.
nodes.negation_formal: There exist a nonempty set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and a real number $s$ such that either $s=\sup A$ but one of the two conditions fails, or the two conditions hold and yet $s \ne \sup A$.

nodes.name: Additivity of Infimum
nodes.question: State Additivity of Infimum.
nodes.statement_display: $\inf(A+B)=\inf A+\inf B$ for nonempty bounded $A,B\subseteq\mathbb{R}$.
nodes.statement_tex: $\inf(A+B)=\inf A+\inf B$ for nonempty bounded $A,B\subseteq\mathbb{R}$.
nodes.hint: Negate: $\inf(A+B)=-\sup(-(A+B))=-(\sup(-A)+\sup(-B))=\inf A+\inf B$.
nodes.formal: For all nonempty bounded sets $A,B \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, $\inf(A+B)=\inf A+\inf B$.
nodes.negation_formal:
There exist nonempty bounded sets $A,B \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ such that $\inf(A+B) 
e \inf A + \inf B$.

nodes.name: Additivity of Supremum
nodes.question: State Additivity of Supremum.
nodes.statement_display: $\sup(A+B)=\sup A+\sup B$ for nonempty bounded $A,B\subseteq\mathbb{R}$.
nodes.statement_tex: $\sup(A+B)=\sup A+\sup B$ for nonempty bounded $A,B\subseteq\mathbb{R}$.
nodes.hint: Show $\sup A+\sup B$ is an upper bound; use $\varepsilon$-characterisation to show tightness.
nodes.formal: For all nonempty bounded sets $A,B \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, $\sup(A+B)=\sup A+\sup B$.
nodes.negation_formal: There exist nonempty bounded sets $A,B \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ such that $\sup(A+B) \ne \sup A + \sup B$.

nodes.name: Archimedean Property
nodes.question: State the Archimedean Property.
nodes.statement_display: For any $x\in\mathbb{R}$ there exists $n\in\mathbb{N}$ with $n>x$. Equivalently, $\mathbb{N}$ is unbounded in $\mathbb{R}$.
nodes.statement_tex: For any $x\in\mathbb{R}$ there exists $n\in\mathbb{N}$ with $n>x$. Equivalently, $\mathbb{N}$ is unbounded in $\mathbb{R}$.
nodes.hint: If $\mathbb{N}$ were bounded, $\sup\mathbb{N}$ would exist; but $\sup\mathbb{N}-1$ is not an upper bound, contradiction.
nodes.formal: For every real number $x$ there exists a natural number $n$ such that $n>x$. Equivalently, for every real numbers $x>0$ and $y$, there exists $n\in\mathbb{N}$ such that $nx>y$.
nodes.negation_formal: There exists a real number $x$ such that for every natural number $n$, $n\le x$.
nodes.contrapositive: If there exists $x\in\mathbb{R}$ with $n\le x$ for all $n\in\mathbb{N}$, then the Archimedean property fails.

nodes.name: Density of $\mathbb{Q}$
nodes.question: State density of $\mathbb{Q}$ in $\mathbb{R}$.
nodes.statement_display: $\mathbb{Q}$ is dense in $\mathbb{R}$: for any $a<b$ there exists $q\in\mathbb{Q}$ with $a<q<b$.
nodes.statement_tex: $\mathbb{Q}$ is dense in $\mathbb{R}$: for any $a<b$ there exists $q\in\mathbb{Q}$ with $a<q<b$.
nodes.hint: Use Archimedean to find $n$ with $1/n<b-a$; then $m=\lfloor na\rfloor+1$ gives the numerator $q=m/n$.
nodes.formal: For all real numbers $a,b$, if $a<b$, then there exists a rational number $q$ such that $a<q<b$.
nodes.negation_formal: There exist real numbers $a<b$ such that for every rational number $q$, either $q\le a$ or $q\ge b$.
nodes.contrapositive: If there exist real numbers $a<b$ with no rational number strictly between them, then $\mathbb{Q}$ is not dense in $\mathbb{R}$.

nodes.name: Density of Irrationals
nodes.question: State the corollary: Density of Irrationals.
nodes.statement_display: The irrationals are dense in $\mathbb{R}$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{cor:density-of-irrationals}
The irrationals are dense in $\mathbb{R}$.

nodes.name: Diameter Monotonicity
nodes.question: State Diameter Monotonicity.
nodes.statement_display: In metric space $(X,d)$: $A\subseteq B\Rightarrow\operatorname{diam}(A)\le\operatorname{diam}(B)$.
nodes.statement_tex: In metric space $(X,d)$: $A\subseteq B\Rightarrow\operatorname{diam}(A)\le\operatorname{diam}(B)$.
nodes.hint: Diameter = supremum of distances; monotonicity of sup applies.
nodes.formal: For every metric space $(X,d)$ and all sets $A,B \subseteq X$, if $A \subseteq B$, then $\operatorname{diam}(A) \le \operatorname{diam}(B)$.
nodes.negation_formal: There exist a metric space $(X,d)$ and sets $A,B \subseteq X$ with $A \subseteq B$ and $\operatorname{diam}(A) > \operatorname{diam}(B)$.

nodes.name: Existence of Square Roots
nodes.question: State the Existence and Uniqueness of Square Roots.
nodes.statement_display: $\forall a \geq 0\; \exists!\, x \geq 0$: $x^2 = a$.
nodes.statement_tex: $\forall a \geq 0\; \exists!\, x \geq 0$: $x^2 = a$.
nodes.hint: Existence: let $S = \{t \geq 0 : t^2 \leq a\}$; show $x = \sup S$ satisfies $x^2 = a$ by ruling out $x^2 < a$ and $x^2 > a$ via $\varepsilon$-characterization.
nodes.formal: For every real number $a\ge 0$, there exists a unique real number $x\ge 0$ such that $x^2=a$.
nodes.negation_display: $\mathbb{Q}$ fails: $\{r \in \mathbb{Q} : r^2 < 2\}$ is bounded but has no rational supremum.
nodes.negation_formal: There exists a real number $a\ge 0$ such that either no real number $x\ge 0$ satisfies $x^2=a$, or two distinct real numbers $x,y\ge 0$ satisfy $x^2=a$ and $y^2=a$.

nodes.name: Floor Lemma
nodes.question: State the Floor Lemma.
nodes.statement_display: For every $x\in\mathbb{R}$ there is a unique $m\in\mathbb{Z}$ with $m\le x<m+1$.
nodes.statement_tex: For every $x\in\mathbb{R}$ there is a unique $m\in\mathbb{Z}$ with $m\le x<m+1$.
nodes.hint: Existence from Archimedean; uniqueness since two consecutive integers cannot both satisfy the condition.
nodes.formal: For every real number $x$ there exists a unique integer $m$ such that $m\le x < m+1$.
nodes.negation_formal: There exists a real number $x$ such that either no integer $m$ satisfies $m\le x < m+1$, or two distinct integers do.

nodes.name: Monotonicity for Functions
nodes.question: State Monotonicity of Supremum for functions.
nodes.statement_display: If $A\subseteq B\subseteq X$: $\sup_{A}f\le\sup_{B}f$ and $\inf_{B}f\le\inf_{A}f$.
nodes.statement_tex: If $A\subseteq B\subseteq X$: $\sup_{A}f\le\sup_{B}f$ and $\inf_{B}f\le\inf_{A}f$.
nodes.hint: Restricting the domain can only shrink or preserve extrema.
nodes.formal: For all sets $A,B \subseteq X$ and all functions $f : X 	o \mathbb{R}$: if $A \subseteq B$ and $f$ is bounded above on $B$, then $\sup_{x\in A} f(x) \le \sup_{x\in B} f(x)$; if $f$ is bounded below on $B$, then $\inf_{x\in B} f(x) \le \inf_{x\in A} f(x)$.
nodes.negation_formal: There exist $A,B \subseteq X$ and $f : X 	o \mathbb{R}$ with $A \subseteq B$ such that either $f$ is bounded above on $B$ and $\sup_A f > \sup_B f$, or $f$ is bounded below on $B$ and $\inf_B f > \inf_A f$.
nodes.contrapositive:
If $\sup_A f > \sup_B f$, then either $A 
subseteq B$ or $f$ is not bounded above on $B$; if $\inf_B f > \inf_A f$, then either $A 
subseteq B$ or $f$ is not bounded below on $B$.
aliases: functional monotonicity of sup and inf

nodes.name: Monotonicity of Supremum and Infimum
nodes.question: State Monotonicity of Supremum and Infimum.
nodes.statement_display: If $A\subseteq B$ (nonempty, bounded): $\sup A\le\sup B$ and $\inf B\le\inf A$.
nodes.statement_tex: If $A\subseteq B$ (nonempty, bounded): $\sup A\le\sup B$ and $\inf B\le\inf A$.
nodes.hint: Every upper bound of $B$ is also an upper bound of $A$.
nodes.formal: For all nonempty sets $A,B \subseteq \mathbb{R}$: if $A \subseteq B$ and $A,B$ are bounded above, then $\sup A \le \sup B$; if $A,B$ are bounded below, then $\inf B \le \inf A$.
nodes.negation_display: $A\subseteq B$ does not force strict inequality: equality is possible.
nodes.negation_formal: There exist nonempty sets $A,B \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ with $A \subseteq B$ such that either both are bounded above and $\sup A > \sup B$, or both are bounded below and $\inf B > \inf A$.
nodes.contrapositive:
If $\sup A > \sup B$, then either $A 
subseteq B$ or one of the two sets is not bounded above; if $\inf B > \inf A$, then either $A 
subseteq B$ or one of the two sets is not bounded below.
aliases: set monotonicity of sup and inf

nodes.name: Negation Rule
nodes.question: State the Negation Rule for sup/inf.
nodes.statement_display: For nonempty bounded $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$: $\sup(-A)=-\inf A$ and $\inf(-A)=-\sup A$.
nodes.statement_tex: For nonempty bounded $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}$: $\sup(-A)=-\inf A$ and $\inf(-A)=-\sup A$.
nodes.hint: Negation flips the order; the new least upper bound is the negation of the old greatest lower bound.
nodes.formal: For every nonempty bounded set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, $\sup(-A) = -\inf A$ and $\inf(-A) = -\sup A$.
nodes.negation_formal:
There exists a nonempty bounded set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ such that either $\sup(-A) 
e -\inf A$ or $\inf(-A) 
e -\sup A$.

nodes.name: Nested Interval Property
nodes.question: State the Nested Interval Property.
nodes.statement_display: If $[a_1,b_1]\supseteq[a_2,b_2]\supseteq\cdots$ are nonempty closed intervals, then $\bigcap_{n=1}^\infty[a_n,b_n]\neq\varnothing$.
nodes.statement_tex: If $[a_1,b_1]\supseteq[a_2,b_2]\supseteq\cdots$ are nonempty closed intervals, then $\bigcap_{n=1}^\infty[a_n,b_n]\neq\varnothing$.
nodes.hint: $(a_n)$ is increasing and bounded above; $s=\sup\{a_n\}$ lies in every interval.
nodes.formal: For every sequence of nonempty closed intervals $[a_n,b_n]$ in $\mathbb{R}$, if $[a_1,b_1] \supseteq [a_2,b_2] \supseteq \cdots$, then there exists $x \in \mathbb{R}$ such that for every $n \in \mathbb{N}$, $x \in [a_n,b_n]$.
nodes.negation_display: Fails for open intervals: $\bigcap_n(0,1/n)=\varnothing$.
nodes.negation_formal: There exists a nested sequence of nonempty closed intervals $[a_n,b_n]$ such that for every $x \in \mathbb{R}$ there exists $n \in \mathbb{N}$ with $x \notin [a_n,b_n]$.
nodes.contrapositive: If $\bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty}[a_n,b_n]=\varnothing$, then the family is not a nested sequence of nonempty closed intervals.

nodes.name: Norm Bounds
nodes.question: State the corollary: Norm Bounds.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A,B \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ with $A \subseteq B$. Then
\[
\sup_{x\in A} \|x\|
\le
\sup_{x\in B} \|x\|.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{cor:norm-bounds}
Let $A,B \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ with $A \subseteq B$. Then
\[
\sup_{x\in A} \|x\|
\le
\sup_{x\in B} \|x\|.
\]

nodes.name: Scalar Multiplication ($c < 0$)
nodes.question: State scalar multiplication for sup/inf ($c<0$).
nodes.statement_display: For $c<0$: $\sup(cA)=c\inf A$ and $\inf(cA)=c\sup A$.
nodes.statement_tex: For $c<0$: $\sup(cA)=c\inf A$ and $\inf(cA)=c\sup A$.
nodes.hint: Scaling by $c<0$ reverses order; sup and inf swap roles.
nodes.formal: For every nonempty bounded set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and every real number $c<0$, $\sup(cA)=c\inf A$ and $\inf(cA)=c\sup A$.
nodes.negation_formal:
There exist a nonempty bounded set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and $c<0$ such that either $\sup(cA) 
e c\inf A$ or $\inf(cA) 
e c\sup A$.
nodes.contrapositive:
If either $\sup(cA) 
e c\inf A$ or $\inf(cA) 
e c\sup A$, then $c \ge 0$ or one of the hypotheses on $A$ fails.

nodes.name: Scalar Multiplication ($c > 0$)
nodes.question: State scalar multiplication for sup/inf ($c>0$).
nodes.statement_display: For $c>0$: $\sup(cA)=c\sup A$ and $\inf(cA)=c\inf A$.
nodes.statement_tex: For $c>0$: $\sup(cA)=c\sup A$ and $\inf(cA)=c\inf A$.
nodes.hint: Scaling by $c>0$ preserves order; extrema scale by $c$.
nodes.formal: For every nonempty bounded set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and every real number $c>0$, $\sup(cA)=c\sup A$ and $\inf(cA)=c\inf A$.
nodes.negation_formal:
There exist a nonempty bounded set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and $c>0$ such that either $\sup(cA) 
e c\sup A$ or $\inf(cA) 
e c\inf A$.
nodes.contrapositive:
If either $\sup(cA) 
e c\sup A$ or $\inf(cA) 
e c\inf A$, then $c \le 0$ or one of the hypotheses on $A$ fails.

nodes.name: Supremum of a Difference
nodes.question: State the Supremum of a Difference.
nodes.statement_display: $\sup(A - B) = \sup A - \inf B$ and $\inf(A-B) = \inf A - \sup B$.
nodes.statement_tex: $\sup(A - B) = \sup A - \inf B$ and $\inf(A-B) = \inf A - \sup B$.
nodes.hint: $A - B = A + (-B)$; apply additivity then negation rule.
nodes.formal: For all nonempty bounded sets $A,B \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, $\sup(A-B)=\sup A-\inf B$ and $\inf(A-B)=\inf A-\sup B$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$\forall\, A, B \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ nonempty and bounded:
$\sup\{a - b : a \in A,\, b \in B\} = \sup A - \inf B$.
nodes.negation_display: Common error: $\sup(A-B) = \sup A - \sup B$. To maximise $a - b$, minimise $b$, so $\inf B$, not $\sup B$.
nodes.negation_formal:
There exist nonempty bounded sets $A,B \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ such that either $\sup(A-B) 
e \sup A-\inf B$ or $\inf(A-B) 
e \inf A-\sup B$.

nodes.name: Uniqueness of Supremum and Infimum
nodes.question: State uniqueness of supremum and infimum.
nodes.statement_display: If $\sup A$ exists it is unique; if $\inf A$ exists it is unique.
nodes.statement_tex: If $\sup A$ exists it is unique; if $\inf A$ exists it is unique.
nodes.hint: If $s,s'$ are both suprema: $s\le s'$ and $s'\le s$, so $s=s'$.
nodes.proposition: Supremum(A,s) \land Supremum(A,t) \to s=t;\quad Infimum(A,s) \land Infimum(A,t) \to s=t
nodes.formal: For every set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, if $s$ and $t$ are both suprema of $A$, then $s=t$; if $u$ and $v$ are both infima of $A$, then $u=v$.
nodes.negation_formal: There exists a set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ with two distinct suprema or two distinct infima.

nodes.name:
%
    Three Equivalent Forms of Continuity
nodes.question:
State the three equivalent forms of continuity of $f$
  at $c \in A$.
nodes.statement_display:
(i) $\varepsilon$-$\delta$: $\forall\varepsilon>0\,
  \exists\delta>0\,\forall x\in A:\,|x-c|<\delta\Rightarrow
  |f(x)-f(c)|<\varepsilon$.
  (ii) Neighbourhood: $f(A\cap V_\delta(c))\subseteq
  V_\varepsilon(f(c))$.
  (iii) Sequential: $x_n\to c\Rightarrow f(x_n)\to f(c)$.
nodes.statement_tex:
(i) $\varepsilon$-$\delta$: $\forall\varepsilon>0\,
  \exists\delta>0\,\forall x\in A:\,|x-c|<\delta\Rightarrow
  |f(x)-f(c)|<\varepsilon$.
  (ii) Neighbourhood: $f(A\cap V_\delta(c))\subseteq
  V_\varepsilon(f(c))$.
  (iii) Sequential: $x_n\to c\Rightarrow f(x_n)\to f(c)$.
nodes.hint:
(i)$\iff$(ii) is notation only.
  (i)$\iff$(iii) is the continuity analogue of Bartle 4.1.8,
  but sequences may now include $x_n = c$.
nodes.proof_sketch:
1. (i)$\Rightarrow$(ii): Fix $\varepsilon_0$, get $\delta_0$
from (i). Any $x_0 \in A \cap V_{\delta_0}(c)$ satisfies
$f(x_0) \in V_{\varepsilon_0}(f(c))$; arbitrariness gives the inclusion.
2. (ii)$\Rightarrow$(iii): Fix $\varepsilon$, get $\delta$ from (ii).
Sequential characterisation gives $N$ with $x_n \in V_\delta(c)$ for
$n \geq N$; neighbourhood condition pulls $f(x_n) \in V_\varepsilon(f(c))$.
3. (iii)$\Rightarrow$(i): Contrapositive. Failure of (i) at $\varepsilon_0$
yields $(x_n)$ via $\delta = 1/n$ with $x_n \to c$ but
$f(x_n) \not\to f(c)$, contradicting~(iii).

nodes.name: Absolute Maximum and Absolute Minimum
nodes.question: What does it mean for a function to have an absolute maximum or minimum on a set?
nodes.statement_display: An absolute maximum is attained at some \(x^{*}\in A\) with \(f(x^{*})\ge f(x)\) for all \(x\in A\). An absolute minimum is attained at some \(x_{*}\in A\) with \(f(x_{*})\le f(x)\) for all \(x\in A\).
nodes.statement_tex: An absolute maximum is attained at some \(x^{*}\in A\) with \(f(x^{*})\ge f(x)\) for all \(x\in A\). An absolute minimum is attained at some \(x_{*}\in A\) with \(f(x_{*})\le f(x)\) for all \(x\in A\).
nodes.hint: “Absolute” means global on the whole set, not merely local.
nodes.formal:
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) and let \(f : A \to \mathbb{R}\).
\[
f \text{ has an absolute maximum on } A
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\exists x^{*}\in A\;\forall x\in A,\ f(x^{*})\ge f(x),
\]
and
\[
f \text{ has an absolute minimum on } A
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\exists x_{*}\in A\;\forall x\in A,\ f(x_{*})\le f(x).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) and let \(f : A \to \mathbb{R}\).
\[
f \text{ has an absolute maximum on } A
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\exists x^{*}\in A\;\forall x\in A,\ f(x^{*})\ge f(x),
\]
and
\[
f \text{ has an absolute minimum on } A
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\exists x_{*}\in A\;\forall x\in A,\ f(x_{*})\le f(x).
\]

nodes.name: Algebra of Continuity at a Point
nodes.question: Which algebraic operations preserve continuity at a point?
nodes.statement_display: If \(f\) and \(g\) are continuous at \(c\), then \(f+g\), \(f-g\), \(fg\), and \(bf\) are continuous at \(c\). If \(h\) is continuous at \(c\) and never vanishes on \(A\), then \(f/h\) is continuous at \(c\).
nodes.statement_tex: If \(f\) and \(g\) are continuous at \(c\), then \(f+g\), \(f-g\), \(fg\), and \(bf\) are continuous at \(c\). If \(h\) is continuous at \(c\) and never vanishes on \(A\), then \(f/h\) is continuous at \(c\).
nodes.hint: Reduce continuity to limits at the point, then apply the algebra of limits of functions.
nodes.formal:
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f,g : A \to \mathbb{R}\), let \(b \in \mathbb{R}\), and let \(c \in A\).
  1. \[ \bigl(f \text{ is continuous at } c \ \wedge\ g \text{ is continuous at } c\bigr) \Rightarrow \bigl(f+g,\ f-g,\ fg,\ bf \text{ are continuous at } c\bigr). \]
  2. \[ \bigl(f \text{ is continuous at } c \ \wedge\ h \text{ is continuous at } c \ \wedge\ \forall x\in A,\ h(x)\neq 0\bigr) \Rightarrow \bigl(f/h \text{ is continuous at } c\bigr). \]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f,g : A \to \mathbb{R}\), let \(b \in \mathbb{R}\), and let \(c \in A\).
  1. \[ \bigl(f \text{ is continuous at } c \ \wedge\ g \text{ is continuous at } c\bigr) \Rightarrow \bigl(f+g,\ f-g,\ fg,\ bf \text{ are continuous at } c\bigr). \]
  2. \[ \bigl(f \text{ is continuous at } c \ \wedge\ h \text{ is continuous at } c \ \wedge\ \forall x\in A,\ h(x)\neq 0\bigr) \Rightarrow \bigl(f/h \text{ is continuous at } c\bigr). \]
nodes.proof_sketch: 1. Rewrite continuity at c as the limit identities lim_{x->c} f(x)=f(c) and lim_{x->c} g(x)=g(c). 2. Apply the sum, difference, product, and scalar-multiple rules for limits to obtain continuity of f+g, f-g, fg, and bf at c. 3. For the quotient part, use continuity of h at c together with h(x) != 0 on A to get h(c) != 0, then apply the quotient rule for limits.
nodes.uses_text: def:continuous-at-a-point, thm:limit-function-sum, thm:limit-function-difference, thm:limit-function-product, thm:limit-function-quotient, thm:limit-function-scalar
implies_titles: Algebra of Continuity on a Set

nodes.name: Algebra of Continuity on a Set
nodes.question: Which algebraic operations preserve continuity on a set?
nodes.statement_display: If \(f\) and \(g\) are continuous on \(A\), then \(f+g\), \(f-g\), \(fg\), and \(bf\) are continuous on \(A\). If \(h\) is continuous on \(A\) and never vanishes on \(A\), then \(f/h\) is continuous on \(A\).
nodes.statement_tex: If \(f\) and \(g\) are continuous on \(A\), then \(f+g\), \(f-g\), \(fg\), and \(bf\) are continuous on \(A\). If \(h\) is continuous on \(A\) and never vanishes on \(A\), then \(f/h\) is continuous on \(A\).
nodes.hint: This is the pointwise theorem applied at an arbitrary \(c\in A\).
nodes.formal:
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f,g : A \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(b \in \mathbb{R}\).
  1. \[ \bigl(f \text{ is continuous on } A \ \wedge\ g \text{ is continuous on } A\bigr) \Rightarrow \bigl(f+g,\ f-g,\ fg,\ bf \text{ are continuous on } A\bigr). \]
  2. \[ \bigl(f \text{ is continuous on } A \ \wedge\ h \text{ is continuous on } A \ \wedge\ \forall x\in A,\ h(x)\neq 0\bigr) \Rightarrow \bigl(f/h \text{ is continuous on } A\bigr). \]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f,g : A \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(b \in \mathbb{R}\).
  1. \[ \bigl(f \text{ is continuous on } A \ \wedge\ g \text{ is continuous on } A\bigr) \Rightarrow \bigl(f+g,\ f-g,\ fg,\ bf \text{ are continuous on } A\bigr). \]
  2. \[ \bigl(f \text{ is continuous on } A \ \wedge\ h \text{ is continuous on } A \ \wedge\ \forall x\in A,\ h(x)\neq 0\bigr) \Rightarrow \bigl(f/h \text{ is continuous on } A\bigr). \]
nodes.proof_sketch: 1. Fix an arbitrary c in A. 2. Since f and g are continuous on A, they are continuous at c. Apply the pointwise algebra-of-continuity theorem to obtain continuity of f+g, f-g, fg, and bf at c. 3. For the quotient part, use that h is continuous on A and nonzero on A, hence continuous at c and nonzero there. Apply the pointwise quotient result. 4. Since c was arbitrary, the derived functions are continuous on A.

nodes.name: Algebra of Lipschitz Functions
nodes.question:
Which algebraic operations preserve the Lipschitz condition,
  and with what constants?
nodes.statement_display:
Always: $cf$ (constant $|c|K_f$), $f \pm g$ (constant
  $K_f+K_g$), $h \circ f$ (constant $K_hK_f$). With boundedness:
  $fg$ (constant $M_gK_f + M_fK_g$), $f/g$ when $|g| \geq k > 0$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Always: $cf$ (constant $|c|K_f$), $f \pm g$ (constant
  $K_f+K_g$), $h \circ f$ (constant $K_hK_f$). With boundedness:
  $fg$ (constant $M_gK_f + M_fK_g$), $f/g$ when $|g| \geq k > 0$.
nodes.hint:
Composition is always clean ($K_hK_f$, multiply the
  constants). Products need the add-and-subtract trick, which
  introduces $|f|$ and $|g|$ as coefficients.
nodes.formal:
\begin{align*}
(a)&\quad |cf(x)-cf(u)| \leq |c|K_f|x-u|.\\
(b)&\quad |(f\pm g)(x)-(f\pm g)(u)| \leq (K_f+K_g)|x-u|.\\
(c)&\quad |h(f(x))-h(f(u))| \leq K_h|f(x)-f(u)| \leq K_hK_f|x-u|.\\
(d)&\quad |(fg)(x)-(fg)(u)| \leq (M_gK_f + M_fK_g)|x-u|.\\
(e)&\quad |f/g(x)-f/g(u)| \leq (K_f/k + M_fK_g/k^2)|x-u|.
\end{align*}
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\begin{align*}
(a)&\quad |cf(x)-cf(u)| \leq |c|K_f|x-u|.\\
(b)&\quad |(f\pm g)(x)-(f\pm g)(u)| \leq (K_f+K_g)|x-u|.\\
(c)&\quad |h(f(x))-h(f(u))| \leq K_h|f(x)-f(u)| \leq K_hK_f|x-u|.\\
(d)&\quad |(fg)(x)-(fg)(u)| \leq (M_gK_f + M_fK_g)|x-u|.\\
(e)&\quad |f/g(x)-f/g(u)| \leq (K_f/k + M_fK_g/k^2)|x-u|.
\end{align*}
nodes.negation_display:
$f = g = \mathrm{id}$ on $\mathbb{R}$: both Lipschitz-1,
  but $fg = x^2$ is not Lipschitz (slope $2x$ unbounded).
nodes.proof_sketch:
%
  (a) $|cf(x)-cf(u)| = |c|\,|f(x)-f(u)| \leq |c|K_f|x-u|$. \\
  (b) Triangle inequality: $|(f+g)(x)-(f+g)(u)| \leq (K_f+K_g)|x-u|$. \\
  (c) TODO: chain $K_hK_f$. \\
  (d) TODO: add-subtract; bound by $M_gK_f + M_fK_g$. \\
  (e) TODO: show $1/g$ Lip with $K_g/k^2$; apply (d).

nodes.name: Algebra of Uniform Continuity on a Bounded Domain
nodes.question:
Which algebraic operations preserve uniform continuity on
  a \emph{bounded} domain?
nodes.statement_display:
All of: $f+g$, $f-g$, $cf$, and $fg$. Also $f/g$ if $g$
  is bounded away from zero. Boundedness of the domain forces $f$
  and $g$ to be bounded, which controls the cross-terms in the
  product estimate.
nodes.statement_tex:
All of: $f+g$, $f-g$, $cf$, and $fg$. Also $f/g$ if $g$
  is bounded away from zero. Boundedness of the domain forces $f$
  and $g$ to be bounded, which controls the cross-terms in the
  product estimate.
nodes.hint:
Key step: UC on bounded set $\Rightarrow$ bounded image.
  Then $|(fg)(x)-(fg)(u)| \leq M_f|g(x)-g(u)| + M_g|f(x)-f(u)|$
  and both terms are controlled.
nodes.formal:
$A$ bounded, $f, g$ UC on $A$
$\;\Rightarrow\;$
$fg$ UC on $A$.

Additionally: $|g| \geq k > 0$ on $A$ $\Rightarrow$ $f/g$ UC on $A$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$A$ bounded, $f, g$ UC on $A$
$\;\Rightarrow\;$
$fg$ UC on $A$.

Additionally: $|g| \geq k > 0$ on $A$ $\Rightarrow$ $f/g$ UC on $A$.
nodes.negation_display:
Product fails on unbounded domain: $x \cdot x = x^2$ not
  UC on $\mathbb{R}$.

nodes.name: Algebra of Uniform Continuity on a General Domain
nodes.question:
Which algebraic operations preserve uniform continuity on
  a general domain?
nodes.statement_display:
$f+g$, $f-g$, and $cf$ are UC whenever $f$ and $g$ are.
  Products fail in general: $x \cdot x = x^2$ is not UC on
  $\mathbb{R}$ even though $x$ is.
nodes.statement_tex:
$f+g$, $f-g$, and $cf$ are UC whenever $f$ and $g$ are.
  Products fail in general: $x \cdot x = x^2$ is not UC on
  $\mathbb{R}$ even though $x$ is.
nodes.hint:
$\delta = \min(\delta_f, \delta_g)$ works for sums and
  differences. Products need a bound on $f$ and $g$ to control the
  cross-terms.
nodes.formal:
$f, g$ UC on $A$
$\;\Rightarrow\;$
$f+g$, $f-g$, $cf$ UC on $A$.

The same $\delta$ works: for $f+g$ and $f-g$ take $\delta = \min(\delta_f, \delta_g)$;
for $cf$ take $\delta = \delta_f$ from $f$ with input $\varepsilon / (|c|+1)$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$f, g$ UC on $A$
$\;\Rightarrow\;$
$f+g$, $f-g$, $cf$ UC on $A$.

The same $\delta$ works: for $f+g$ and $f-g$ take $\delta = \min(\delta_f, \delta_g)$;
for $cf$ take $\delta = \delta_f$ from $f$ with input $\varepsilon / (|c|+1)$.
implies_titles: Algebra of Uniform Continuity on a Bounded Domain

nodes.name: Any Real Polynomial Is a Continuous Function
nodes.question: State the theorem: Any Real Polynomial Is a Continuous Function.
nodes.statement_display:
Let
\[
P(x)=a_0+a_1x+a_2x^2+\cdots+a_nx^n
\]
be a real polynomial.
Then $P$ is a continuous function.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:polynomials-are-continuous}

Let
\[
P(x)=a_0+a_1x+a_2x^2+\cdots+a_nx^n
\]
be a real polynomial.
Then $P$ is a continuous function.
nodes.proof_sketch:
%
  1. Write $P = \sum_{k=0}^n a_k x^k$; each term $T_k = a_k x^k$ is
     continuous (constant / power function / constant multiple rule). \\
  2. $P$ is a finite sum of continuous functions, hence continuous by
     the sum rule.

nodes.name: Bernstein Approximation Theorem
nodes.question: State the theorem: Bernstein Approximation Theorem.
nodes.statement_display:
\cite[Theorem~5.4.8]{BartleSherbertRealAnalysis4}
Let $f : [0,1] \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous and let $\varepsilon > 0$.
There exists $n_\varepsilon \in \mathbb{N}$ such that for all $n \geq
n_\varepsilon$,
\[
  |f(x) - B_n(x)| < \varepsilon
  \qquad \text{for all } x \in [0,1],
\]
where $B_n(x) = \sum_{k=0}^{n} f\!\left(\tfrac{k}{n}\right)
\binom{n}{k} x^k (1-x)^{n-k}$ is the $n$-th Bernstein polynomial for $f$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:bernstein-approximation}
\cite[Theorem~5.4.8]{BartleSherbertRealAnalysis4}
Let $f : [0,1] \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous and let $\varepsilon > 0$.
There exists $n_\varepsilon \in \mathbb{N}$ such that for all $n \geq
n_\varepsilon$,
\[
  |f(x) - B_n(x)| < \varepsilon
  \qquad \text{for all } x \in [0,1],
\]
where $B_n(x) = \sum_{k=0}^{n} f\!\left(\tfrac{k}{n}\right)
\binom{n}{k} x^k (1-x)^{n-k}$ is the $n$-th Bernstein polynomial for $f$.

nodes.name: Bolzano's Intermediate Value Theorem
nodes.question: State Bolzano's Intermediate Value Theorem.
nodes.statement_display: If \(f:I\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous on an interval \(I\), and if \(a,b\in I\) with \(f(a)<k<f(b)\), then there exists a point \(c\) between \(a\) and \(b\) such that \(f(c)=k\).
nodes.statement_tex: If \(f:I\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous on an interval \(I\), and if \(a,b\in I\) with \(f(a)<k<f(b)\), then there exists a point \(c\) between \(a\) and \(b\) such that \(f(c)=k\).
nodes.hint: Subtract \(k\) and reduce to the Location of Roots Theorem.
nodes.formal:
Let \(I\subseteq\mathbb{R}\) be an interval and let \(f:I\to\mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
f \text{ continuous on } I
\ \wedge\
a\in I
\ \wedge\
b\in I
\ \wedge\
f(a)<k<f(b)
\]
implies
\[
\exists c\in I
\text{ such that }
\min\{a,b\}<c<\max\{a,b\}
\text{ and }
f(c)=k.
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
Let \(I\subseteq\mathbb{R}\) be an interval and let \(f:I\to\mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
f \text{ continuous on } I
\ \wedge\
a\in I
\ \wedge\
b\in I
\ \wedge\
f(a)<k<f(b)
\]
implies
\[
\exists c\in I
\text{ such that }
\min\{a,b\}<c<\max\{a,b\}
\text{ and }
f(c)=k.
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
Set $g = f - k$. Continuity of $g$ is free from algebra of
continuous functions. $g(a) < 0 < g(b)$ by hypothesis. Location of
Roots gives $c$ between $a$ and $b$ with $g(c) = 0$, i.e., $f(c) = k$.
Second case: apply to $-f$.
nodes.uses_text: Algebra of continuous functions, Location of Roots Theorem
implies_titles: Every Value Between \(\inf f(I)\) and \(\sup f(I)\) Is Attained
Image of a Closed Bounded Interval

nodes.name: Bounded on a Set
nodes.question: What does it mean for a function to be bounded on a set?
nodes.statement_display: A function \(f:A\to\mathbb{R}\) is bounded on \(A\) if there exists \(M>0\) such that \(|f(x)|\le M\) for all \(x\in A\).
nodes.statement_tex: A function \(f:A\to\mathbb{R}\) is bounded on \(A\) if there exists \(M>0\) such that \(|f(x)|\le M\) for all \(x\in A\).
nodes.hint: Bounded means all function values stay inside a fixed finite interval centered at \(0\).
nodes.formal:
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) and let \(f : A \to \mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
f \text{ is bounded on } A
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\exists M>0\;\forall x\in A,\ |f(x)|\le M.
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) and let \(f : A \to \mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
f \text{ is bounded on } A
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\exists M>0\;\forall x\in A,\ |f(x)|\le M.
\]

nodes.name: Boundedness Theorem
nodes.question: What does continuity on a closed bounded interval guarantee first?
nodes.statement_display: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous on \([a,b]\), then \(f\) is bounded on \([a,b]\).
nodes.statement_tex: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous on \([a,b]\), then \(f\) is bounded on \([a,b]\).
nodes.hint: This is the first compactness consequence for continuous functions on closed intervals.
nodes.formal:
For all \(a,b\in\mathbb{R}\) with \(a\le b\), for every function \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\),
\[
f \text{ continuous on } [a,b]
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\exists M>0\;\forall x\in[a,b],\ |f(x)|\le M.
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
For all \(a,b\in\mathbb{R}\) with \(a\le b\), for every function \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\),
\[
f \text{ continuous on } [a,b]
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\exists M>0\;\forall x\in[a,b],\ |f(x)|\le M.
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
%
  1. Assume $f$ unbounded: choose $x_n \in I$ with $|f(x_n)| > n$. \\
  2. $(x_n) \subseteq [a,b]$ bounded $\xrightarrow{\text{B-W}}$
     subsequence $x_{n_k} \to x^* \in I$ (closed). \\
  3. Continuity: $f(x_{n_k}) \to f(x^*)$, so image subsequence bounded;
     but $|f(x_{n_k})| > n_k \to \infty$. Contradiction.

nodes.name: Composition of Continuous Functions
nodes.question: State the theorem: Composition of Continuous Functions.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $Y \subseteq \mathbb{R}$.
Let $f : X \to \mathbb{R}$ and $g : Y \to \mathbb{R}$ with $f(X)\subseteq Y$.
If $f$ is continuous at $x_0 \in X$ and $g$ is continuous at $f(x_0)\in Y$,
then the composition
\[
h=g\circ f : X \to \mathbb{R}
\]
is continuous at $x_0$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:composition-of-continuous-functions}

Let $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ and let $Y \subseteq \mathbb{R}$.
Let $f : X \to \mathbb{R}$ and $g : Y \to \mathbb{R}$ with $f(X)\subseteq Y$.
If $f$ is continuous at $x_0 \in X$ and $g$ is continuous at $f(x_0)\in Y$,
then the composition
\[
h=g\circ f : X \to \mathbb{R}
\]
is continuous at $x_0$.
nodes.proof_sketch:
%
  1. Fix $\varepsilon > 0$. Apply continuity of $g$ at $f(x_0)$: get
     $\delta_1 > 0$ with $|y - f(x_0)| < \delta_1 \Rightarrow
     |g(y) - g(f(x_0))| < \varepsilon$.\\
  2. Set $\varepsilon_0 := \delta_1$. Apply continuity of $f$ at $x_0$:
     get $\delta_0 > 0$ with $|x - x_0| < \delta_0 \Rightarrow
     |f(x) - f(x_0)| < \delta_1$.\\
  3. Chain: $|x - x_0| < \delta_0 \Rightarrow |f(x) - f(x_0)| <
     \delta_1 \Rightarrow |h(x) - h(x_0)| < \varepsilon$.

nodes.name: Continuity Characterised by Oscillation
nodes.question: How does oscillation characterise continuity at a point?
nodes.statement_display:
A function \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous at \(x_0\in E\) if and only if
\[
\omega(f;x_0)=0.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
A function \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous at \(x_0\in E\) if and only if
\[
\omega(f;x_0)=0.
\]
nodes.hint: Continuity means nearby values of \(f\) squeeze together. Oscillation packages that squeeze into one number.
nodes.formal:
Let \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : E \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(x_0\in E\). Then
\[
\Bigl(
\forall \varepsilon>0\;\exists \delta>0\;\forall x\in E:
|x-x_0|<\delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-f(x_0)|<\varepsilon
\Bigr)
\]
holds if and only if
\[
\lim_{\delta\to 0^+}\omega\bigl(f;U_\delta(x_0)\cap E\bigr)=0.
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
Let \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : E \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(x_0\in E\). Then
\[
\Bigl(
\forall \varepsilon>0\;\exists \delta>0\;\forall x\in E:
|x-x_0|<\delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-f(x_0)|<\varepsilon
\Bigr)
\]
holds if and only if
\[
\lim_{\delta\to 0^+}\omega\bigl(f;U_\delta(x_0)\cap E\bigr)=0.
\]
nodes.negation_display: If \(\omega(f;x_0)>0\), then some positive spread persists arbitrarily close to \(x_0\), so \(f\) is discontinuous there.

nodes.name: Continuity Criterion for Monotone Functions
nodes.question: Define the jump of an increasing function $f$ at $c$.
nodes.statement_display:
$j_f(c) = \lim_{x \to c^+} f(x) - \lim_{x \to c^-} f(x)$.
  Continuity at $c$ iff $j_f(c) = 0$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$j_f(c) = \lim_{x \to c^+} f(x) - \lim_{x \to c^-} f(x)$.
  Continuity at $c$ iff $j_f(c) = 0$.
nodes.hint:
The jump measures the gap in the graph at $c$. Positive
  jump = jump discontinuity; zero jump = continuous.
nodes.formal:
$f$ continuous at $c$
$\;\iff\;$
$f(c^-) = f(c) = f(c^+)$
$\;\iff\;$
$\sup\{f(x): x < c\} = f(c) = \inf\{f(x): x > c\}$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$f$ continuous at $c$
$\;\iff\;$
$f(c^-) = f(c) = f(c^+)$
$\;\iff\;$
$\sup\{f(x): x < c\} = f(c) = \inf\{f(x): x > c\}$.
nodes.proof_sketch:
1. $(\Rightarrow)$: continuity gives two-sided limit, so both one-sided
limits exist and equal $f(c)$; in particular they are equal. 2. $(\Leftarrow)$:
one-sided limits exist by \#116; ordering $f(c^{-}) \leq f(c) \leq f(c^{+})$ plus
$f(c^{-}) = f(c^{+})$ forces all three equal; hence two-sided limit $= f(c)$.
implies_titles: Discontinuities of a Monotone Function Are Countable

nodes.name: Continuity on a Set
nodes.question: What does it mean for $f$ to be continuous on a set $B \subseteq A$?
nodes.statement_display:
$f$ is continuous at every point of $B$ — the $\varepsilon$-$\delta$
condition holds pointwise for each $c \in B$, with $\delta$ permitted to
depend on both $\varepsilon$ and $c$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$f$ is continuous at every point of $B$ — the $\varepsilon$-$\delta$
condition holds pointwise for each $c \in B$, with $\delta$ permitted to
depend on both $\varepsilon$ and $c$.
nodes.hint:
Pointwise condition — $\delta$ may vary with $c$. Contrast with
uniform continuity where $\delta$ depends only on $\varepsilon$.

nodes.name: Continuous Extension to a Closed Interval
nodes.question: State the proposition: Continuous Extension to a Closed Interval.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f:(a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be a continuous function such that
\[
\lim_{x\to a^+} f(x)
\]
exists.

Define $\widetilde{f}:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ by
\[
\widetilde{f}(x)=
\begin{cases}
\lim_{x\to a^+} f(x), & \text{if } x=a,\\[4pt]
f(x), & \text{if } x\in(a,b].
\end{cases}
\]

Then $\widetilde{f}$ is also a continuous function.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{prop:continuous-extension-closed-interval}

Let $f:(a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ be a continuous function such that
\[
\lim_{x\to a^+} f(x)
\]
exists.

Define $\widetilde{f}:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ by
\[
\widetilde{f}(x)=
\begin{cases}
\lim_{x\to a^+} f(x), & \text{if } x=a,\\[4pt]
f(x), & \text{if } x\in(a,b].
\end{cases}
\]

Then $\widetilde{f}$ is also a continuous function.
nodes.proof_sketch:
%
  1. Case $x_0 \in (a,b]$: $\widetilde{f} = f$ there and $f$ is
     continuous, so $\widetilde{f}$ is continuous. \\
  2. Case $x_0 = a$: fix $\varepsilon > 0$; right-hand limit gives
     $\delta$ with $0 < x-a < \delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-L| < \varepsilon$;
     split $x \in [a,b]$ with $|x-a|<\delta$ into $x=a$ (trivial) and
     $x \in (a,b]$ ($|f(x)-L|<\varepsilon$).

nodes.name: Continuous Injective Map on a Closed Interval Is Strictly Monotone
nodes.question: When is a continuous function on $[a,b]$ injective?
nodes.statement_display:
Exactly when it is strictly monotone. Continuity plus
  injectivity forces strict monotonicity; conversely strict
  monotonicity implies injectivity.
nodes.statement_tex:
Exactly when it is strictly monotone. Continuity plus
  injectivity forces strict monotonicity; conversely strict
  monotonicity implies injectivity.
nodes.hint:
Non-monotonicity forces a local extremum; IVT then
  produces two preimages of some value, contradicting injectivity.

nodes.name: Continuous Inverse Theorem
nodes.question: When is the inverse of a function continuous?
nodes.statement_display:
If $f$ is strictly monotone and continuous on an interval $I$,
  then $f^{-1}$ is strictly monotone and continuous on $f(I)$.
nodes.statement_tex:
If $f$ is strictly monotone and continuous on an interval $I$,
  then $f^{-1}$ is strictly monotone and continuous on $f(I)$.
nodes.hint:
Continuity of $f$ + Preservation of Intervals ensures
  $f(I)$ is an interval with no gaps; a jump in $f^{-1}$ would
  create a gap in $f(I)$.
nodes.formal:
$f$ strictly monotone and continuous on $I$
$\;\Rightarrow\;$
$f^{-1} : f(I) \to I$ is strictly monotone and continuous on $f(I)$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$f$ strictly monotone and continuous on $I$
$\;\Rightarrow\;$
$f^{-1} : f(I) \to I$ is strictly monotone and continuous on $f(I)$.
implies_titles: Injective Continuous Function Is Strictly Monotone

nodes.name: Criterion for Continuity of a Monotone Function
nodes.question: When is a monotone function on $[a,b]$ continuous?
nodes.statement_display:
Exactly when $f([a,b])$ is the closed interval $[f(a),f(b)]$
  (or $[f(b),f(a)]$ if decreasing) — no gaps in the range.
nodes.statement_tex:
Exactly when $f([a,b])$ is the closed interval $[f(a),f(b)]$
  (or $[f(b),f(a)]$ if decreasing) — no gaps in the range.
nodes.hint:
Each jump discontinuity creates a gap; no gaps means
  no jumps means continuous.
nodes.formal:
$f$ monotone on $[a,b]$:
\;
$f$ continuous on $[a,b]$
$\;\iff\;$
$f([a,b]) = [\min(f(a),f(b)),\, \max(f(a),f(b))]$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$f$ monotone on $[a,b]$:
\;
$f$ continuous on $[a,b]$
$\;\iff\;$
$f([a,b]) = [\min(f(a),f(b)),\, \max(f(a),f(b))]$.

nodes.name: Discontinuities of a Monotone Function Are Countable
nodes.question: How many discontinuities can a monotone function have?
nodes.statement_display:
At most countably many. The jump intervals at distinct
  discontinuities are disjoint; each contains a rational; density
  of $\mathbb{Q}$ gives the injection into $\mathbb{Q}$.
nodes.statement_tex:
At most countably many. The jump intervals at distinct
  discontinuities are disjoint; each contains a rational; density
  of $\mathbb{Q}$ gives the injection into $\mathbb{Q}$.
nodes.hint:
Disjoint intervals $\hookrightarrow$ distinct rationals
  $\hookrightarrow$ countable.
nodes.formal:
$f$ monotone on $I$
$\;\Rightarrow\;$
$D := \{c \in I : f \text{ discontinuous at } c\}$ is countable.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$f$ monotone on $I$
$\;\Rightarrow\;$
$D := \{c \in I : f \text{ discontinuous at } c\}$ is countable.
nodes.proof_sketch:
1. WLOG $f$ increasing. 2. Each $c \in D$ gives non-empty
$J_c(f) = (f(c^-), f(c^+))$; pick $r(c) \in J_c(f) \cap \mathbb{Q}$ by density.
3. Jump intervals are pairwise disjoint (Jump Intervals corollary), so $r$ is
injective. 4. Injection $D \hookrightarrow \mathbb{Q}$, $\mathbb{Q}$ countable,
so $D$ at most countable.

nodes.name: Discontinuities of a Monotone Function Are of First Kind
nodes.question: What kind of discontinuities can a monotone function have?
nodes.statement_display:
Only discontinuities of first kind — both one-sided limits
  exist at every discontinuity. Oscillation is impossible for a
  monotone function.
nodes.statement_tex:
Only discontinuities of first kind — both one-sided limits
  exist at every discontinuity. Oscillation is impossible for a
  monotone function.
nodes.hint:
Monotonicity forces all nearby values to lie on the same
  side, so one-sided limits must converge. No oscillation is possible.
nodes.formal:
$f$ monotone on $E$, $a \in E$ a discontinuity
$\;\Rightarrow\;$
both $\lim_{x \to a^-} f(x)$ and $\lim_{x \to a^+} f(x)$ exist.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$f$ monotone on $E$, $a \in E$ a discontinuity
$\;\Rightarrow\;$
both $\lim_{x \to a^-} f(x)$ and $\lim_{x \to a^+} f(x)$ exist.
implies_titles: Jump Intervals of a Monotone Function Are Disjoint

nodes.name: Discontinuity Criterion
nodes.question: What is the Discontinuity Criterion?
nodes.statement_display:
$f$ is discontinuous at $c$ if and only if there exists a sequence
$(x_n) \subseteq A$ with $x_n \to c$ but $f(x_n) \not\to f(c)$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$f$ is discontinuous at $c$ if and only if there exists a sequence
$(x_n) \subseteq A$ with $x_n \to c$ but $f(x_n) \not\to f(c)$.
nodes.hint: One witness sequence suffices. The sequence converges; its image does not.
nodes.formal:
$\exists (x_n) \subseteq A : x_n \to c \ \text{and} \ f(x_n) \not\to f(c)
\iff f \text{ is discontinuous at } c.$
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$\exists (x_n) \subseteq A : x_n \to c \ \text{and} \ f(x_n) \not\to f(c)
\iff f \text{ is discontinuous at } c.$
nodes.negation_display:
Continuity requires \emph{every} sequence $x_n \to c$ to satisfy
$f(x_n) \to f(c)$ — a single bad sequence breaks it.

nodes.name: Discontinuity Set via Oscillation
nodes.question: How is the discontinuity set described in terms of oscillation?
nodes.statement_display:
For \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\),
\[
D(f)=\{x\in E:\omega(f;x)>0\}
=
\bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty}\left\{x\in E:\omega(f;x)\ge \frac{1}{n}\right\}.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
For \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\),
\[
D(f)=\{x\in E:\omega(f;x)>0\}
=
\bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty}\left\{x\in E:\omega(f;x)\ge \frac{1}{n}\right\}.
\]
nodes.hint: Combine the oscillation criterion for continuity with the Archimedean step \(r>0 \iff \exists n,\ r\ge 1/n\).
nodes.formal:
Let \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) and let \(f : E \to \mathbb{R}\). Then for every \(x\in E\),
\[
x\in D(f)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\omega(f;x)>0.
\]
Hence
\[
D(f)
=
\left\{x\in E:\omega(f;x)>0\right\}
=
\bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty}
\left\{x\in E:\omega(f;x)\ge \frac{1}{n}\right\}.
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
Let \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) and let \(f : E \to \mathbb{R}\). Then for every \(x\in E\),
\[
x\in D(f)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\omega(f;x)>0.
\]
Hence
\[
D(f)
=
\left\{x\in E:\omega(f;x)>0\right\}
=
\bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty}
\left\{x\in E:\omega(f;x)\ge \frac{1}{n}\right\}.
\]

nodes.name: Discontinuity on a Set
nodes.question: What does it mean for a function to be discontinuous on a set?
nodes.statement_display: A function \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) is discontinuous on \(E\) if and only if there exists at least one point \(x_0\in E\) at which \(f\) is discontinuous.
nodes.statement_tex: A function \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) is discontinuous on \(E\) if and only if there exists at least one point \(x_0\in E\) at which \(f\) is discontinuous.
nodes.hint: Continuity on a set is a pointwise condition at every point; discontinuity on a set is failure at some point.
nodes.formal:
Let \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) and let \(f : E \to \mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
f \text{ is discontinuous on } E
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\exists x_0\in E\;\exists \varepsilon_0>0\;\forall \delta>0\;\exists x\in E:
\bigl(|x-x_0|<\delta \ \wedge\ |f(x)-f(x_0)|\ge \varepsilon_0\bigr).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
Let \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) and let \(f : E \to \mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
f \text{ is discontinuous on } E
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\exists x_0\in E\;\exists \varepsilon_0>0\;\forall \delta>0\;\exists x\in E:
\bigl(|x-x_0|<\delta \ \wedge\ |f(x)-f(x_0)|\ge \varepsilon_0\bigr).
\]
implies_titles: Discontinuity on a Set Is Pointwise Failure Somewhere

nodes.name: Discontinuity on a Set Is Pointwise Failure Somewhere
nodes.question: How is discontinuity on a set related to pointwise discontinuity?
nodes.statement_display: A function \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) is discontinuous on \(E\) if and only if there exists a point \(x_0\in E\) where it is discontinuous. By the equivalence lemma, the bad point may be witnessed in epsilon-delta, sequential, or neighborhood form.
nodes.statement_tex: A function \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) is discontinuous on \(E\) if and only if there exists a point \(x_0\in E\) where it is discontinuous. By the equivalence lemma, the bad point may be witnessed in epsilon-delta, sequential, or neighborhood form.
nodes.hint: Set-level discontinuity is just one outer existential quantifier added to the pointwise notion.
nodes.formal:
Let \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) and let \(f : E \to \mathbb{R}\). Then the following are equivalent:
  1. \[ f \text{ is discontinuous on } E. \]
  2. \[ \exists x_0\in E\;\exists \varepsilon_0>0\;\forall \delta>0\;\exists x\in E: \bigl(|x-x_0|<\delta \ \wedge\ |f(x)-f(x_0)|\ge \varepsilon_0\bigr). \]
  3. \[ \exists x_0\in E\;\exists (x_n)\subseteq E: \quad x_n\to x_0 \quad\text{and}\quad f(x_n)\not\to f(x_0). \]
  4. \[ \exists x_0\in E\;\exists V \text{ a neighborhood of } f(x_0)\; \forall U \text{ a neighborhood of } x_0\; \exists x\in U\cap E: \quad f(x)\notin V. \]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
Let \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) and let \(f : E \to \mathbb{R}\). Then the following are equivalent:
  1. \[ f \text{ is discontinuous on } E. \]
  2. \[ \exists x_0\in E\;\exists \varepsilon_0>0\;\forall \delta>0\;\exists x\in E: \bigl(|x-x_0|<\delta \ \wedge\ |f(x)-f(x_0)|\ge \varepsilon_0\bigr). \]
  3. \[ \exists x_0\in E\;\exists (x_n)\subseteq E: \quad x_n\to x_0 \quad\text{and}\quad f(x_n)\not\to f(x_0). \]
  4. \[ \exists x_0\in E\;\exists V \text{ a neighborhood of } f(x_0)\; \forall U \text{ a neighborhood of } x_0\; \exists x\in U\cap E: \quad f(x)\notin V. \]

nodes.name: Epsilon--Delta Characterization of Discontinuity at a Point
nodes.question: What is the epsilon-delta characterization of discontinuity at a point?
nodes.statement_display:
A function \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) is discontinuous at \(x_0\in E\) in the epsilon-delta sense if and only if there exists \(\varepsilon_0>0\) such that for every \(\delta>0\), there exists \(x\in E\) with
\[
|x-x_0|<\delta
\quad\text{and}\quad
|f(x)-f(x_0)|\ge \varepsilon_0.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
A function \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) is discontinuous at \(x_0\in E\) in the epsilon-delta sense if and only if there exists \(\varepsilon_0>0\) such that for every \(\delta>0\), there exists \(x\in E\) with
\[
|x-x_0|<\delta
\quad\text{and}\quad
|f(x)-f(x_0)|\ge \varepsilon_0.
\]
nodes.hint: Negate continuity by reversing the quantifiers in order: \(\forall\exists\forall\) becomes \(\exists\forall\exists\).
nodes.formal:
Let \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : E \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(x_0\in E\). Then
\[
f \text{ is discontinuous at } x_0 \text{ in the \(\varepsilon\)–\(\delta\) sense}
\]
if and only if
\[
\exists \varepsilon_0>0\;\forall \delta>0\;\exists x\in E:
\bigl(|x-x_0|<\delta \ \wedge\ |f(x)-f(x_0)|\ge \varepsilon_0\bigr).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
Let \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : E \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(x_0\in E\). Then
\[
f \text{ is discontinuous at } x_0 \text{ in the \(\varepsilon\)–\(\delta\) sense}
\]
if and only if
\[
\exists \varepsilon_0>0\;\forall \delta>0\;\exists x\in E:
\bigl(|x-x_0|<\delta \ \wedge\ |f(x)-f(x_0)|\ge \varepsilon_0\bigr).
\]
implies_titles: Equivalent Formulations of Discontinuity at a Point

nodes.name: Equivalent Formulations of Discontinuity at a Point
nodes.question: What are the equivalent formulations of discontinuity at a point?
nodes.statement_display: For \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) and \(x_0\in E\), the epsilon-delta, sequential, and neighborhood characterizations of discontinuity at \(x_0\) are equivalent.
nodes.statement_tex: For \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) and \(x_0\in E\), the epsilon-delta, sequential, and neighborhood characterizations of discontinuity at \(x_0\) are equivalent.
nodes.hint: This is the failure-version of the standard equivalence theorem for continuity.
nodes.formal:
Let \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : E \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(x_0 \in E\). Then the following are equivalent:
  1. \[ \exists \varepsilon_0>0\;\forall \delta>0\;\exists x\in E: \bigl(|x-x_0|<\delta \ \wedge\ |f(x)-f(x_0)|\ge \varepsilon_0\bigr). \]
  2. \[ \exists (x_n)\subseteq E: \quad x_n\to x_0 \quad\text{and}\quad f(x_n)\not\to f(x_0). \]
  3. \[ \exists V \text{ a neighborhood of } f(x_0)\; \forall U \text{ a neighborhood of } x_0\; \exists x\in U\cap E: \quad f(x)\notin V. \]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
Let \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : E \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(x_0 \in E\). Then the following are equivalent:
  1. \[ \exists \varepsilon_0>0\;\forall \delta>0\;\exists x\in E: \bigl(|x-x_0|<\delta \ \wedge\ |f(x)-f(x_0)|\ge \varepsilon_0\bigr). \]
  2. \[ \exists (x_n)\subseteq E: \quad x_n\to x_0 \quad\text{and}\quad f(x_n)\not\to f(x_0). \]
  3. \[ \exists V \text{ a neighborhood of } f(x_0)\; \forall U \text{ a neighborhood of } x_0\; \exists x\in U\cap E: \quad f(x)\notin V. \]
nodes.proof_sketch: 1. Prove epsilon-delta discontinuity implies sequential discontinuity by choosing x_n with delta=1/n. 2. Prove sequential discontinuity implies epsilon-delta discontinuity by using the negation of convergence of f(x_n). 3. Rewrite the epsilon-delta condition in neighborhood language using open intervals around x_0 and f(x_0).
implies_titles: Discontinuity on a Set Is Pointwise Failure Somewhere

nodes.name: Every Value Between \(\inf f(I)\) and \(\sup f(I)\) Is Attained
nodes.question: What does IVT say on a closed bounded interval after extrema are known to exist?
nodes.statement_display: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous and \(k\) satisfies \(\inf f([a,b])\le k\le \sup f([a,b])\), then there exists \(c\in[a,b]\) such that \(f(c)=k\).
nodes.statement_tex: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous and \(k\) satisfies \(\inf f([a,b])\le k\le \sup f([a,b])\), then there exists \(c\in[a,b]\) such that \(f(c)=k\).
nodes.hint: Use the Maximum–Minimum Theorem to identify points attaining the infimum and supremum, then apply IVT.
nodes.formal:
Let \(a,b\in\mathbb{R}\) with \(a\le b\), and let \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
f \text{ continuous on } [a,b]
\ \wedge\
\inf f([a,b])\le k\le \sup f([a,b])
\]
implies
\[
\exists c\in[a,b]\text{ such that }f(c)=k.
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
Let \(a,b\in\mathbb{R}\) with \(a\le b\), and let \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
f \text{ continuous on } [a,b]
\ \wedge\
\inf f([a,b])\le k\le \sup f([a,b])
\]
implies
\[
\exists c\in[a,b]\text{ such that }f(c)=k.
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
EVT gives $x_m, x_M$ with $f(x_m)=m$, $f(x_M)=M$. Set
$g = f-k$; continuous on $J = [\min(x_m,x_M), \max(x_m,x_M)]$.
$g(x_m) = m-k \leq 0$, $g(x_M) = M-k \geq 0$. Three cases: $k=m$
gives $c=x_m$; $k=M$ gives $c=x_M$; $m < k < M$ gives strict
inequalities so Location of Roots applies on $J$.
nodes.uses_text:
Extreme Value Theorem, algebra of continuous functions,
Location of Roots Theorem
implies_titles: Image of a Closed Bounded Interval

nodes.name: Exponentiation Is Continuous, I
nodes.question: State the proposition: Exponentiation Is Continuous, I.
nodes.statement_display:
\cite[Proposition~9.4.10]{TaoAnalysis1}

Let $a>0$ be a positive real number.
Then the function $f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ defined by
\[
f(x):=a^x
\]
is continuous.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:exponentiation-continuous-base}
\cite[Proposition~9.4.10]{TaoAnalysis1}

Let $a>0$ be a positive real number.
Then the function $f:\mathbb{R}\to\mathbb{R}$ defined by
\[
f(x):=a^x
\]
is continuous.

nodes.name: Exponentiation Is Continuous, II
nodes.question: State the proposition: Exponentiation Is Continuous, II.
nodes.statement_display:
\cite[Proposition~9.4.11]{TaoAnalysis1}

Let $p$ be a real number.
Then the function $f:(0,\infty)\to\mathbb{R}$ defined by
\[
f(x):=x^p
\]
is continuous.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:exponentiation-continuous-exponent}
\cite[Proposition~9.4.11]{TaoAnalysis1}

Let $p$ be a real number.
Then the function $f:(0,\infty)\to\mathbb{R}$ defined by
\[
f(x):=x^p
\]
is continuous.

nodes.name: Global Continuity Theorem
nodes.question: State the Global Continuity Theorem.
nodes.statement_display:
$f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous iff preimages of
  closed sets are closed, iff preimages of open sets are open.
nodes.statement_tex:
$f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ is continuous iff preimages of
  closed sets are closed, iff preimages of open sets are open.
nodes.hint:
Continuity = preimage preserves open/closed structure.
  This is the topological definition of continuity.
nodes.formal:
$f$ continuous on $\mathbb{R}$
$\;\iff\;$
$\forall F$ closed$:$ $f^{-1}(F)$ closed
$\;\iff\;$
$\forall G$ open$:$ $f^{-1}(G)$ open.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$f$ continuous on $\mathbb{R}$
$\;\iff\;$
$\forall F$ closed$:$ $f^{-1}(F)$ closed
$\;\iff\;$
$\forall G$ open$:$ $f^{-1}(G)$ open.

nodes.name: Image of a Closed Bounded Interval
nodes.question: What is the image of a closed bounded interval under a continuous function?
nodes.statement_display: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous, then \(f([a,b])\) is a closed bounded interval.
nodes.statement_tex: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous, then \(f([a,b])\) is a closed bounded interval.
nodes.hint: Boundedness gives bounded, extrema give endpoints, and IVT removes gaps.
nodes.formal:
Let \(I=[a,b]\subseteq\mathbb{R}\), and let \(f:I\to\mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
f \text{ continuous on } I
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
f(I) \text{ is a closed bounded interval.}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
Let \(I=[a,b]\subseteq\mathbb{R}\), and let \(f:I\to\mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
f \text{ continuous on } I
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
f(I) \text{ is a closed bounded interval.}
\]

nodes.name: Injective Continuous Function Is Strictly Monotone
nodes.question:
If $f$ is continuous and injective on an interval, what
  can be said?
nodes.statement_display:
$f$ must be strictly monotone — either strictly increasing
  or strictly decreasing throughout.
nodes.statement_tex:
$f$ must be strictly monotone — either strictly increasing
  or strictly decreasing throughout.
nodes.hint:
Non-monotonicity forces a local max or min, which IVT
  turns into a repeated value, contradicting injectivity.
nodes.formal:
$f$ injective and continuous on interval $I$
$\;\Rightarrow\;$
$f$ strictly increasing or strictly decreasing on $I$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$f$ injective and continuous on interval $I$
$\;\Rightarrow\;$
$f$ strictly increasing or strictly decreasing on $I$.

nodes.name: Inverse of a Bi-Lipschitz Map Is Lipschitz
nodes.question:
If $f$ is bi-Lipschitz with bounds $\alpha$ and $K$, what
  can be said about $f^{-1}$?
nodes.statement_display:
$f^{-1}$ exists and is bi-Lipschitz with bounds $1/K$
  and $1/\alpha$. In particular $f^{-1}$ is Lipschitz with
  constant $1/\alpha$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$f^{-1}$ exists and is bi-Lipschitz with bounds $1/K$
  and $1/\alpha$. In particular $f^{-1}$ is Lipschitz with
  constant $1/\alpha$.
nodes.hint:
Substitute $y=f(x)$, $v=f(u)$ and divide the lower
  Lipschitz bound of $f$ by $\alpha$.
nodes.formal:
$\alpha|x-u| \leq |f(x)-f(u)| \leq K|x-u|$
$\;\Rightarrow\;$
$\tfrac{1}{K}|y-v| \leq |f^{-1}(y)-f^{-1}(v)| \leq \tfrac{1}{\alpha}|y-v|$
for all $y,v \in f(A)$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$\alpha|x-u| \leq |f(x)-f(u)| \leq K|x-u|$
$\;\Rightarrow\;$
$\tfrac{1}{K}|y-v| \leq |f^{-1}(y)-f^{-1}(v)| \leq \tfrac{1}{\alpha}|y-v|$
for all $y,v \in f(A)$.
nodes.negation_display:
$x^3$ on $[-1,1]$: Lipschitz but not bi-Lipschitz (lower
  bound fails at $0$), so $x^{1/3}$ is not Lipschitz.
nodes.proof_sketch:
%
  1. Injectivity: lower bound forces $x \neq u \Rightarrow f(x) \neq f(u)$. \\
  2. Set $y = f(x)$, $v = f(u)$. Lower bound on $f$:
     $\alpha|f^{-1}(y)-f^{-1}(v)| \leq |y-v|$;
     divide by $\alpha$ to get Lipschitz constant $1/\alpha$ for $f^{-1}$. \\
  3. Upper bound on $f$: $|y-v| \leq K|f^{-1}(y)-f^{-1}(v)|$;
     divide by $K$ to get lower bound $1/K$ for $f^{-1}$.
implies_titles: Uniformly Continuous Bijection on a Compact Domain Has Uniformly Continuous Inverse

nodes.name: Inverse of a Strictly Monotone Function
nodes.question:
If $f$ is strictly monotone on $X$, what can be said about
  $f^{-1}$?
nodes.statement_display:
$f^{-1}$ exists on $f(X)$ and has the same monotonicity type
  as $f$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$f^{-1}$ exists on $f(X)$ and has the same monotonicity type
  as $f$.
nodes.hint:
Strict monotonicity gives injectivity; the inverse then
  inherits the order direction.
nodes.formal:
$f$ strictly increasing on $X$
$\;\Rightarrow\;$
$f^{-1}$ strictly increasing on $f(X)$.
Similarly for strictly decreasing.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$f$ strictly increasing on $X$
$\;\Rightarrow\;$
$f^{-1}$ strictly increasing on $f(X)$.
Similarly for strictly decreasing.

nodes.name: Jump Intervals of a Monotone Function Are Disjoint
nodes.question:
What is the jump interval at a discontinuity of a monotone
  function, and why are they disjoint?
nodes.statement_display:
The jump interval at $a$ is $(f(a^-), f(a^+))$ — the gap
  in the range. Distinct discontinuities produce disjoint jump
  intervals because monotonicity forces the ranges to be ordered.
nodes.statement_tex:
The jump interval at $a$ is $(f(a^-), f(a^+))$ — the gap
  in the range. Distinct discontinuities produce disjoint jump
  intervals because monotonicity forces the ranges to be ordered.
nodes.hint:
Monotonicity separates the jump intervals: $a < b$
  forces the jump at $a$ below the jump at $b$ with no overlap.
nodes.formal:
$a \neq b$ discontinuities of $f$ monotone
$\;\Rightarrow\;$
$(f(a^-), f(a^+)) \cap (f(b^-), f(b^+)) = \emptyset$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$a \neq b$ discontinuities of $f$ monotone
$\;\Rightarrow\;$
$(f(a^-), f(a^+)) \cap (f(b^-), f(b^+)) = \emptyset$.
nodes.proof_sketch:
1. WLOG $f$ increasing, $c < d$. 2. For any $x \in (c,d)$:
$f(c^+) \leq f(x) \leq f(d^-)$ by monotonicity. 3. Hence $f(c^+) \leq f(d^-)$.
4. Every element of $J_c(f)$ is $< f(c^+)$ and every element of $J_d(f)$ is
$> f(d^-)$, so the intervals are disjoint.
implies_titles: Discontinuities of a Monotone Function Are Countable

nodes.name: Lim Sup Is At Least Lim Inf
nodes.question:
What is the relationship between $\limsup$ and $\liminf$
  of a function?
nodes.statement_display:
$\limsup_{x \to x_0} f(x) \geq \liminf_{x \to x_0} f(x)$
  always.
nodes.statement_tex:
$\limsup_{x \to x_0} f(x) \geq \liminf_{x \to x_0} f(x)$
  always.
nodes.hint:
$\sup \geq \inf$ on any set; take the inf/sup over
  $\delta$ and the inequality is preserved.
implies_titles: Limit Exists iff Lim Sup Equals Lim Inf

nodes.name: Limit Exists iff Lim Sup Equals Lim Inf
nodes.question:
When does $\lim_{x \to x_0} f(x)$ exist in terms of
  $\limsup$ and $\liminf$?
nodes.statement_display:
Exactly when $\limsup_{x \to x_0} f(x) = \liminf_{x \to x_0}
  f(x) \in \mathbb{R}$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Exactly when $\limsup_{x \to x_0} f(x) = \liminf_{x \to x_0}
  f(x) \in \mathbb{R}$.
nodes.hint:
The limit squeezes the upper and lower envelopes to the
  same finite value. If they disagree or diverge, no limit exists.
nodes.formal:
$\lim_{x \to x_0} f(x) = L \in \mathbb{R}$
$\;\iff\;$
$\limsup_{x \to x_0} f(x) = L = \liminf_{x \to x_0} f(x)$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$\lim_{x \to x_0} f(x) = L \in \mathbb{R}$
$\;\iff\;$
$\limsup_{x \to x_0} f(x) = L = \liminf_{x \to x_0} f(x)$.
nodes.negation_display:
$\sin(1/x)$ at $0$: $\limsup = 1 \neq -1 = \liminf$, so
  no limit.

nodes.name: Lipschitz Implies Uniform Continuity
nodes.question: State the proposition: Lipschitz Implies Uniform Continuity.

nodes.name: Local Properties of Continuous Functions
nodes.question: What four local properties does continuity at a point guarantee?
nodes.statement_display:
(1) Local boundedness near $a$. (2) Sign stability near $a$
  when $f(a)\neq 0$. (3) Algebra: sums, differences, products,
  quotients (when denominator $\neq 0$) are continuous at $a$.
  (4) Composition with a function continuous at $f(a)$ is continuous
  at $a$.
nodes.statement_tex:
(1) Local boundedness near $a$. (2) Sign stability near $a$
  when $f(a)\neq 0$. (3) Algebra: sums, differences, products,
  quotients (when denominator $\neq 0$) are continuous at $a$.
  (4) Composition with a function continuous at $f(a)$ is continuous
  at $a$.
nodes.hint:
Zorich bundles four separate results into one theorem.
  Each part corresponds to a theorem elsewhere in the notes.
nodes.formal:
Let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous at $c \in I$. Then:
(1)~$\exists\,\delta_1 > 0,\, M > 0$ such that $|f(x)| \leq M$ for all
$x \in I$ with $|x - c| < \delta_1$.
(2)~If $f(c) \neq 0$: $\exists\,\delta_2 > 0,\, \alpha > 0$ such that
$|f(x)| \geq \alpha$ for all $x \in I$ with $|x - c| < \delta_2$.
(3)~If $f(c) > 0$: $\exists\,\delta_3 > 0$ such that $f(x) > 0$ for all
$x \in I$ with $|x - c| < \delta_3$. If $f(c) < 0$: $\exists\,\delta_4 > 0$
such that $f(x) < 0$ for all $x \in I$ with $|x - c| < \delta_4$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
Let $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous at $c \in I$. Then:
(1)~$\exists\,\delta_1 > 0,\, M > 0$ such that $|f(x)| \leq M$ for all
$x \in I$ with $|x - c| < \delta_1$.
(2)~If $f(c) \neq 0$: $\exists\,\delta_2 > 0,\, \alpha > 0$ such that
$|f(x)| \geq \alpha$ for all $x \in I$ with $|x - c| < \delta_2$.
(3)~If $f(c) > 0$: $\exists\,\delta_3 > 0$ such that $f(x) > 0$ for all
$x \in I$ with $|x - c| < \delta_3$. If $f(c) < 0$: $\exists\,\delta_4 > 0$
such that $f(x) < 0$ for all $x \in I$ with $|x - c| < \delta_4$.
nodes.proof_sketch:
%
  1. Continuity at $c$ gives $\lim_{x \to c}f(x) = f(c)$. \\
  2. Part (1): cite Limit Implies Local Bounding. \\
  3. Part (2): cite Limits Bounded Away from Zero (needs $f(c) \neq 0$). \\
  4. Part (3): cite Sign Preservation.
implies_titles: Algebra of Continuity at a Point

nodes.name: Location of Roots Theorem
nodes.question: What does a sign change force for a continuous function on a closed interval?
nodes.statement_display: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous and \(f(a)\) and \(f(b)\) have opposite signs, then there exists \(c\in(a,b)\) such that \(f(c)=0\).
nodes.statement_tex: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous and \(f(a)\) and \(f(b)\) have opposite signs, then there exists \(c\in(a,b)\) such that \(f(c)=0\).
nodes.hint: A continuous path from negative to positive cannot jump over zero.
nodes.formal:
Let \(a,b\in\mathbb{R}\) with \(a<b\), and let \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
f \text{ continuous on } [a,b]
\ \wedge\
\bigl(f(a)<0<f(b)\ \vee\ f(a)>0>f(b)\bigr)
\]
implies
\[
\exists c\in(a,b)\text{ such that } f(c)=0.
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
Let \(a,b\in\mathbb{R}\) with \(a<b\), and let \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
f \text{ continuous on } [a,b]
\ \wedge\
\bigl(f(a)<0<f(b)\ \vee\ f(a)>0>f(b)\bigr)
\]
implies
\[
\exists c\in(a,b)\text{ such that } f(c)=0.
\]

nodes.name: Maximum--Minimum Theorem
nodes.question: What does continuity on a closed bounded interval guarantee about extrema?
nodes.statement_display: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous on \([a,b]\), then \(f\) has an absolute maximum and an absolute minimum on \([a,b]\).
nodes.statement_tex: If \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\) is continuous on \([a,b]\), then \(f\) has an absolute maximum and an absolute minimum on \([a,b]\).
nodes.hint: Continuity on a compact interval forces the extreme values not only to exist as bounds but to be attained.
nodes.formal:
For all \(a,b\in\mathbb{R}\) with \(a\le b\), for every function \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\),
\[
f \text{ continuous on } [a,b]
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\Bigl(
\exists x^{*}\in[a,b]\;\forall x\in[a,b],\ f(x^{*})\ge f(x)
\Bigr)
\]
and
\[
\Bigl(
\exists x_{*}\in[a,b]\;\forall x\in[a,b],\ f(x_{*})\le f(x)
\Bigr).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
For all \(a,b\in\mathbb{R}\) with \(a\le b\), for every function \(f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}\),
\[
f \text{ continuous on } [a,b]
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\Bigl(
\exists x^{*}\in[a,b]\;\forall x\in[a,b],\ f(x^{*})\ge f(x)
\Bigr)
\]
and
\[
\Bigl(
\exists x_{*}\in[a,b]\;\forall x\in[a,b],\ f(x_{*})\le f(x)
\Bigr).
\]
nodes.proof_sketch: 1. Use boundedness to define sup f(I) and inf f(I). 2. Choose sequences whose function values approach these extrema. 3. Extract convergent subsequences in [a,b] and apply continuity to show the extreme values are attained.
implies_titles: Every Value Between \(\inf f(I)\) and \(\sup f(I)\) Is Attained
Image of a Closed Bounded Interval

nodes.name: Monotone Function Is Continuous on a Dense Set
nodes.question: Where is a monotone function continuous?
nodes.statement_display:
At a set dense in its domain. The discontinuity set is
  countable, hence nowhere dense, so its complement is dense.
nodes.statement_tex:
At a set dense in its domain. The discontinuity set is
  countable, hence nowhere dense, so its complement is dense.
nodes.hint:
Countable $\Rightarrow$ nowhere dense $\Rightarrow$
  complement is dense.
nodes.formal:
$f$ monotone on $[a,b]$
$\;\Rightarrow\;$
$C_f := \{x \in [a,b] : f \text{ continuous at } x\}$ is dense in
$[a,b]$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$f$ monotone on $[a,b]$
$\;\Rightarrow\;$
$C_f := \{x \in [a,b] : f \text{ continuous at } x\}$ is dense in
$[a,b]$.
nodes.proof_sketch:
1. $D$ countable by Discontinuities of Monotone Function Are Countable.
2. Any open subinterval of $I$ is uncountable, so not contained in $D$. 3. Hence
every open subinterval meets $I \setminus D$, so $I \setminus D$ is dense in $I$.

nodes.name: Neighborhood Characterization of Discontinuity at a Point
nodes.question: What is the neighborhood characterization of discontinuity at a point?
nodes.statement_display: A function \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) is discontinuous at \(x_0\) in the neighborhood sense if there exists a neighborhood \(V\) of \(f(x_0)\) such that every neighborhood \(U\) of \(x_0\) contains a point \(x\in U\cap E\) with \(f(x)\notin V\).
nodes.statement_tex: A function \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) is discontinuous at \(x_0\) in the neighborhood sense if there exists a neighborhood \(V\) of \(f(x_0)\) such that every neighborhood \(U\) of \(x_0\) contains a point \(x\in U\cap E\) with \(f(x)\notin V\).
nodes.hint: Continuity sends some neighborhood of the point into any chosen neighborhood of the value. Discontinuity means one target neighborhood cannot be captured.
nodes.formal:
Let \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : E \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(x_0 \in E\). Then
\[
f \text{ is discontinuous at } x_0 \text{ in the neighborhood sense}
\]
if and only if
\[
\exists V \text{ a neighborhood of } f(x_0)\;
\forall U \text{ a neighborhood of } x_0\;
\exists x\in U\cap E:
\quad
f(x)\notin V.
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
Let \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : E \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(x_0 \in E\). Then
\[
f \text{ is discontinuous at } x_0 \text{ in the neighborhood sense}
\]
if and only if
\[
\exists V \text{ a neighborhood of } f(x_0)\;
\forall U \text{ a neighborhood of } x_0\;
\exists x\in U\cap E:
\quad
f(x)\notin V.
\]
implies_titles: Equivalent Formulations of Discontinuity at a Point

nodes.name: Non-Uniform Continuity Criteria
nodes.question: State the sequential criterion for failure of uniform continuity.
nodes.statement_display:
$f$ fails to be uniformly continuous on $A$ iff there exist
  $\varepsilon_0 > 0$ and sequences $(x_n),(u_n) \subseteq A$ with
  $x_n - u_n \to 0$ but $|f(x_n)-f(u_n)| \geq \varepsilon_0$ for all $n$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$f$ fails to be uniformly continuous on $A$ iff there exist
  $\varepsilon_0 > 0$ and sequences $(x_n),(u_n) \subseteq A$ with
  $x_n - u_n \to 0$ but $|f(x_n)-f(u_n)| \geq \varepsilon_0$ for all $n$.
nodes.hint:
Inputs squeeze together; outputs stay apart. This is the
  negation of uniform continuity with $\delta = 1/n$.
nodes.formal:
$f$ not uniformly continuous on $A$
$\;\iff\;$
$\exists\,\varepsilon_0 > 0,\;
\exists\,(x_n),(u_n) \subseteq A:\;
\lim(x_n - u_n) = 0$
and
$|f(x_n)-f(u_n)| \geq \varepsilon_0\;\forall n$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$f$ not uniformly continuous on $A$
$\;\iff\;$
$\exists\,\varepsilon_0 > 0,\;
\exists\,(x_n),(u_n) \subseteq A:\;
\lim(x_n - u_n) = 0$
and
$|f(x_n)-f(u_n)| \geq \varepsilon_0\;\forall n$.
nodes.negation_display:
$f(x)=x^2$ on $\mathbb{R}$: take $x_n = n+1/n$,
  $u_n = n$; then $x_n - u_n = 1/n \to 0$ but
  $|f(x_n)-f(u_n)| = 2 + 1/n^2 \not\to 0$.
nodes.proof_sketch:
%
  $(\Rightarrow)$: Negate UC; set $\delta=1/n$ to get $(x_n),(u_n)$ with
  $|x_n-u_n|<1/n$ and $|f(x_n)-f(u_n)|\geq\varepsilon_0$. Squeeze gives
  $x_n-u_n\to 0$. \\
  $(\Leftarrow)$: Suppose UC holds; get $\delta>0$. Since $x_n-u_n\to 0$,
  pick $N$ with $|x_N-u_N|<\delta$, giving $|f(x_N)-f(u_N)|<\varepsilon_0$.
  Contradicts $|f(x_n)-f(u_n)|\geq\varepsilon_0$ for all $n$.

nodes.name: One-Sided Limits of Monotone Functions
nodes.question:
What are the one-sided limits of an increasing function at
  an interior point $c$?
nodes.statement_display:
$\lim_{x \to c^-} f = \sup\{f(x): x < c\}$ and
  $\lim_{x \to c^+} f = \inf\{f(x): x > c\}$; both exist.
nodes.statement_tex:
$\lim_{x \to c^-} f = \sup\{f(x): x < c\}$ and
  $\lim_{x \to c^+} f = \inf\{f(x): x > c\}$; both exist.
nodes.formal:
$f$ increasing on $I$,\; $c$ interior to $I$
$\;\Rightarrow\;$
$\lim_{x \to c^-} f = \sup\{f(x): x < c\}$
and
$\lim_{x \to c^+} f = \inf\{f(x): x > c\}$,
both finite.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$f$ increasing on $I$,\; $c$ interior to $I$
$\;\Rightarrow\;$
$\lim_{x \to c^-} f = \sup\{f(x): x < c\}$
and
$\lim_{x \to c^+} f = \inf\{f(x): x > c\}$,
both finite.
nodes.proof_sketch:
1. Set $s = \sup S$, $j = \inf J$; both finite by boundedness from interior
point. 2. For $\lim_{x \to c^{-}}$: $s - \varepsilon$ not an upper bound gives $x_0 
c$; take $\delta = c - x_0$; monotonicity pins $f(x) \in (s-\varepsilon, s]$. 3.
Right-hand limit is the mirror via $\inf J$. 4. Ordering follows from monotonicity
applied to all $x < c$ (resp.\ $x > c$).

nodes.name: Oscillation at a Point
nodes.question: What is the oscillation of a function at a point?
nodes.statement_display:
If \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) and \(x_0\in E\), then
\[
\omega(f;x_0)=\lim_{\delta\to 0^+}\omega\bigl(f;U_\delta(x_0)\cap E\bigr).
\]
It measures the residual spread of the nearby function values arbitrarily close to \(x_0\).
nodes.statement_tex:
If \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) and \(x_0\in E\), then
\[
\omega(f;x_0)=\lim_{\delta\to 0^+}\omega\bigl(f;U_\delta(x_0)\cap E\bigr).
\]
It measures the residual spread of the nearby function values arbitrarily close to \(x_0\).
nodes.hint: Shrink neighborhoods around \(x_0\) and track how much the image can still spread.
nodes.formal:
Let \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : E \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(x_0\in E\). Then
\[
\omega(f;x_0)
=
\lim_{\delta\to 0^+}
\sup \bigl\{\, |f(x_1)-f(x_2)| : x_1,x_2\in U_\delta(x_0)\cap E \,\bigr\}.
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
Let \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : E \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(x_0\in E\). Then
\[
\omega(f;x_0)
=
\lim_{\delta\to 0^+}
\sup \bigl\{\, |f(x_1)-f(x_2)| : x_1,x_2\in U_\delta(x_0)\cap E \,\bigr\}.
\]

nodes.name: Oscillation on a Set
nodes.question: What is the oscillation of a function on a set?
nodes.statement_display:
If \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\), then
\[
\omega(f;E)=\sup_{x_1,x_2\in E}|f(x_1)-f(x_2)|.
\]
It is the diameter of the image \(f(E)\).
nodes.statement_tex:
If \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\), then
\[
\omega(f;E)=\sup_{x_1,x_2\in E}|f(x_1)-f(x_2)|.
\]
It is the diameter of the image \(f(E)\).
nodes.hint: Oscillation on a set measures the spread of the function values on that set.
nodes.formal:
Let \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) and let \(f : E \to \mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
\omega(f;E)
=
\sup \bigl\{\, |f(x_1)-f(x_2)| : x_1,x_2\in E \,\bigr\}.
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
Let \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) and let \(f : E \to \mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
\omega(f;E)
=
\sup \bigl\{\, |f(x_1)-f(x_2)| : x_1,x_2\in E \,\bigr\}.
\]

nodes.name: Piecewise Linear Approximation
nodes.question: State the Piecewise Linear Approximation Theorem.
nodes.statement_display:
Every continuous $f$ on $[a,b]$ can be approximated uniformly
  to within $\varepsilon$ by a piecewise linear function.
nodes.statement_tex:
Every continuous $f$ on $[a,b]$ can be approximated uniformly
  to within $\varepsilon$ by a piecewise linear function.
nodes.hint:
Heine–Cantor gives $\delta$; partition $[a,b]$ into
  pieces of width $< \delta$; connect the graph values at the nodes
  by straight line segments.
nodes.formal:
$f$ continuous on $[a,b]$,\; $\varepsilon > 0$
$\;\Rightarrow\;$
$\exists$ piecewise linear $\ell_\varepsilon$ on $[a,b]$ with
$\sup_{x \in [a,b]} |f(x) - \ell_\varepsilon(x)| < \varepsilon$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$f$ continuous on $[a,b]$,\; $\varepsilon > 0$
$\;\Rightarrow\;$
$\exists$ piecewise linear $\ell_\varepsilon$ on $[a,b]$ with
$\sup_{x \in [a,b]} |f(x) - \ell_\varepsilon(x)| < \varepsilon$.

nodes.name: Power Function Is Continuous
nodes.question: State the lemma: Power Function Is Continuous.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ be defined by
\[
f(x)=x^n
\]
for some $n \in \mathbb{N}$.
Then $f$ is a continuous function.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{lem:power-function-continuous}

Let $f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$ be defined by
\[
f(x)=x^n
\]
for some $n \in \mathbb{N}$.
Then $f$ is a continuous function.
nodes.proof_sketch:
%
  1. Base case $n=1$: set $\delta := \varepsilon$; then
     $|x - x_0| < \delta \Rightarrow |x - x_0| < \varepsilon$. \\
  2. Inductive step: $x^{n+1} = x^n \cdot x$; product of two continuous
     functions (IH + base case) is continuous by the product rule. \\
  3. Conclude by induction.

nodes.name: Preservation of Intervals Theorem
nodes.question: State the theorem: Preservation of Intervals Theorem.
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(I\) be an interval and let \(f : I \to \mathbb{R}\) be continuous on \(I\).
Then the set \(f(I)\) is an interval.
nodes.formal:
Let \(I\subseteq\mathbb{R}\) be an interval and let \(f:I\to\mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
f \text{ continuous on } I
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
f(I) \text{ is an interval.}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
Let \(I\subseteq\mathbb{R}\) be an interval and let \(f:I\to\mathbb{R}\). Then
\[
f \text{ continuous on } I
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
f(I) \text{ is an interval.}
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
Take $y_1, y_2 \in f(I)$, $y_1 < k < y_2$. Pull back:
$f(x_1)=y_1$, $f(x_2)=y_2$. $J = [\min(x_1,x_2),\max(x_1,x_2)]
\subseteq I$ since $I$ is an interval. IVT on $J$ gives $c$ with
$f(c)=k$. So $k \in f(I)$. Hence $f(I)$ is closed under betweenness,
i.e., an interval.
nodes.uses_text: Characterization of Intervals, Intermediate Value Theorem

nodes.name: Rational Functions Are Continuous on Their Domain
nodes.question: State the continuity theorem for rational functions.
nodes.statement_display:
If $P$ and $Q$ are real polynomials and $D = \{x \in \mathbb{R} : Q(x) \neq 0\}$,
  then $R(x) = P(x)/Q(x)$ is continuous on $D$.
nodes.statement_tex:
If $P$ and $Q$ are real polynomials and $D = \{x \in \mathbb{R} : Q(x) \neq 0\}$,
  then $R(x) = P(x)/Q(x)$ is continuous on $D$.
nodes.hint:
Polynomials are continuous everywhere; quotient rule for continuous
  functions applies whenever the denominator is nonzero.
nodes.formal:
$\forall$ real polynomials $P$, $Q$,
$\forall x_0 \in D = \{x \in \mathbb{R} : Q(x) \neq 0\}$:
$R = P/Q$ is continuous at $x_0$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$\forall$ real polynomials $P$, $Q$,
$\forall x_0 \in D = \{x \in \mathbb{R} : Q(x) \neq 0\}$:
$R = P/Q$ is continuous at $x_0$.
implies_titles: Any Real Polynomial Is a Continuous Function

nodes.name: Sequential Characterization of Discontinuity at a Point
nodes.question: What is the sequential characterization of discontinuity at a point?
