nodes.name: Preservation of Open and Closed Sets
nodes.question: State: bijective isometries preserve open/closed sets.
nodes.statement_display: If $f:X\to Y$ is a bijective isometry: $U\subseteq X$ is open iff $f(U)\subseteq Y$ is open.
nodes.statement_tex: If $f:X\to Y$ is a bijective isometry: $U\subseteq X$ is open iff $f(U)\subseteq Y$ is open.

nodes.name: Reflections are Isometries
nodes.question: State: reflections are isometries.
nodes.statement_display: Reflection across any hyperplane in $\mathbb{R}^n$ is an isometry.
nodes.statement_tex: Reflection across any hyperplane in $\mathbb{R}^n$ is an isometry.
nodes.hint: Reflection is an orthogonal map (matrix with determinant $-1$), hence an isometry.

nodes.name: Rigid Motion
nodes.question: Define a rigid motion of $\mathbb{R}^n$.
nodes.statement_display: A rigid motion is a bijective isometry $f:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^n$ w.r.t.\ the Euclidean metric.
nodes.statement_tex: A rigid motion is a bijective isometry $f:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^n$ w.r.t.\ the Euclidean metric.
nodes.hint: Every rigid motion is of the form $f(x)=Rx+a$ with $R\in\mathrm{O}(n)$ and $a\in\mathbb{R}^n$.

nodes.name: Rotations are Isometries
nodes.question: State: orthogonal maps are isometries.
nodes.statement_display: If $R:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^n$ is orthogonal ($R^\top R=I$) then $R$ is an isometry.
nodes.statement_tex: If $R:\mathbb{R}^n\to\mathbb{R}^n$ is orthogonal ($R^\top R=I$) then $R$ is an isometry.
nodes.hint: $\|Rx-Ry\|_2=\|R(x-y)\|_2=\|x-y\|_2$ since orthogonal maps preserve norms.

nodes.name: Subspace Metric
nodes.question: Define the subspace metric.
nodes.statement_display: For $A\subseteq X$: $d|_A(x,y):=d(x,y)$ for $x,y\in A$. The pair $(A,d|_A)$ is a metric subspace.
nodes.statement_tex: For $A\subseteq X$: $d|_A(x,y):=d(x,y)$ for $x,y\in A$. The pair $(A,d|_A)$ is a metric subspace.

nodes.name: Translations are Isometries
nodes.question: State: translations are isometries.
nodes.statement_display: $T_a(x):=x+a$ is an isometry of $\mathbb{R}^n$ for any $a\in\mathbb{R}^n$.
nodes.statement_tex: $T_a(x):=x+a$ is an isometry of $\mathbb{R}^n$ for any $a\in\mathbb{R}^n$.

nodes.name: Closed Sets and Derived Sets
nodes.question: State: $E$ is closed iff $E'\subseteq E$.
nodes.statement_display: $E$ closed $\iff E'\subseteq E$.
nodes.statement_tex: $E$ closed $\iff E'\subseteq E$.
nodes.hint: Closed $\Rightarrow$ sequentially closed $\Rightarrow$ limit points in $E$.

nodes.name: Closure Formula
nodes.question: State the Closure Formula.
nodes.statement_display: $\overline{E}=E\cup E'$.
nodes.statement_tex: $\overline{E}=E\cup E'$.
nodes.hint: Adherent points are either in $E$ or are limits of sequences in $E$ (i.e.\ limit points).

nodes.name: Derived Set
nodes.question: Define the derived set $E'$.
nodes.statement_display: $E':=\{x\in X:x\text{ is a limit point of }E\}$.
nodes.statement_tex: $E':=\{x\in X:x\text{ is a limit point of }E\}$.
nodes.hint: $E'$ is always closed; $E$ closed iff $E'\subseteq E$; $\overline{E}=E\cup E'$.

nodes.name: Derived Set is Closed
nodes.question: State: $E'$ is closed.
nodes.statement_display: For any $E\subseteq X$, the derived set $E'$ is a closed set.
nodes.statement_tex: For any $E\subseteq X$, the derived set $E'$ is a closed set.
nodes.hint: Take a limit point of $E'$; diagonal sequence argument shows it is in $E'$.

nodes.name: Infinite Neighbourhood Characterization
nodes.question: State the Infinite Neighbourhood Characterisation of limit points.
nodes.statement_display: $x\in E'$ iff $\forall r>0$, $B_r(x)\cap E$ is infinite.
nodes.statement_tex: $x\in E'$ iff $\forall r>0$, $B_r(x)\cap E$ is infinite.
nodes.hint: If some ball met $E$ in only finitely many points, the smallest distance to those finitely many points gives a ball missing $E\setminus\{x\}$.

nodes.name: Isolated Point
nodes.question: Define an isolated point.
nodes.statement_display: $a\in E$ is isolated if $a\notin E'$: some ball $B_r(a)$ contains no other point of $E$.
nodes.statement_tex: $a\in E$ is isolated if $a\notin E'$: some ball $B_r(a)$ contains no other point of $E$.
nodes.hint: Isolated = no sequence in $E\setminus\{a\}$ approaches $a$.

nodes.name: Limit Point
nodes.question: Define a limit point of $E$.
nodes.statement_display: $x$ is a limit point of $E$ if $\forall r>0,B_r(x)\cap(E\setminus\{x\})\neq\varnothing$.
nodes.statement_tex: $x$ is a limit point of $E$ if $\forall r>0,B_r(x)\cap(E\setminus\{x\})\neq\varnothing$.
nodes.hint: Every ball around $x$ must contain a point of $E$ other than $x$ itself.

nodes.name: Perfect Set
nodes.question: Define a perfect set.
nodes.statement_display: $A$ is perfect if $A$ is closed and $A = A'$ (every point of $A$ is a limit point of $A$).
nodes.statement_tex: $A$ is perfect if $A$ is closed and $A = A'$ (every point of $A$ is a limit point of $A$).
nodes.hint: Perfect sets are uncountable if nonempty. The Cantor set is perfect.

nodes.name: Punctured Ball
nodes.question: Define the punctured ball $B_r^*(x)$.
nodes.statement_display: $B_r^*(x) := B_r(x)\setminus\{x\} = \{y : 0 < d(x,y) < r\}$. The open ball with centre removed.
nodes.statement_tex: $B_r^*(x) := B_r(x)\setminus\{x\} = \{y : 0 < d(x,y) < r\}$. The open ball with centre removed.
nodes.hint: In $\mathbb{R}$: $(x-r,x)\cup(x,x+r)$. The point $x$ itself is excluded.
nodes.formal: \[ B_r^*(x) := \{y \in X : 0 < d(x,y) < r\}. \] The strict inequality $0 < d(x,y)$ is the set-subtraction $X \setminus \{x\}$ made explicit: the witness $y$ must be distinct from $x$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: \[ B_r^*(x) := \{y \in X : 0 < d(x,y) < r\}. \] The strict inequality $0 < d(x,y)$ is the set-subtraction $X \setminus \{x\}$ made explicit: the witness $y$ must be distinct from $x$.

nodes.name: Sequential Characterization of Limit Points
nodes.question: State the Sequential Characterisation of Limit Points.
nodes.statement_display: $x\in E'$ iff there exists a sequence $(x_n)\subseteq E$ with all $x_n\neq x$ and $x_n\to x$.
nodes.statement_tex: $x\in E'$ iff there exists a sequence $(x_n)\subseteq E$ with all $x_n\neq x$ and $x_n\to x$.
nodes.hint: Forward: take $x_n\in B_{1/n}(x)\cap(E\setminus\{x\})$. Backward: any sequence converging to $x$ witnesses every ball is met.

nodes.name: $\delta$-Closeness of a Function Near a Point
nodes.question:
State the $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ definition of
  $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L$.
nodes.statement_display:
$\forall\varepsilon>0,\,\exists\delta>0,\,\forall x\in A:\,
  0<|x-c|<\delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-L|<\varepsilon$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$\forall\varepsilon>0,\,\exists\delta>0,\,\forall x\in A:\,
  0<|x-c|<\delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-L|<\varepsilon$.
nodes.negation_display:
$f(x)=\sin(1/x)$ at $c=0$: no $\delta$ works for
  $\varepsilon < 1$ since $f$ oscillates between $\pm 1$ in
  every punctured neighbourhood of $0$.

nodes.name: $\varepsilon$-Closeness of a Function to a Value
nodes.question: Define: $f : X \to \mathbb{R}$ is $\varepsilon$-close to $L$.
nodes.statement_display:
$|f(x) - L| \leq \varepsilon$ for all $x \in X$.
  Every output is within $\varepsilon$ of $L$. Global condition.
nodes.statement_tex:
$|f(x) - L| \leq \varepsilon$ for all $x \in X$.
  Every output is within $\varepsilon$ of $L$. Global condition.
nodes.hint:
Tao scaffolding only. Not used by Bartle or Lebl.
  Contrast with $\delta$-closeness which is local.
nodes.formal:
\[
  f \text{ is } \varepsilon\text{-close to } L
  \;\iff\;
  \forall x \in X,\; |f(x) - L| \leq \varepsilon.
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
  f \text{ is } \varepsilon\text{-close to } L
  \;\iff\;
  \forall x \in X,\; |f(x) - L| \leq \varepsilon.
\]

nodes.name:
%
    $\varepsilon$--$\delta$ Divergence (Negation Form)
nodes.question:
State the definition: %
    $\varepsilon$--$\delta$ Divergence (Negation Form).
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and
let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. Then
\[
\lim_{x \to c} f(x) \neq L
\]
if and only if
\[
\forall \delta > 0,\; \exists x \in A:
\quad 0 < |x-c| < \delta
\;\wedge\;
|f(x) - L| \ge \varepsilon
\]
for some $\varepsilon > 0$.

Equivalently (fully quantified):
\[
\forall L \in \mathbb{R},\;
\exists \varepsilon > 0,\;
\forall \delta > 0,\;
\exists x \in A:
\quad 0 < |x-c| < \delta
\;\wedge\;
|f(x) - L| \ge \varepsilon.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:limit-divergence-eps}
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and
let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. Then
\[
\lim_{x \to c} f(x) \neq L
\]
if and only if
\[
\forall \delta > 0,\; \exists x \in A:
\quad 0 < |x-c| < \delta
\;\wedge\;
|f(x) - L| \ge \varepsilon
\]
for some $\varepsilon > 0$.

Equivalently (fully quantified):
\[
\forall L \in \mathbb{R},\;
\exists \varepsilon > 0,\;
\forall \delta > 0,\;
\exists x \in A:
\quad 0 < |x-c| < \delta
\;\wedge\;
|f(x) - L| \ge \varepsilon.
\]

nodes.name:
%
    Bounded on a Neighborhood
nodes.question: What does it mean for a function to be bounded near a point $c$?
nodes.statement_display:
$\exists \delta > 0,\exists M > 0$ such that
$|f(x)| \le M$ for all $x$ with $0 < |x-c| < \delta$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$\exists \delta > 0,\exists M > 0$ such that
$|f(x)| \le M$ for all $x$ with $0 < |x-c| < \delta$.
nodes.hint: Local bound on a punctured neighborhood.
nodes.negation_display: Do not require boundedness on all of $A$—only near $c$.

nodes.name:
%
    Divergence Criteria
nodes.question: State the divergence criteria (Bartle 4.1.9).
nodes.statement_display:
(a) $\lim f \neq L$ iff $\exists (x_n)\subseteq
  A\setminus\{c\}$ with $x_n\to c$ and $f(x_n)\not\to L$.
  (b) $\lim f$ does not exist iff $\exists (x_n)\subseteq
  A\setminus\{c\}$ with $x_n\to c$ and $(f(x_n))$ diverges
  in $\mathbb{R}$.
nodes.statement_tex:
(a) $\lim f \neq L$ iff $\exists (x_n)\subseteq
  A\setminus\{c\}$ with $x_n\to c$ and $f(x_n)\not\to L$.
  (b) $\lim f$ does not exist iff $\exists (x_n)\subseteq
  A\setminus\{c\}$ with $x_n\to c$ and $(f(x_n))$ diverges
  in $\mathbb{R}$.
nodes.hint:
(a) is the negation of the sequential criterion for
  a fixed $L$. (b) says no candidate $L$ works at all.
  Both are used to disprove limits by exhibiting sequences.
nodes.negation_display:
Two sequences suffice for (b): $x_n\to 0$ via
  $1/(2n\pi)$ gives $\sin(1/x_n)=0$; via $1/(\pi/2+2n\pi)$
  gives $\sin(1/x_n)=1$. Different limits, so no limit exists.

nodes.name:
%
    Limit Implies Local Bounding
nodes.question: What local boundedness property follows from a finite function limit?
nodes.statement_display: If $\lim_{x \to c} f(x)=L \in \mathbb{R}$, then $f$ is bounded on some punctured neighborhood of $c$.
nodes.statement_tex: If $\lim_{x \to c} f(x)=L \in \mathbb{R}$, then $f$ is bounded on some punctured neighborhood of $c$.
nodes.hint: Choose $\varepsilon=1$ and use $|f(x)| \le |f(x)-L| + |L|$.
nodes.negation_display: This gives only local boundedness near $c$, not boundedness on all of the domain.

nodes.name:
%
    Neighbourhood Form of the Limit
nodes.question: State the neighbourhood form of $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$.
nodes.statement_display:
For every $V_\varepsilon(L)$ there exists $V_\delta(c)$
  such that $x\in V_\delta(c)\cap A$, $x\neq c$ implies
  $f(x)\in V_\varepsilon(L)$.
nodes.statement_tex:
For every $V_\varepsilon(L)$ there exists $V_\delta(c)$
  such that $x\in V_\delta(c)\cap A$, $x\neq c$ implies
  $f(x)\in V_\varepsilon(L)$.
nodes.hint:
Pure notation: $V_\delta(c) = (c-\delta, c+\delta)$,
  so $x\in V_\delta(c)$ and $x\neq c$ is exactly
  $0<|x-c|<\delta$. The two forms are identical.
nodes.proof_sketch:
1. $(i)\Rightarrow(ii)$: $x\in V_\delta(c)$,
  $x\neq c$ translates to $0<|x-c|<\delta$; (i) gives
  $|f(x)-L|<\varepsilon$, which translates to
  $f(x)\in V_\varepsilon(L)$.
2. $(ii)\Rightarrow(i)$: reverse the translation.

nodes.name:
%
    Sequential Criterion for Function Limits
nodes.question: State the sequential criterion for function limits.
nodes.statement_display:
$\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ iff for every sequence
  $(x_n)\subseteq A\setminus\{c\}$ with $x_n\to c$,
  we have $f(x_n)\to L$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L$ iff for every sequence
  $(x_n)\subseteq A\setminus\{c\}$ with $x_n\to c$,
  we have $f(x_n)\to L$.
nodes.hint:
$(a)\Rightarrow(b)$: use $\delta$ from the limit
  definition once $x_n$ is close enough.
  $(b)\Rightarrow(a)$: contrapositive — if limit fails,
  build a sequence witnessing failure.
nodes.negation_display:
$f(x)=\sin(1/x)$: take $x_n=1/(2n\pi)\to 0$
  giving $f(x_n)=0$, and $y_n=1/(\pi/2+2n\pi)\to 0$
  giving $f(y_n)=1$. Two sequences, two limits: diverges.
nodes.proof_sketch:
1. $(i)\Rightarrow(ii)$: Given $\varepsilon>0$, limit
  def gives $\delta$; convergence $x_n\to c$ gives $N$ with
  $|x_n-c|<\delta$ for $n\geq N$; limit def fires:
  $|f(x_n)-L|<\varepsilon$.
2. $(ii)\Rightarrow(i)$: Contrapositive. Negate limit def:
  $\exists\varepsilon_0>0$ s.t.\ $\forall\delta>0$ there is a
  bad point. Apply at $\delta=1/n$ to get $(x_n)\subseteq
  A\setminus\{c\}$ with $x_n\to c$ but $|f(x_n)-L|\geq\varepsilon_0$.
  Contradicts (ii).

nodes.name:
%
    Types of Divergence
nodes.question: What are the three types of divergence?
nodes.statement_display: $+\infty$, $-\infty$, and oscillation (no limit).
nodes.statement_tex: $+\infty$, $-\infty$, and oscillation (no limit).
nodes.hint: $\pm\infty$ are controlled divergence; oscillation is chaotic.
nodes.negation_display: Divergence does not always mean $\infty$—it can oscillate.

nodes.name: Algebra of Limits (Functions)
nodes.question: How do you prove algebra of limits for functions?
nodes.statement_display: Use the Sequential Criterion: reduce to sequence limits, apply algebra there, then lift back.
nodes.statement_tex: Use the Sequential Criterion: reduce to sequence limits, apply algebra there, then lift back.
nodes.hint: Never do $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ directly unless required.

nodes.name: Bounded From Above, Bounded From Below, Bounded
nodes.question: What does it mean for a function $f:A\to\mathbb{R}$ to be bounded?
nodes.statement_display: $f$ is bounded if both $\sup_A f$ and $\inf_A f$ are finite; equivalently, if there exists $M\ge 0$ such that $|f(x)|\le M$ for all $x\in A$.
nodes.statement_tex: $f$ is bounded if both $\sup_A f$ and $\inf_A f$ are finite; equivalently, if there exists $M\ge 0$ such that $|f(x)|\le M$ for all $x\in A$.
nodes.hint: Bounded = bounded above and bounded below.
implies_titles: Bounded From Above, Bounded From Below, Bounded

nodes.name: Bounded Limits
nodes.question: What does a two-sided bound on $f(x)$ near $c$ imply about $\lim_{x \to c} f(x)$?
nodes.statement_display:
If $a \le f(x) \le b$ for all $x \in A$, $x \ne c$, and $\lim_{x \to c} f(x)$ exists, then
$a \le \lim_{x \to c} f(x) \le b$.
nodes.statement_tex:
If $a \le f(x) \le b$ for all $x \in A$, $x \ne c$, and $\lim_{x \to c} f(x)$ exists, then
$a \le \lim_{x \to c} f(x) \le b$.
nodes.hint: Trap every nearby value of $f$ between $a$ and $b$, then trap the limit there too.
nodes.formal:
\[
\forall A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\;
\forall f : A \to \mathbb{R}\;
\forall c \in \mathbb{R}\;
\forall a,b \in \mathbb{R},
\]
\[
\Bigl[
c \text{ is a cluster point of } A
\;\wedge\;
\forall x \in A,\ x \ne c \Rightarrow a \le f(x) \le b
\;\wedge\;
\exists L \in \mathbb{R}\,(\lim_{x \to c} f(x)=L)
\Bigr]
\]
\[
\Rightarrow
\Bigl[
a \le L \le b
\Bigr].
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\forall A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\;
\forall f : A \to \mathbb{R}\;
\forall c \in \mathbb{R}\;
\forall a,b \in \mathbb{R},
\]
\[
\Bigl[
c \text{ is a cluster point of } A
\;\wedge\;
\forall x \in A,\ x \ne c \Rightarrow a \le f(x) \le b
\;\wedge\;
\exists L \in \mathbb{R}\,(\lim_{x \to c} f(x)=L)
\Bigr]
\]
\[
\Rightarrow
\Bigl[
a \le L \le b
\Bigr].
\]

nodes.name: Cantor ternary function
nodes.question: State the definition: Cantor ternary function.
nodes.statement_display: \cite[Example~4.3.13]{SohrabBasicRealAnalysis2} The Cantor function $\varphi : [0,1] \to [0,1]$ is defined as follows. Write $x \in [0,1]$ in base $3$. Let $N$ be the first index with digit $1$ (set $N = \infty$ if no digit is $1$). Replace each digit before position $N$ by the digit divided by $2$, replace digit at $N$ by $1$, and read the result in base $2$: \[ \varphi(0.x_1 x_2 x_3 …)_3 := (0.y_1 y_2 y_3 …)_2, \quad y_k = x_k/2 \text{ for } k < N,\; y_N = 1. \]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:cantor-function}
\cite[Example~4.3.13]{SohrabBasicRealAnalysis2}
The \emph{Cantor function} $\varphi : [0,1] \to [0,1]$ is defined as
follows. Write $x \in [0,1]$ in base $3$. Let $N$ be the first index
with digit $1$ (set $N = \infty$ if no digit is $1$). Replace each digit
before position $N$ by the digit divided by $2$, replace digit at $N$
by $1$, and read the result in base $2$:
\[
  \varphi(0.x_1 x_2 x_3 \ldots)_3
  := (0.y_1 y_2 y_3 \ldots)_2,
  \quad y_k = x_k/2 \text{ for } k < N,\; y_N = 1.
\]

nodes.name: Cauchy Criterion for Function Limits
nodes.question: State the Cauchy criterion for function limits.
nodes.statement_display:
$\lim_{x\to c}f(x)$ exists iff: $\forall\varepsilon>0\;\exists\delta>0$
such that $0<|x-c|,|y-c|<\delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-f(y)|<\varepsilon$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$\lim_{x\to c}f(x)$ exists iff: $\forall\varepsilon>0\;\exists\delta>0$
such that $0<|x-c|,|y-c|<\delta \Rightarrow |f(x)-f(y)|<\varepsilon$.
nodes.hint:
No limit value appears. The criterion says the outputs cluster,
not that they cluster around anything specific.
nodes.formal: The condition asserts that $f$ is uniformly small in oscillation near $c$: any two outputs from the punctured $\delta$-ball are within $\varepsilon$ of each other. Equivalently, $\omega_f(x_0) = 0$ in the language of the oscillation function.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: The condition asserts that $f$ is uniformly small in oscillation near $c$: any two outputs from the punctured $\delta$-ball are within $\varepsilon$ of each other. Equivalently, $\omega_f(x_0) = 0$ in the language of the oscillation function.
nodes.negation_display:
$f(x) = \sin(1/x)$ fails: nearby inputs give outputs differing
by up to $2$, so no $\delta$ works for $\varepsilon < 2$.

nodes.name: Change of Variables in Limits
nodes.question: State the change of variables rule for limits.
nodes.statement_display:
If $\varphi(t)\to c$ as $t\to t_0$ and $f(x)\to L$ as $x\to c$,
and $\varphi(t)\neq c$ near $t_0$ (or $f(c)=L$), then
$f(\varphi(t))\to L$ as $t\to t_0$.
nodes.statement_tex:
If $\varphi(t)\to c$ as $t\to t_0$ and $f(x)\to L$ as $x\to c$,
and $\varphi(t)\neq c$ near $t_0$ (or $f(c)=L$), then
$f(\varphi(t))\to L$ as $t\to t_0$.
nodes.hint:
This is just composition of limits. The hypothesis prevents
the pathology: $f$ might not have $f(c)=L$, and $\varphi$ might sit
exactly at $c$ for many $t$.
nodes.negation_display:
Without the hypothesis: take $f(x)=0$ for $x\neq 0$, $f(0)=1$,
$\varphi(t)=0$ for all $t$. Then $f(x)\to 0$ as $x\to 0$ but
$f(\varphi(t))=1\not\to 0$.

nodes.name: Characteristic function of the Cantor set
nodes.question: State the definition: Characteristic function of the Cantor set.

nodes.name: Composition of Limits
nodes.question: State the composition of limits theorem.
nodes.statement_display:
If $f(x)\to c_2$ as $x\to c_1$ and $g(y)\to L$ as $y\to c_2$,
  and $g(c_2)=L$ whenever $c_2\in B$, then $g(f(x))\to L$ as
  $x\to c_1$.
nodes.statement_tex:
If $f(x)\to c_2$ as $x\to c_1$ and $g(y)\to L$ as $y\to c_2$,
  and $g(c_2)=L$ whenever $c_2\in B$, then $g(f(x))\to L$ as
  $x\to c_1$.
nodes.hint:
The hypothesis $g(c_2)=L$ handles the case $f(x)=c_2$
  which the punctured-neighbourhood condition on $g$ does not cover.
nodes.negation_display:
Without $g(c_2)=L$: take $f\equiv c_2$ and $g(c_2)\neq L$.
  Then $g(f(x))=g(c_2)\neq L$ for all $x$.

nodes.name: Continuity of the distance function
nodes.question: Why is $d(x,C)$ always continuous?
nodes.statement_display:
Because $|d(x,C)-d(y,C)|\leq|x-y|$: the distance function
is Lipschitz with constant $1$.
nodes.statement_tex:
Because $|d(x,C)-d(y,C)|\leq|x-y|$: the distance function
is Lipschitz with constant $1$.
nodes.hint:
The triangle inequality for $|x-y|$ controls how fast the
distance can change as $x$ moves.
nodes.negation_display:
$\chi_C$ is not continuous on the Cantor set. The distance
function and the indicator function of the same set behave completely
differently.

nodes.name: Differentiability of $f$
nodes.question: State the proposition: Differentiability of $f$.
nodes.statement_display:
$f$ is differentiable at every $x \in \mathbb{R}$, with $f'(0) = 0$.
The derivative $f'$ is not continuous at $0$.

nodes.name: Dirichlet function
nodes.question: State the definition: Dirichlet function.

nodes.name: Distance from a point to a closed set
nodes.question: State the definition: Distance from a point to a closed set.
nodes.statement_display: \cite[§5.2.6]{ThomsonBrucknerBrucknerERA} Let $C \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be a closed non-empty set. The distance function is $d(\cdot, C) : \mathbb{R} \to [0,\infty)$ defined by \[ d(x, C) := \inf_{y \in C} |x - y|. \]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:distance-function}
\cite[§5.2.6]{ThomsonBrucknerBrucknerERA}
Let $C \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ be a closed non-empty set. The
\emph{distance function} is $d(\cdot, C) : \mathbb{R} \to [0,\infty)$
defined by
\[
  d(x, C) := \inf_{y \in C} |x - y|.
\]

nodes.name: Floor function
nodes.question: State the definition: Floor function.

nodes.name: Horizontal Asymptote
nodes.question: Name the seven indeterminate forms.
nodes.statement_display:
$0/0$, $\infty/\infty$, $0\cdot\infty$, $\infty-\infty$,
$0^0$, $1^\infty$, $\infty^0$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$0/0$, $\infty/\infty$, $0\cdot\infty$, $\infty-\infty$,
$0^0$, $1^\infty$, $\infty^0$.
nodes.hint:
The exponential forms ($0^0$, $1^\infty$, $\infty^0$) are
handled by writing $f^g = e^{g\ln f}$ and reducing to $0\cdot\infty$.
nodes.formal:
$y = y_0$ is a horizontal asymptote $\iff$
$\lim_{x\to+\infty} f(x) = y_0$ or $\lim_{x\to-\infty} f(x) = y_0$
(or both).
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$y = y_0$ is a horizontal asymptote $\iff$
$\lim_{x\to+\infty} f(x) = y_0$ or $\lim_{x\to-\infty} f(x) = y_0$
(or both).
nodes.negation_display:
$0/\infty = 0$ is NOT indeterminate. $\infty/0 = \infty$
is not indeterminate (assuming $f/g>0$ near $x_0$). Only the seven
listed forms are genuinely indeterminate.

nodes.name: Infinite Limit as \(x\to\infty\)
nodes.question: State the definition: Infinite Limit as \(x\to\infty\).
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : A \to \mathbb{R}\), and suppose that
\[
(a,\infty)\subseteq A
\]
for some \(a\in\mathbb{R}\).

We say that \(f\) tends to \(+\infty\) as \(x\to\infty\), and write
\[
\lim_{x\to\infty} f(x)=\infty,
\]
if
\[
\forall \alpha\in\mathbb{R}\;\exists K>a\;\forall x\in A:
\quad
x>K \;\Rightarrow\; f(x)>\alpha.
\]

We say that \(f\) tends to \(-\infty\) as \(x\to\infty\), and write
\[
\lim_{x\to\infty} f(x)=-\infty,
\]
if
\[
\forall \alpha\in\mathbb{R}\;\exists K>a\;\forall x\in A:
\quad
x>K \;\Rightarrow\; f(x)<\alpha.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:infinite-limit-as-x-to-infinity}
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : A \to \mathbb{R}\), and suppose that
\[
(a,\infty)\subseteq A
\]
for some \(a\in\mathbb{R}\).

We say that \(f\) tends to \(+\infty\) as \(x\to\infty\), and write
\[
\lim_{x\to\infty} f(x)=\infty,
\]
if
\[
\forall \alpha\in\mathbb{R}\;\exists K>a\;\forall x\in A:
\quad
x>K \;\Rightarrow\; f(x)>\alpha.
\]

We say that \(f\) tends to \(-\infty\) as \(x\to\infty\), and write
\[
\lim_{x\to\infty} f(x)=-\infty,
\]
if
\[
\forall \alpha\in\mathbb{R}\;\exists K>a\;\forall x\in A:
\quad
x>K \;\Rightarrow\; f(x)<\alpha.
\]

nodes.name: Infinite Limit at a Point
nodes.question: State the definition: Infinite Limit at a Point.
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : A \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(c \in \mathbb{R}\) be a cluster point of \(A\).
  1. We say that \(f\) tends to \(+\infty\) as \(x \to c\), and write \[ \lim_{x\to c} f(x)=\infty, \] if \[ \forall \alpha \in \mathbb{R}\;\exists \delta>0\;\forall x\in A: \quad 0<|x-c|<\delta \;\Rightarrow\; f(x)>\alpha. \]
  2. We say that \(f\) tends to \(-\infty\) as \(x \to c\), and write \[ \lim_{x\to c} f(x)=-\infty, \] if \[ \forall \beta \in \mathbb{R}\;\exists \delta>0\;\forall x\in A: \quad 0<|x-c|<\delta \;\Rightarrow\; f(x)<\beta. \]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:infinite-limit-at-point}
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : A \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(c \in \mathbb{R}\) be a cluster point of \(A\).

\begin{enumerate}[label=\textup{(\roman*)}]
\item We say that \(f\) tends to \(+\infty\) as \(x \to c\), and write
\[
\lim_{x\to c} f(x)=\infty,
\]
if
\[
\forall \alpha \in \mathbb{R}\;\exists \delta>0\;\forall x\in A:
\quad
0<|x-c|<\delta \;\Rightarrow\; f(x)>\alpha.
\]

\item We say that \(f\) tends to \(-\infty\) as \(x \to c\), and write
\[
\lim_{x\to c} f(x)=-\infty,
\]
if
\[
\forall \beta \in \mathbb{R}\;\exists \delta>0\;\forall x\in A:
\quad
0<|x-c|<\delta \;\Rightarrow\; f(x)<\beta.
\]
\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: Jump function
nodes.question: State the definition: Jump function.
nodes.statement_display: \cite[Definition~4.4.10]{SohrabBasicRealAnalysis2} Let $I$ be an interval, $(x_n)$ a sequence in $I$, and $(h_n)$ a sequence of positive numbers with $\sum_{n=1}^\infty h_n < \infty$. The jump function associated to these data is \[ J(x) := \sum_{x_n < x} h_n \qquad (x \in I), \] where the sum runs over all $n$ with $x_n < x$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:jump-function}
\cite[Definition~4.4.10]{SohrabBasicRealAnalysis2}
Let $I$ be an interval, $(x_n)$ a sequence in $I$, and $(h_n)$ a sequence
of positive numbers with $\sum_{n=1}^\infty h_n < \infty$.
The \emph{jump function} associated to these data is
\[
  J(x) := \sum_{x_n < x} h_n \qquad (x \in I),
\]
where the sum runs over all $n$ with $x_n < x$.

nodes.name: Limit as \(x\to\infty\)
nodes.question: State the definition: Limit as \(x\to\infty\).
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : A \to \mathbb{R}\), and suppose that
\[
(a,\infty)\subseteq A
\]
for some \(a\in\mathbb{R}\).
We say that \(L \in \mathbb{R}\) is the limit of \(f\) as \(x\to\infty\), and write
\[
\lim_{x\to\infty} f(x)=L,
\]
if
\[
\forall \varepsilon>0\;\exists K>a\;\forall x\in A:
\quad
x>K \;\Rightarrow\; |f(x)-L|<\varepsilon.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:limit-as-x-to-infinity}
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : A \to \mathbb{R}\), and suppose that
\[
(a,\infty)\subseteq A
\]
for some \(a\in\mathbb{R}\).
We say that \(L \in \mathbb{R}\) is the limit of \(f\) as \(x\to\infty\), and write
\[
\lim_{x\to\infty} f(x)=L,
\]
if
\[
\forall \varepsilon>0\;\exists K>a\;\forall x\in A:
\quad
x>K \;\Rightarrow\; |f(x)-L|<\varepsilon.
\]

nodes.name: Limit behaviour
nodes.question: State the proposition: Limit behaviour.
nodes.statement_display:
$f$ is differentiable at $0$ with $f'(0) = 0$.
$f$ is discontinuous at every $c \neq 0$.

nodes.name: Limit behaviour
nodes.question: State the proposition: Limit behaviour.
nodes.statement_display:
$f$ has no limit at any $c \in \mathbb{R}$, yet $|f(x)| = |x|$ for all $x$,
so $|f|$ is continuous everywhere.

nodes.name: Limit behaviour of $\chi_K$
nodes.question: At which points does $\chi_K$ have a limit?
nodes.statement_display:
At every $x_0 \notin K$ (limit $= 0$). At every $x_0 \in K$
the limit fails to exist.
nodes.statement_tex:
At every $x_0 \notin K$ (limit $= 0$). At every $x_0 \in K$
the limit fails to exist.
nodes.hint:
$K$ is perfect (every point is a limit of others in $K$)
and $K^c$ is dense: both witnesses are always present near any $x_0\in K$.
nodes.negation_display:
$\chi_K$ is \emph{not} the same as $d(\cdot,K) = 0$.
The distance function is continuous everywhere; the characteristic
function is not.

nodes.name: Limit behaviour of $\operatorname{sgn}$
nodes.question: State the proposition: Limit behaviour of $\operatorname{sgn}$.
nodes.statement_display: $\lim_{x\to 0^-}\operatorname{sgn}(x) = -1$ and $\lim_{x\to 0^+}\operatorname{sgn}(x) = 1$, so $\lim_{x\to 0}\operatorname{sgn}(x)$ does not exist. $\operatorname{sgn}$ is continuous on $\mathbb{R}\setminus\{0\}$ and has a jump discontinuity of magnitude $2$ at $0$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{prop:sgn-limits}
$\lim_{x\to 0^-}\operatorname{sgn}(x) = -1$ and
$\lim_{x\to 0^+}\operatorname{sgn}(x) = 1$, so
$\lim_{x\to 0}\operatorname{sgn}(x)$ does not exist.
$\operatorname{sgn}$ is continuous on $\mathbb{R}\setminus\{0\}$ and
has a \emph{jump discontinuity of magnitude $2$} at $0$.

nodes.name: Limit behaviour of Thomae's function
nodes.question: State the proposition: Limit behaviour of Thomae's function.
nodes.statement_display:
$\lim_{x\to c} T(x) = 0$ for every $c \in (0,1)$. Consequently:
  1. $T$ is continuous at every irrational $c$ (since $T(c) = 0$).
  2. $T$ is discontinuous at every rational $c$ (since $T(c) = 1/q > 0$ but $\lim T = 0$): each rational is a removable discontinuity.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{prop:thomae-limits}
$\lim_{x\to c} T(x) = 0$ for every $c \in (0,1)$.
Consequently:
\begin{enumerate}[label=\textup{(\roman*)}]
  \item $T$ is continuous at every irrational $c$ (since $T(c) = 0$).
  \item $T$ is discontinuous at every rational $c$ (since $T(c) = 1/q > 0$
        but $\lim T = 0$): each rational is a removable discontinuity.
\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: Limit of Absolute Value
nodes.question: If $\lim_{x\to c} f(x) = L$, what is $\lim_{x\to c}|f(x)|$?
nodes.statement_display: $\lim_{x\to c}|f(x)| = |L|$.
nodes.statement_tex: $\lim_{x\to c}|f(x)| = |L|$.
nodes.hint: Reverse triangle inequality: $\bigl||f(x)|-|L|\bigr| \leq |f(x)-L|$.
nodes.formal:
\[
  \lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L
  \;\Rightarrow\;
  \lim_{x \to c} |f(x)| = |L|.
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
  \lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L
  \;\Rightarrow\;
  \lim_{x \to c} |f(x)| = |L|.
\]
nodes.negation_display:
Converse fails: $|f(x)|\to|L|$ does not imply $f(x)\to L$.
  Counterexample: $\operatorname{sgn}(x)$ at $0$.

nodes.name: Limits Are Local
nodes.question: What does it mean to say that limits are local?
nodes.statement_display: Replacing the domain set $E$ by $E\cap (x_0-\delta,x_0+\delta)$ does not change the limit at $x_0$.
nodes.statement_tex: Replacing the domain set $E$ by $E\cap (x_0-\delta,x_0+\delta)$ does not change the limit at $x_0$.
nodes.hint: A limit only depends on behavior arbitrarily near the point.

nodes.name: Limits Bounded Away From Zero
nodes.question: What happens near $c$ if $\lim_{x\to c} f(x)=L\neq 0$?
nodes.statement_display:
There exist $\delta>0$ and $m>0$ such that
$|f(x)|\ge m$ whenever $0<|x-c|<\delta$.
nodes.statement_tex:
There exist $\delta>0$ and $m>0$ such that
$|f(x)|\ge m$ whenever $0<|x-c|<\delta$.
nodes.hint: Choose $\varepsilon=|L|/2$.

nodes.name: Modified Dirichlet
nodes.question: State the definition: Modified Dirichlet.

nodes.name: Monotone Limit Theorem
nodes.question: What does the Monotone Limit Theorem guarantee?
nodes.statement_display:
A bounded monotone function on $(a,b)$ has both one-sided
  limits at every interior point. For increasing $f$: $f(x_0^-) =
  \sup\{f(x):x<x_0\}$ and $f(x_0^+) = \inf\{f(x):x>x_0\}$.
nodes.statement_tex:
A bounded monotone function on $(a,b)$ has both one-sided
  limits at every interior point. For increasing $f$: $f(x_0^-) =
  \sup\{f(x):x<x_0\}$ and $f(x_0^+) = \inf\{f(x):x>x_0\}$.
nodes.hint:
Boundedness supplies sup/inf. Monotonicity forces the
  function toward those values from each side.
nodes.negation_display:
Boundedness alone is not enough: $\sin(1/x)$ is bounded
  but has no one-sided limit at $0$. Monotonicity is essential.

nodes.name: One-Sided Infinite Limits
nodes.question: State the definition: One-Sided Infinite Limits.
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : A \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(c \in \mathbb{R}\).

Suppose \(c\) is a cluster point of \(A \cap (c,\infty)\). We say that \(f\) tends to \(+\infty\) as \(x\to c^+\), and write
\[
\lim_{x\to c^+} f(x)=\infty,
\]
if
\[
\forall \alpha\in\mathbb{R}\;\exists \delta>0\;\forall x\in A:
\quad
0<x-c<\delta \;\Rightarrow\; f(x)>\alpha.
\]

Similarly, we say that \(f\) tends to \(-\infty\) as \(x\to c^+\), and write
\[
\lim_{x\to c^+} f(x)=-\infty,
\]
if
\[
\forall \alpha\in\mathbb{R}\;\exists \delta>0\;\forall x\in A:
\quad
0<x-c<\delta \;\Rightarrow\; f(x)<\alpha.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:one-sided-infinite-limits}
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : A \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(c \in \mathbb{R}\).

Suppose \(c\) is a cluster point of \(A \cap (c,\infty)\). We say that \(f\) tends to \(+\infty\) as \(x\to c^+\), and write
\[
\lim_{x\to c^+} f(x)=\infty,
\]
if
\[
\forall \alpha\in\mathbb{R}\;\exists \delta>0\;\forall x\in A:
\quad
0<x-c<\delta \;\Rightarrow\; f(x)>\alpha.
\]

Similarly, we say that \(f\) tends to \(-\infty\) as \(x\to c^+\), and write
\[
\lim_{x\to c^+} f(x)=-\infty,
\]
if
\[
\forall \alpha\in\mathbb{R}\;\exists \delta>0\;\forall x\in A:
\quad
0<x-c<\delta \;\Rightarrow\; f(x)<\alpha.
\]

nodes.name: Order Theorem for Infinite Limits
nodes.question: State the theorem: Order Theorem for Infinite Limits.
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f,g : A \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(c \in \mathbb{R}\) be a cluster point of \(A\). Suppose that \[ f(x)\le g(x)\qquad\text{for all } x\in A,\ x\ne c. \] Then:
  1. If \[ \lim_{x\to c} f(x)=\infty, \] then \[ \lim_{x\to c} g(x)=\infty. \]
  2. If \[ \lim_{x\to c} g(x)=-\infty, \] then \[ \lim_{x\to c} f(x)=-\infty. \]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:order-theorem-infinite-limits}
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f,g : A \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(c \in \mathbb{R}\) be a cluster point of \(A\).
Suppose that
\[
f(x)\le g(x)\qquad\text{for all } x\in A,\ x\ne c.
\]
Then:
\begin{enumerate}[label=\textup{(\alph*)}]
\item If
\[
\lim_{x\to c} f(x)=\infty,
\]
then
\[
\lim_{x\to c} g(x)=\infty.
\]

\item If
\[
\lim_{x\to c} g(x)=-\infty,
\]
then
\[
\lim_{x\to c} f(x)=-\infty.
\]
\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: Properties of $W$
nodes.question: State the proposition: Properties of $W$.
nodes.statement_display:
$W$ is continuous on $\mathbb{R}$ and differentiable at no point
of $\mathbb{R}$.

nodes.name: Properties of the Cantor function
nodes.question: State the proposition: Properties of the Cantor function.
nodes.statement_display:
$\varphi$ is:
  1. Monotone increasing and maps $[0,1]$ onto $[0,1]$.
  2. Continuous on $[0,1]$ (no jump discontinuities).
  3. Constant on each interval in $[0,1]\setminus C$ (the complement of the Cantor set $C$), i.e., constant on each removed middle-third open interval.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{prop:cantor-function}
$\varphi$ is:
\begin{enumerate}[label=\textup{(\roman*)}]
  \item \textbf{Monotone increasing} and maps $[0,1]$ onto $[0,1]$.
  \item \textbf{Continuous} on $[0,1]$ (no jump discontinuities).
  \item \textbf{Constant} on each interval in $[0,1]\setminus C$
        (the complement of the Cantor set $C$), i.e., constant on
        each removed middle-third open interval.
\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: Properties of the jump function
nodes.question: State the proposition: Properties of the jump function.
nodes.statement_display:
$J$ is increasing, left-continuous on $I$, continuous on
$I \setminus \{x_1, x_2, …\}$, and has a jump of magnitude $h_n$
at each $x_n$.
Moreover, every bounded left-continuous increasing function $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$
decomposes as
\[
  f = \varphi + J
\]
where $\varphi$ is continuous and increasing and $J$ is a jump function.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{prop:jump-function-properties}
$J$ is increasing, left-continuous on $I$, continuous on
$I \setminus \{x_1, x_2, \ldots\}$, and has a jump of magnitude $h_n$
at each $x_n$.
Moreover, every bounded left-continuous increasing function $f : I \to \mathbb{R}$
decomposes as
\[
  f = \varphi + J
\]
where $\varphi$ is continuous and increasing and $J$ is a jump function.

nodes.name: Quadratic Dirichlet
nodes.question: State the definition: Quadratic Dirichlet.

nodes.name: Quotient Comparison for Infinite Growth
nodes.question: What does ``$P(f(x))$ is true near $+\infty$'' mean?
nodes.statement_display: There exists $A$ such that $P(f(x))$ holds for all $x > A$.
nodes.statement_tex: There exists $A$ such that $P(f(x))$ holds for all $x > A$.
nodes.hint:
It is the ``sufficiently large $x$'' analogue of the
punctured $\delta$-ball condition for finite $x_0$.

nodes.name: Restriction Locality
nodes.question: When does restricting the domain preserve the limit?
nodes.statement_display:
When $A$ and $S$ agree near $c$: $(A\setminus\{c\})\cap
  B_\alpha(c) = (S\setminus\{c\})\cap B_\alpha(c)$ for some
  $\alpha>0$. Then $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L \iff \lim_{x\to
  c}f|_A(x)=L$.
nodes.statement_tex:
When $A$ and $S$ agree near $c$: $(A\setminus\{c\})\cap
  B_\alpha(c) = (S\setminus\{c\})\cap B_\alpha(c)$ for some
  $\alpha>0$. Then $\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L \iff \lim_{x\to
  c}f|_A(x)=L$.
nodes.hint:
Agreement near $c$ means the two domains have the same
  witnesses for any $\delta < \alpha$.
nodes.formal:
\[
  \Bigl(\exists\alpha>0,\;
  (A\setminus\{c\})\cap(c-\alpha,c+\alpha)
  =(S\setminus\{c\})\cap(c-\alpha,c+\alpha)\Bigr)
  \;\Rightarrow\;
  \Bigl(\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L
  \;\iff\; \lim_{x\to c}f|_A(x)=L\Bigr).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
  \Bigl(\exists\alpha>0,\;
  (A\setminus\{c\})\cap(c-\alpha,c+\alpha)
  =(S\setminus\{c\})\cap(c-\alpha,c+\alpha)\Bigr)
  \;\Rightarrow\;
  \Bigl(\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L
  \;\iff\; \lim_{x\to c}f|_A(x)=L\Bigr).
\]
nodes.negation_display:
One-sided limits violate this: $A=(c,\infty)$ and
  $S=\mathbb{R}$ do not agree near $c$ since the left side is
  missing from $A$.

nodes.name: Right-Hand and Left-Hand Limits
nodes.question: State the definition: Right-Hand and Left-Hand Limits.
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : A \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(c \in \mathbb{R}\).
  1. Suppose \(c\) is a cluster point of \(A \cap (c,\infty)\). We say that \(L \in \mathbb{R}\) is the right-hand limit of \(f\) at \(c\), and write \[ \lim_{x \to c^+} f(x)=L, \] if \[ \forall \varepsilon>0\;\exists \delta>0\;\forall x\in A: \quad 0
  2. Suppose \(c\) is a cluster point of \(A \cap (-\infty,c)\). We say that \(L \in \mathbb{R}\) is the left-hand limit of \(f\) at \(c\), and write \[ \lim_{x \to c^-} f(x)=L, \] if \[ \forall \varepsilon>0\;\exists \delta>0\;\forall x\in A: \quad 0
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:one-sided-limits}
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : A \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(c \in \mathbb{R}\).

\begin{enumerate}[label=\textup{(\roman*)}]
\item Suppose \(c\) is a cluster point of \(A \cap (c,\infty)\).
We say that \(L \in \mathbb{R}\) is the \emph{right-hand limit} of \(f\) at \(c\), and write
\[
\lim_{x \to c^+} f(x)=L,
\]
if
\[
\forall \varepsilon>0\;\exists \delta>0\;\forall x\in A:
\quad
0<x-c<\delta \;\Rightarrow\; |f(x)-L|<\varepsilon.
\]

\item Suppose \(c\) is a cluster point of \(A \cap (-\infty,c)\).
We say that \(L \in \mathbb{R}\) is the \emph{left-hand limit} of \(f\) at \(c\), and write
\[
\lim_{x \to c^-} f(x)=L,
\]
if
\[
\forall \varepsilon>0\;\exists \delta>0\;\forall x\in A:
\quad
0<c-x<\delta \;\Rightarrow\; |f(x)-L|<\varepsilon.
\]
\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: Sequential Criterion for Infinite Limits as \(x\to\infty\)
nodes.question: State the theorem: Sequential Criterion for Infinite Limits as \(x\to\infty\).
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : A \to \mathbb{R}\), and suppose that \[ (a,\infty)\subseteq A \] for some \(a\in\mathbb{R}\). Then the following are equivalent:
  1. \[ \lim_{x\to\infty} f(x)=\infty \qquad [\text{respectively, } \lim_{x\to\infty} f(x)=-\infty]. \]
  2. For every sequence \((x_n)\) in \((a,\infty)\) such that \[ x_n\to\infty, \] we have \[ f(x_n)\to\infty \qquad [\text{respectively, } f(x_n)\to-\infty]. \]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:sequential-criterion-infinite-limit-at-infinity}
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : A \to \mathbb{R}\), and suppose that
\[
(a,\infty)\subseteq A
\]
for some \(a\in\mathbb{R}\).
Then the following are equivalent:
\begin{enumerate}[label=\textup{(\roman*)}]
\item
\[
\lim_{x\to\infty} f(x)=\infty
\qquad
[\text{respectively, } \lim_{x\to\infty} f(x)=-\infty].
\]
\item For every sequence \((x_n)\) in \((a,\infty)\) such that
\[
x_n\to\infty,
\]
we have
\[
f(x_n)\to\infty
\qquad
[\text{respectively, } f(x_n)\to-\infty].
\]
\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: Sequential Criterion for Left-Hand Limits
nodes.question: State the corollary: Sequential Criterion for Left-Hand Limits.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in \mathbb{R}$ be a cluster point of $A \cap (-\infty, c)$. Then the following are equivalent:
  1. $\displaystyle\lim_{x \to c^-} f(x) = L$.
  2. For every sequence $(x_n)$ such that \[ x_n \in A, \qquad x_n < c \text{ for all } n, \qquad x_n \to c, \] the image sequence $(f(x_n))$ converges to $L$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:sequential-criterion-left-hand-limit}
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let
$c \in \mathbb{R}$ be a cluster point of $A \cap (-\infty, c)$.
Then the following are equivalent:
\begin{enumerate}[label=\textup{(\roman*)}]
  \item $\displaystyle\lim_{x \to c^-} f(x) = L$.
  \item For every sequence $(x_n)$ such that
        \[
          x_n \in A, \qquad x_n < c \text{ for all } n, \qquad x_n \to c,
        \]
        the image sequence $(f(x_n))$ converges to $L$.
\end{enumerate}
nodes.proof_sketch:
%
  1. ($\Rightarrow$) Fix $\varepsilon$; get $\delta$; use $x_n \to c$ and
     $x_n < c$ to find $N_0$ with $0 < c - x_n < \delta$; conclude
     $|f(x_n) - L| < \varepsilon$. \\
  2. ($\Leftarrow$) Negate limit: fix $\varepsilon_0$; for each $n$ pick
     $x_n \in A$ with $0 < c - x_n < 1/n$ and $|f(x_n) - L| \geq
     \varepsilon_0$. \\
  3. Archimedean $N_1 > 1/\varepsilon$ gives $x_n \to c$; sequence witnesses
     failure of the sequential condition.

nodes.name: Sequential Criterion for Limits as \(x\to\infty\)
nodes.question: State the theorem: Sequential Criterion for Limits as \(x\to\infty\).
nodes.statement_display:
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : A \to \mathbb{R}\), and suppose that \[ (a,\infty)\subseteq A \] for some \(a\in\mathbb{R}\). Then the following are equivalent:
  1. \[ \lim_{x\to\infty} f(x)=L. \]
  2. For every sequence \((x_n)\) in \(A\cap(a,\infty)\) such that \[ x_n\to\infty, \] the sequence \((f(x_n))\) converges to \(L\).
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:sequential-criterion-limit-at-infinity}
Let \(A \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : A \to \mathbb{R}\), and suppose that
\[
(a,\infty)\subseteq A
\]
for some \(a\in\mathbb{R}\).
Then the following are equivalent:
\begin{enumerate}[label=\textup{(\roman*)}]
\item
\[
\lim_{x\to\infty} f(x)=L.
\]
\item For every sequence \((x_n)\) in \(A\cap(a,\infty)\) such that
\[
x_n\to\infty,
\]
the sequence \((f(x_n))\) converges to \(L\).
\end{enumerate}

nodes.name: Sequential Criterion for Right-Hand Limits
nodes.question: State the theorem: Sequential Criterion for Right-Hand Limits.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in \mathbb{R}$ be a cluster point of $A \cap (c, \infty)$. Then the following are equivalent:
  1. $\displaystyle\lim_{x \to c^+} f(x) = L$.
  2. For every sequence $(x_n)$ such that \[ x_n \in A, \qquad x_n > c \text{ for all } n, \qquad x_n \to c, \] the image sequence $(f(x_n))$ converges to $L$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:sequential-criterion-right-hand-limit}
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let
$c \in \mathbb{R}$ be a cluster point of $A \cap (c, \infty)$.
Then the following are equivalent:
\begin{enumerate}[label=\textup{(\roman*)}]
  \item $\displaystyle\lim_{x \to c^+} f(x) = L$.
  \item For every sequence $(x_n)$ such that
        \[
          x_n \in A, \qquad x_n > c \text{ for all } n, \qquad x_n \to c,
        \]
        the image sequence $(f(x_n))$ converges to $L$.
\end{enumerate}
nodes.proof_sketch:
%
  1. ($\Rightarrow$) Fix $\varepsilon$; get $\delta$; use $x_n \to c$ to find $N_0$; conclude $|f(x_n)-L|<\varepsilon$. \\
  2. ($\Leftarrow$) Negate limit: fix $\varepsilon_0$; for each $n$ pick $x_n \in A$ with $0 < x_n - c < 1/n$ and $|f(x_n)-L| \geq \varepsilon_0$. \\
  3. Archimedean $N_1 > 1/\varepsilon$ gives $x_n \to c$; sequence witnesses failure of the sequential condition.

nodes.name: Sequential Definition of Limit
nodes.question:
State the sequential definition of
  $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L$.
nodes.statement_display:
For every sequence $(x_n) \subseteq A \setminus \{c\}$
  with $x_n \to c$, the sequence $f(x_n) \to L$.
nodes.statement_tex:
For every sequence $(x_n) \subseteq A \setminus \{c\}$
  with $x_n \to c$, the sequence $f(x_n) \to L$.
nodes.hint:
Equivalent to the $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ definition
  by the Sequential Criterion. The condition $x_n \neq c$ mirrors
  the $0 <$ in $0 < |x_n - c| < \delta$.
nodes.negation_display:
The sequence must lie in $A \setminus \{c\}$:
  a constant sequence $x_n = c$ is excluded, just as
  $x = c$ is excluded in the $\varepsilon$-$\delta$ form.

nodes.name: Signum Function
nodes.question: State the definition: Signum Function.
nodes.statement_display: The signum (or sign) function $\operatorname{sgn} : \mathbb{R} \to \{-1,0,1\}$ is \[ \operatorname{sgn}(x) := \begin{cases} -1 & x < 0 \\ 0 & x = 0 \\ 1 & x > 0. \end{cases} \]

nodes.name: Squeeze Theorem for Function Limits
nodes.question: State the theorem: Squeeze Theorem for Function Limits.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f, g, h : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c$ be a
cluster point of $A$. Suppose that
\[
\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L = \lim_{x \to c} g(x).
\]
If there exists $\delta_0 > 0$ such that
\[
\forall x \in A,\quad 0 < |x - c| < \delta_0 \implies f(x) \leq h(x) \leq g(x),
\]
then $\displaystyle\lim_{x \to c} h(x) = L$.

\begin{remark*}[Fully quantified form]
\[
\Bigl(\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L=\lim_{x\to c}g(x)\Bigr)
\;\wedge\;
\Bigl(\exists\delta_0>0,\;\forall x\in A,\;
0<|x-c|<\delta_0\implies f(x)\leq h(x)\leq g(x)\Bigr)
\implies \lim_{x\to c}h(x)=L.
\]
\end{remark*}

\begin{remark*}[Relationship to sequential version]
This is the function-limit analogue of the Squeeze Theorem for sequences.
The proof routes entirely through the Sequential Criterion: translate to
sequences, apply the sequential squeeze, translate back. No new $\varepsilon$-$\delta$
work is needed.
\end{remark*}

\begin{remark*}[Flash]\end{remark*}

\begin{remark*}[Proof link]
Proof: Squeeze Theorem for Function Limits
\end{remark*}
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:squeeze-function-limits}
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f, g, h : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c$ be a
cluster point of $A$. Suppose that
\[
\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L = \lim_{x \to c} g(x).
\]
If there exists $\delta_0 > 0$ such that
\[
\forall x \in A,\quad 0 < |x - c| < \delta_0 \implies f(x) \leq h(x) \leq g(x),
\]
then $\displaystyle\lim_{x \to c} h(x) = L$.

\begin{remark*}[Fully quantified form]
\[
\Bigl(\lim_{x\to c}f(x)=L=\lim_{x\to c}g(x)\Bigr)
\;\wedge\;
\Bigl(\exists\delta_0>0,\;\forall x\in A,\;
0<|x-c|<\delta_0\implies f(x)\leq h(x)\leq g(x)\Bigr)
\implies \lim_{x\to c}h(x)=L.
\]
\end{remark*}

\begin{remark*}[Relationship to sequential version]
This is the function-limit analogue of the Squeeze Theorem for sequences.
The proof routes entirely through the Sequential Criterion: translate to
sequences, apply the sequential squeeze, translate back. No new $\varepsilon$-$\delta$
work is needed.
\end{remark*}

\begin{remark*}[Flash]\end{remark*}

\begin{remark*}[Proof link]
Proof: \hyperref[prf:squeeze-function-limits]{Squeeze Theorem for Function Limits}
\end{remark*}

nodes.name: Supremum and Infimum of a Function
nodes.question: What do $\sup_A f$ and $\inf_A f$ mean?
nodes.statement_display:
$\sup_A f = \sup\{f(x):x\in A\}$ and
$\inf_A f = \inf\{f(x):x\in A\}$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$\sup_A f = \sup\{f(x):x\in A\}$ and
$\inf_A f = \inf\{f(x):x\in A\}$.
nodes.hint: They are the supremum and infimum of the range $f(A)$.
implies_titles: Supremum and Infimum of a Function

nodes.name: Symmetric Dirichlet
nodes.question: State the definition: Symmetric Dirichlet.

nodes.name: Thomae function
nodes.question: State the definition: Thomae function.

nodes.name: Three Equivalent Forms of the Function Limit
nodes.question: State the corollary: Three Equivalent Forms of the Function Limit.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$. The following are equivalent:
  1. $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L$ \hfill ($\varepsilon$-$\delta$)
  2. For every $V_\varepsilon(L)$ there exists $V_\delta(c)$ such that $x \in V_\delta^*(c) \cap A$ implies $f(x) \in V_\varepsilon(L)$. \hfill (neighbourhood)
  3. For every $(x_n) \subseteq A \setminus \{c\}$ with $x_n \to c$, $f(x_n) \to L$. \hfill (sequential)
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{cor:limit-three-equiv}
Let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$ and let $c$ be a cluster point of $A$.
The following are equivalent:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
  \item $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L$ \hfill ($\varepsilon$-$\delta$)
  \item For every $V_\varepsilon(L)$ there exists $V_\delta(c)$ such
        that $x \in V_\delta^*(c) \cap A$ implies
        $f(x) \in V_\varepsilon(L)$. \hfill (neighbourhood)
  \item For every $(x_n) \subseteq A \setminus \{c\}$ with
        $x_n \to c$, $f(x_n) \to L$. \hfill (sequential)
\end{enumerate}
nodes.proof_sketch:
1. (i)$\iff$(ii) by Neighbourhood Form proposition.
2. (i)$\iff$(iii) by Sequential Criterion proposition.
3. Transitivity gives all three equivalent.

nodes.name: Two-Sided Limit via One-Sided Limits
nodes.question: State the theorem: Two-Sided Limit via One-Sided Limits.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in \mathbb{R}$
be a cluster point of both
\[
A \cap (c,\infty)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
A \cap (-\infty,c).
\]
Then
\[
\lim_{x\to c} f(x)=L
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\lim_{x\to c^+} f(x)=L=\lim_{x\to c^-} f(x).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:two-sided-limit-iff-matching-one-sided-limits}
Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, let $f : A \to \mathbb{R}$, and let $c \in \mathbb{R}$
be a cluster point of both
\[
A \cap (c,\infty)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
A \cap (-\infty,c).
\]
Then
\[
\lim_{x\to c} f(x)=L
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\lim_{x\to c^+} f(x)=L=\lim_{x\to c^-} f(x).
\]
nodes.proof_sketch:
%
  1. $(\Rightarrow)$: two-sided $\delta$ restricts to each side directly.\\
  2. $(\Leftarrow)$: set $\delta = \min\{\delta_+, \delta_-\}$.\\
  3. Split on $x > c$ and $x < c$; apply respective one-sided hypothesis.\\
  4. Conclude $|f(x) - L| < \varepsilon$ in both cases.

nodes.name: Uniqueness of Limits of Functions
nodes.question: Why can a function have at most one limit at a cluster point?
nodes.statement_display:
Assume $\lim f = L$ and $\lim f = L'$. Set
  $\varepsilon = |L-L'|/2$. Invoke each limit definition to get
  $\delta_1$, $\delta_2$; take $\delta = \min(\delta_1,\delta_2)$.
  Triangle inequality gives $|L-L'| < |L-L'|$, contradiction.
nodes.statement_tex:
Assume $\lim f = L$ and $\lim f = L'$. Set
  $\varepsilon = |L-L'|/2$. Invoke each limit definition to get
  $\delta_1$, $\delta_2$; take $\delta = \min(\delta_1,\delta_2)$.
  Triangle inequality gives $|L-L'| < |L-L'|$, contradiction.
nodes.hint:
The key pattern: invoke the limit definition twice,
  once for each candidate, then reconcile with
  $\delta=\min(\delta_1,\delta_2)$.
nodes.formal:
\[
  \lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L
  \;\wedge\;
  \lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L'
  \;\Rightarrow\; L = L'.
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
  \lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L
  \;\wedge\;
  \lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L'
  \;\Rightarrow\; L = L'.
\]
nodes.negation_display:
Without the cluster point condition the limit can be
  vacuously satisfied by every $L$ (isolated point).
nodes.proof_sketch:
1. Assume $L \neq L'$; set $\varepsilon = |L-L'|/2 > 0$.
2. Invoke limit definition twice to get $\delta_1$, $\delta_2$; set
   $\delta = \min(\delta_1, \delta_2)$.
3. Find $x \in A$ with $0 < |x-c| < \delta$; triangle inequality gives
   $|L-L'| < |L-L'|$, contradiction.

nodes.name: Van der Waerden function
nodes.question: State the definition: Van der Waerden function.
nodes.statement_display: \cite[Theorem~8.3.8]{SohrabBasicRealAnalysis2} Let $f_0(x) := d(x, \mathbb{Z}) = \min_{n\in\mathbb{Z}}|x-n|$ be the distance from $x$ to the nearest integer (a continuous sawtooth with period $1$ and amplitude $1/2$). Define $f_n(x) := 4^{-n} f_0(4^n x)$ for $n \geq 0$. The Van der Waerden function is \[ W(x) := \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} f_n(x) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 4^{-n} f_0(4^n x). \]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:van-der-waerden}
\cite[Theorem~8.3.8]{SohrabBasicRealAnalysis2}
Let $f_0(x) := d(x, \mathbb{Z}) = \min_{n\in\mathbb{Z}}|x-n|$
be the distance from $x$ to the nearest integer (a continuous sawtooth
with period $1$ and amplitude $1/2$). Define
$f_n(x) := 4^{-n} f_0(4^n x)$ for $n \geq 0$. The
\emph{Van der Waerden function} is
\[
  W(x) := \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} f_n(x)
         = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 4^{-n} f_0(4^n x).
\]

nodes.name: Vertical Asymptote
nodes.question: Define vertical asymptote.
nodes.statement_display:
$x = x_0$ is a vertical asymptote if any one of
$\lim_{x\to x_0}f(x)$, $\lim_{x\to x_0^\pm}f(x)$ is $\pm\infty$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$x = x_0$ is a vertical asymptote if any one of
$\lim_{x\to x_0}f(x)$, $\lim_{x\to x_0^\pm}f(x)$ is $\pm\infty$.
nodes.hint:
Vertical asymptotes describe blow-up at a finite input.
Only one one-sided limit needs to be infinite.
nodes.formal:
$x = x_0$ is a vertical asymptote $\iff$
at least one of $\lim_{x\to x_0} f(x)$,
$\lim_{x\to x_0^-} f(x)$, $\lim_{x\to x_0^+} f(x)$
is $\pm\infty$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$x = x_0$ is a vertical asymptote $\iff$
at least one of $\lim_{x\to x_0} f(x)$,
$\lim_{x\to x_0^-} f(x)$, $\lim_{x\to x_0^+} f(x)$
is $\pm\infty$.
nodes.negation_display:
Both one-sided limits need not be infinite — one suffices.
$f(x)=1/x$ has $\lim_{x\to 0^+}=+\infty$ and $\lim_{x\to 0^-}=-\infty$,
both infinite but with opposite signs.

nodes.name: Volterra-type example
nodes.question: State the definition: Volterra-type example.

nodes.name: Every Power Dominates the Logarithm
nodes.question: Compare $\ln x$ and $x^r$ as $x \to \infty$ and as $x \to 0^+$.
nodes.statement_display:
$x^r$ dominates: $(\ln x)/x^r \to 0$ as $x \to \infty$;
$x^r \ln x \to 0$ as $x \to 0^+$ (even though $\ln x \to -\infty$).
nodes.statement_tex:
$x^r$ dominates: $(\ln x)/x^r \to 0$ as $x \to \infty$;
$x^r \ln x \to 0$ as $x \to 0^+$ (even though $\ln x \to -\infty$).
nodes.hint: Substitute $x = e^t$ and use exponential dominance.

nodes.name: Fundamental Logarithmic Limit
nodes.question: State the fundamental logarithmic limit.
nodes.hint: $\ln(1+x) \approx x$ near $x = 0$. The derivative of $\ln(1+x)$ at $0$ is $1$.
nodes.formal:
$\forall \varepsilon > 0\;\exists \delta > 0:\;
0 < |x| < \delta \Rightarrow |\ln(1+x)/x - 1| < \varepsilon.$
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$\forall \varepsilon > 0\;\exists \delta > 0:\;
0 < |x| < \delta \Rightarrow |\ln(1+x)/x - 1| < \varepsilon.$

nodes.name: $\mathbb{R}^2$ with Three Standard Metrics
nodes.question: State the three standard metrics on $\mathbb{R}^2$.
nodes.hint: Unit balls: diamond / disk / square.

nodes.name: $\mathbb{R}^n$ as an iterated product
nodes.question: State: $\mathbb{R}^n$ is an iterated product metric space.
nodes.statement_display: $(\mathbb{R}^n,d_2)$ is the $n$-fold product of $(\mathbb{R},|\cdot|)$ with itself.
nodes.statement_tex: $(\mathbb{R}^n,d_2)$ is the $n$-fold product of $(\mathbb{R},|\cdot|)$ with itself.

nodes.name: $d(A,B) = 0$ does not imply intersection
nodes.question: State: $d(A,B)=0$ does not imply $A\cap B\neq\varnothing$.
nodes.statement_display: In general $d(A,B)=0$ does not imply $A\cap B\neq\varnothing$.
nodes.statement_tex: In general $d(A,B)=0$ does not imply $A\cap B\neq\varnothing$.
nodes.hint: Example: $A=(0,1)$, $B=\{0\}$ in $\mathbb{R}$: $d(A,B)=0$ but $A\cap B=\varnothing$.

nodes.name: Bounded Set
nodes.question: Define a bounded set in a metric space.
nodes.statement_display: $A\subseteq X$ is bounded if $\operatorname{diam}(A)<\infty$, i.e.\ $\exists M: d(x,y)\le M$ for all $x,y\in A$.
nodes.statement_tex: $A\subseteq X$ is bounded if $\operatorname{diam}(A)<\infty$, i.e.\ $\exists M: d(x,y)\le M$ for all $x,y\in A$.
nodes.hint: Equivalently: $A$ is contained in some open ball.

nodes.name: Bounded and totally bounded coincide in $\mathbb{R}^n$
nodes.question: State: in $\mathbb{R}^n$, bounded iff totally bounded.
nodes.statement_display: $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}^n$ is totally bounded iff it is bounded.
nodes.statement_tex: $A\subseteq\mathbb{R}^n$ is totally bounded iff it is bounded.
nodes.hint: Bounded subsets of $\mathbb{R}^n$ can always be covered by finitely many $\varepsilon$-boxes.

nodes.name: Bounded does not imply totally bounded
nodes.question: State: bounded does not imply totally bounded.
nodes.statement_display: The unit ball in an infinite-dimensional space (or any infinite discrete metric space) is bounded but not totally bounded.
nodes.statement_tex: The unit ball in an infinite-dimensional space (or any infinite discrete metric space) is bounded but not totally bounded.

nodes.name: Convergence in a product is component-wise
nodes.question: State convergence in the product metric.
nodes.statement_display: $(x_n,y_n)\to(x,y)$ in $X\times Y$ iff $x_n\to x$ in $X$ and $y_n\to y$ in $Y$.
nodes.statement_tex: $(x_n,y_n)\to(x,y)$ in $X\times Y$ iff $x_n\to x$ in $X$ and $y_n\to y$ in $Y$.
nodes.hint: Since product metrics are equivalent and each is controlled by coordinate distances.

nodes.name: Coordinate Metric Spaces
nodes.question: Define coordinate projections in a product metric space.
nodes.statement_display: The coordinate projections $\pi_X:X\times Y\to X$ and $\pi_Y:X\times Y\to Y$ are continuous; $(x_n,y_n)\to(x,y)$ iff $x_n\to x$ and $y_n\to y$.
nodes.statement_tex: The coordinate projections $\pi_X:X\times Y\to X$ and $\pi_Y:X\times Y\to Y$ are continuous; $(x_n,y_n)\to(x,y)$ iff $x_n\to x$ and $y_n\to y$.

nodes.name: Diameter
nodes.question: Define the diameter of a set.
nodes.statement_display: $\operatorname{diam}(A):=\sup\{d(x,y):x,y\in A\}$. $\operatorname{diam}(\varnothing)=0$.
nodes.statement_tex: $\operatorname{diam}(A):=\sup\{d(x,y):x,y\in A\}$. $\operatorname{diam}(\varnothing)=0$.
nodes.hint: Diameter measures the greatest spread in a set.

nodes.name: Discrete Metric
nodes.question: Is a discrete metric space complete?
nodes.statement_display: Every discrete metric space is complete: Cauchy sequences are eventually constant, hence convergent.
nodes.statement_tex: Every discrete metric space is complete: Cauchy sequences are eventually constant, hence convergent.
nodes.hint: For $\varepsilon < 1$, Cauchy condition forces $x_m = x_n$ for large $m, n$.
nodes.formal:
\[
\forall x \in X,\;
B_{1/2}(x) = \{x\},
\]
so $\{x\}$ is open for every $x$, and any $S \subseteq X$ is a union
of open singletons, hence open.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\forall x \in X,\;
B_{1/2}(x) = \{x\},
\]
so $\{x\}$ is open for every $x$, and any $S \subseteq X$ is a union
of open singletons, hence open.

nodes.name: Discrete Metric Space
nodes.question: Define a discrete metric space.
nodes.statement_display: $(X,d)$ is discrete if every subset is open, equivalently every point is isolated.
nodes.statement_tex: $(X,d)$ is discrete if every subset is open, equivalently every point is isolated.
nodes.hint: The discrete metric $d(x,y)=\mathbf{1}_{x\neq y}$ makes any set discrete.

nodes.name: Distance Between Sets
nodes.question: Define the distance between two sets.
nodes.statement_display: $d(A,B):=\inf\{d(a,b):a\in A,b\in B\}$ for nonempty $A,B\subseteq X$.
nodes.statement_tex: $d(A,B):=\inf\{d(a,b):a\in A,b\in B\}$ for nonempty $A,B\subseteq X$.
nodes.hint: $d(A,B)=0$ does NOT imply $A\cap B\neq\varnothing$.

nodes.name: Distance from a Point to a Set
nodes.question: Define the distance from a point to a set.
nodes.statement_display: $d(x,A):=\inf\{d(x,a):a\in A\}$ for $x\in X$, $A\neq\varnothing$.
nodes.statement_tex: $d(x,A):=\inf\{d(x,a):a\in A\}$ for $x\in X$, $A\neq\varnothing$.
nodes.hint: $d(x,A)=0\iff x\in\overline{A}$.
implies_titles: Distance zero to a set characterizes closure

nodes.name: Distance zero to a set characterizes closure
nodes.question: State: $d(x,A)=0$ iff $x\in\overline{A}$.
nodes.statement_display: $d(x,A)=0\iff x\in\overline{A}$.
nodes.statement_tex: $d(x,A)=0\iff x\in\overline{A}$.
nodes.hint: $d(x,A)=0$ means every ball around $x$ meets $A$, which is exactly the definition of adherence.

nodes.name: Isolated Point
nodes.question: Define an isolated point in a metric space.
nodes.statement_display: $x\in X$ is isolated if $\{x\}$ is open in $X$, i.e.\ $\exists r>0: B_r(x)=\{x\}$.
nodes.statement_tex: $x\in X$ is isolated if $\{x\}$ is open in $X$, i.e.\ $\exists r>0: B_r(x)=\{x\}$.

nodes.name: Monotonicity of Diameter
nodes.question: State Monotonicity of Diameter.
nodes.statement_display: If $A\subseteq B\subseteq X$ then $\operatorname{diam}(A)\le\operatorname{diam}(B)$.
nodes.statement_tex: If $A\subseteq B\subseteq X$ then $\operatorname{diam}(A)\le\operatorname{diam}(B)$.
nodes.hint: $\operatorname{diam}(A)=\sup_{A\times A}d$; restricting to a subset can only decrease or preserve the sup.

nodes.name: Product Metric
nodes.question: Define the product metric space.
nodes.statement_display: $(X\times Y,d)$ with $d$ being any of $d_1=d_X+d_Y$, $d_2=\sqrt{d_X^2+d_Y^2}$, $d_\infty=\max(d_X,d_Y)$; all equivalent.
nodes.statement_tex: $(X\times Y,d)$ with $d$ being any of $d_1=d_X+d_Y$, $d_2=\sqrt{d_X^2+d_Y^2}$, $d_\infty=\max(d_X,d_Y)$; all equivalent.

nodes.name: Product Metrics
nodes.question: State the three equivalent product metrics.
nodes.statement_display: $d_2((x_1,y_1),(x_2,y_2))=\sqrt{d_X^2+d_Y^2}$; $d_1=d_X+d_Y$; $d_\infty=\max(d_X,d_Y)$; all are equivalent.
nodes.statement_tex: $d_2((x_1,y_1),(x_2,y_2))=\sqrt{d_X^2+d_Y^2}$; $d_1=d_X+d_Y$; $d_\infty=\max(d_X,d_Y)$; all are equivalent.
implies_titles: The three product metrics are equivalent

nodes.name: Symmetry of set distance
nodes.question: State: set distance is symmetric.
nodes.statement_display: $d(A,B)=d(B,A)$ for nonempty $A,B\subseteq X$.
nodes.statement_tex: $d(A,B)=d(B,A)$ for nonempty $A,B\subseteq X$.

nodes.name: The three product metrics are equivalent
nodes.question: State the equivalence of product metrics.
nodes.statement_display: $d_\infty\le d_2\le d_1\le 2d_\infty$ on $X\times Y$; in particular $d_1,d_2,d_\infty$ are equivalent.
nodes.statement_tex: $d_\infty\le d_2\le d_1\le 2d_\infty$ on $X\times Y$; in particular $d_1,d_2,d_\infty$ are equivalent.

nodes.name: Totally Bounded
nodes.question: State the definition: Totally Bounded.
nodes.statement_display: Let $(X, d)$ be a metric space and $A \subseteq X$. We say $A$ is totally bounded if for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there exist finitely many points $x_1, …, x_n \in X$ such that \[ A \;\subseteq\; \bigcup_{k=1}^n B_\varepsilon(x_k). \] The finite collection $\{x_1, …, x_n\}$ is called an $\varepsilon$-net for $A$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:totally-bounded}
Let $(X, d)$ be a metric space and $A \subseteq X$.
We say $A$ is \emph{totally bounded} if for every $\varepsilon > 0$ there
exist finitely many points $x_1, \ldots, x_n \in X$ such that
\[
A \;\subseteq\; \bigcup_{k=1}^n B_\varepsilon(x_k).
\]
The finite collection $\{x_1, \ldots, x_n\}$ is called an
\emph{$\varepsilon$-net} for $A$.

nodes.name: Totally bounded implies bounded
nodes.question: State: totally bounded implies bounded.
nodes.statement_display: If $A\subseteq X$ is totally bounded then $A$ is bounded.
nodes.statement_tex: If $A\subseteq X$ is totally bounded then $A$ is bounded.
nodes.hint: The finite cover by $\varepsilon$-balls has bounded union.

nodes.name: Totally bounded sets have Cauchy subsequences
nodes.question: State: totally bounded sets have Cauchy subsequences.
nodes.statement_display: If $A\subseteq X$ is totally bounded then every sequence in $A$ has a Cauchy subsequence.
nodes.statement_tex: If $A\subseteq X$ is totally bounded then every sequence in $A$ has a Cauchy subsequence.
nodes.hint: Diagonal argument: at each stage pass to a subsequence in a ball of radius $1/k$.

nodes.name: Unit Ball under $d_1$
nodes.question: Describe the unit ball of $d_1$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$.
nodes.statement_display: $B_1^{d_1}(0)=\{(x_1,x_2):|x_1|+|x_2|<1\}$ — a diamond (rotated square).
nodes.statement_tex: $B_1^{d_1}(0)=\{(x_1,x_2):|x_1|+|x_2|<1\}$ — a diamond (rotated square).

nodes.name: Unit Ball under $d_2$
nodes.question: Describe the unit ball of $d_2$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$.

nodes.name: Unit Ball under $d_\infty$
nodes.question: Describe the unit ball of $d_\infty$ on $\mathbb{R}^2$.
nodes.statement_display: $B_1^{d_\infty}(0)=\{(x_1,x_2):|x_1|<1\text{ and }|x_2|<1\}$ — an open square.
nodes.statement_tex: $B_1^{d_\infty}(0)=\{(x_1,x_2):|x_1|<1\text{ and }|x_2|<1\}$ — an open square.

nodes.name: Unit Ball under Standard $\times$ Discrete Product
nodes.question: Describe the unit ball under the standard$\times$discrete product metric on $\mathbb{R}^2$.
nodes.statement_display: Using $d=|x_1-x_2|+\mathbf{1}_{y_1\neq y_2}$: the unit ball centred at $(0,0)$ is $\{(x,0):|x|<1\}$ — a horizontal open interval.
nodes.statement_tex: Using $d=|x_1-x_2|+\mathbf{1}_{y_1\neq y_2}$: the unit ball centred at $(0,0)$ is $\{(x,0):|x|<1\}$ — a horizontal open interval.

nodes.name: Unit Ball under Standard $\times$ Standard Product
nodes.question: Describe the unit ball under the standard$\times$standard product metric on $\mathbb{R}^2$.
nodes.statement_display: Using $d_\mathrm{sum}=|x_1-y_1|+|x_2-y_2|$: the unit ball is the same diamond as $d_1$.
nodes.statement_tex: Using $d_\mathrm{sum}=|x_1-y_1|+|x_2-y_2|$: the unit ball is the same diamond as $d_1$.

nodes.name: Antimetric
nodes.question: Define an antimetric.
nodes.statement_display: Like a metric but with reversed triangle inequality $d(x,z)\ge d(x,y)+d(y,z)$. Any such function is identically zero.
nodes.statement_tex: Like a metric but with reversed triangle inequality $d(x,z)\ge d(x,y)+d(y,z)$. Any such function is identically zero.

nodes.name: Continuity of Distance
nodes.question: State continuity of the distance function.
nodes.statement_display: For fixed $z$, $f(x):=d(x,z)$ is 1-Lipschitz: $|d(x,z)-d(y,z)|\le d(x,y)$.
nodes.statement_tex: For fixed $z$, $f(x):=d(x,z)$ is 1-Lipschitz: $|d(x,z)-d(y,z)|\le d(x,y)$.

nodes.name: Distance to Itself
nodes.question: State: $d(x,x)=0$.
nodes.statement_display: $d(x,x)=0$ for all $x$ (from M2: $d(x,x)=0\iff x=x$).
nodes.statement_tex: $d(x,x)=0$ for all $x$ (from M2: $d(x,x)=0\iff x=x$).

nodes.name: Equivalent Metrics
nodes.question: Define equivalent metrics.
nodes.statement_display: $d_1\sim d_2$ if they induce the same topology: every $d_1$-open set is $d_2$-open and vice versa.
nodes.statement_tex: $d_1\sim d_2$ if they induce the same topology: every $d_1$-open set is $d_2$-open and vice versa.
nodes.hint: Equivalent iff they have the same convergent sequences.

nodes.name: Generalised Triangle Inequality
nodes.question: State the Generalised Triangle Inequality.
nodes.statement_display: $d(x_0,x_n)\le\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}d(x_k,x_{k+1})$ for any finite chain in $X$.
nodes.statement_tex: $d(x_0,x_n)\le\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}d(x_k,x_{k+1})$ for any finite chain in $X$.
nodes.hint: Induction on $n$ using M4.

nodes.name: Metric
nodes.question: State the four axioms defining a metric.
nodes.hint: Non-negativity, identity of indiscernibles, symmetry, triangle inequality.

nodes.name: Positivity
nodes.question: State: $x\neq y\Rightarrow d(x,y)>0$.
nodes.statement_display: If $x\neq y$ then $d(x,y)>0$ (by M1 and M2).
nodes.statement_tex: If $x\neq y$ then $d(x,y)>0$ (by M1 and M2).

nodes.name: Reverse Triangle Inequality
nodes.question: State the proposition: Reverse Triangle Inequality.
nodes.statement_display:
For all $x,y,z \in X$,
\[
|d(x,z) - d(y,z)| \le d(x,y).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{prop:reverse-triangle}
For all $x,y,z \in X$,
\[
|d(x,z) - d(y,z)| \le d(x,y).
\]

nodes.name: Ultrametric
nodes.question: Define an ultrametric.
nodes.statement_display: $d$ is an ultrametric if $d(x,z)\le\max\{d(x,y),d(y,z)\}$ (strong triangle inequality).
nodes.statement_tex: $d$ is an ultrametric if $d(x,z)\le\max\{d(x,y),d(y,z)\}$ (strong triangle inequality).
nodes.hint: In ultrametric spaces every triangle is isosceles with the two equal sides longest.

nodes.name: Exponentiation — Tao 2.3.11
nodes.question: State the definition: Exponentiation — Tao 2.3.11.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $m \in \mathbb{N}$. Exponentiation is defined recursively:
\[
m^0 := 1, \qquad m^{n\pp} := m^n \times m.
\]
In particular, $0^0 := 1$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:exp}
Let $m \in \mathbb{N}$. Exponentiation is defined recursively:
\[
m^0 := 1, \qquad m^{n\pp} := m^n \times m.
\]
In particular, $0^0 := 1$.

nodes.name: Tao 2.3.2 — Commutativity
nodes.question: State the lemma: Tao 2.3.2 — Commutativity.
nodes.statement_display: For all $n, m \in \mathbb{N}$,\quad $n \times m = m \times n$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{lem:mult-comm}
For all $n, m \in \mathbb{N}$,\quad $n \times m = m \times n$.

nodes.name: Tao 2.3.3 — No zero divisors
nodes.question: State the lemma: Tao 2.3.3 — No zero divisors.
nodes.statement_display:
For all $n, m \in \mathbb{N}$,\quad $n \times m = 0$ if and only if
$n = 0$ or $m = 0$.

In particular, if $n$ and $m$ are both positive, then $nm$ is positive.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{lem:no-zero-div}
For all $n, m \in \mathbb{N}$,\quad $n \times m = 0$ if and only if
$n = 0$ or $m = 0$.

In particular, if $n$ and $m$ are both positive, then $nm$ is positive.

nodes.name: Tao 2.3.4 — Distributive law
nodes.question: State the proposition: Tao 2.3.4 — Distributive law.
nodes.statement_display:
For all $a, b, c \in \mathbb{N}$:
\[
a(b+c) = ab + ac \qquad \text{and} \qquad (b+c)a = ba + ca.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{prop:distrib}
For all $a, b, c \in \mathbb{N}$:
\[
a(b+c) = ab + ac \qquad \text{and} \qquad (b+c)a = ba + ca.
\]

nodes.name: Tao 2.3.5 — Associativity
nodes.question: State the proposition: Tao 2.3.5 — Associativity.
nodes.statement_display: For all $a, b, c \in \mathbb{N}$,\quad $(a \times b) \times c = a \times (b \times c)$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{prop:mult-assoc}
For all $a, b, c \in \mathbb{N}$,\quad $(a \times b) \times c = a \times (b \times c)$.

nodes.name: Tao 2.3.6 — Multiplication preserves order
nodes.question: State the proposition: Tao 2.3.6 — Multiplication preserves order.
nodes.statement_display: If $a < b$ and $c$ is positive, then $ac < bc$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{prop:mult-order}
If $a < b$ and $c$ is positive, then $ac < bc$.

nodes.name: Tao 2.3.7 — Cancellation for multiplication
nodes.question: State the corollary: Tao 2.3.7 — Cancellation for multiplication.
nodes.statement_display: Let $a, b, c \in \mathbb{N}$ with $ac = bc$ and $c \neq 0$. Then $a = b$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{cor:mult-cancel}
Let $a, b, c \in \mathbb{N}$ with $ac = bc$ and $c \neq 0$. Then $a = b$.

nodes.name: Tao 2.3.9 — Euclidean algorithm
nodes.question: State the proposition: Tao 2.3.9 — Euclidean algorithm.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $n \in \mathbb{N}$ and let $q$ be positive. Then there exist
$m, r \in \mathbb{N}$ such that:
\[
0 \leq r < q \qquad \text{and} \qquad n = mq + r.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{prop:euclid}
Let $n \in \mathbb{N}$ and let $q$ be positive. Then there exist
$m, r \in \mathbb{N}$ such that:
\[
0 \leq r < q \qquad \text{and} \qquad n = mq + r.
\]

nodes.name: Adherent Point
nodes.question: Define an adherent point of $A$.
nodes.statement_display: $x$ is adherent to $A$ if $\forall r>0,B_r(x)\cap A\neq\varnothing$.
nodes.statement_tex: $x$ is adherent to $A$ if $\forall r>0,B_r(x)\cap A\neq\varnothing$.
nodes.hint: Compare: limit point requires $B_r(x)\cap(A\setminus\{x\})\neq\varnothing$.

nodes.name: Balls are Open and Closed
nodes.question: State: open balls are open; closed balls are closed.
nodes.statement_display: $B_r(x)$ is open; $\overline{B}_r(x)$ is closed.
nodes.statement_tex: $B_r(x)$ is open; $\overline{B}_r(x)$ is closed.
nodes.hint: For open ball: for any $y\in B_r(x)$ take $\delta=r-d(x,y)>0$; then $B_\delta(y)\subseteq B_r(x)$.

nodes.name: Boundary
nodes.question: Define the boundary $\partial A$.
nodes.statement_display: $\partial A:=\{x\in X:\forall r>0,B_r(x)\cap A\neq\varnothing\text{ and }B_r(x)\cap(X\setminus A)\neq\varnothing\}$.
nodes.statement_tex: $\partial A:=\{x\in X:\forall r>0,B_r(x)\cap A\neq\varnothing\text{ and }B_r(x)\cap(X\setminus A)\neq\varnothing\}$.
nodes.hint: Boundary points cannot be pushed into either $A$ or its complement.

nodes.name: Boundary via Closure
nodes.question: State: boundary via closure.
nodes.statement_display: $\partial A=\overline{A}\cap\overline{X\setminus A}$ and $\overline{A}=A\cup\partial A$.
nodes.statement_tex: $\partial A=\overline{A}\cap\overline{X\setminus A}$ and $\overline{A}=A\cup\partial A$.

nodes.name: Closed Sets Contain Their Boundary
nodes.question: When does a set contain its boundary?
nodes.statement_display: $A$ is closed $\iff$ $\partial A \subseteq A$.
nodes.statement_tex: $A$ is closed $\iff$ $\partial A \subseteq A$.
nodes.hint: $A$ closed means it contains all its limit points; boundary points are limit points from outside, so closure captures them.

nodes.name: Closure
nodes.question: Define the closure $\overline{A}$.
nodes.statement_display: $\overline{A}:=\{x\in X:\forall r>0,B_r(x)\cap A\neq\varnothing\}$ — the set of all adherent points.
nodes.statement_tex: $\overline{A}:=\{x\in X:\forall r>0,B_r(x)\cap A\neq\varnothing\}$ — the set of all adherent points.
nodes.hint: Smallest closed set containing $A$; equals $A\cup A'$.

nodes.name: Exterior
nodes.question: Define the exterior $\operatorname{ext}(A)$.
nodes.statement_display: $\operatorname{ext}(A):=\operatorname{int}(X\setminus A)$ — points with a ball entirely outside $A$.
nodes.statement_tex: $\operatorname{ext}(A):=\operatorname{int}(X\setminus A)$ — points with a ball entirely outside $A$.

nodes.name: Interior
nodes.question: Define the interior $\operatorname{int}(A)$.
nodes.statement_display: $\operatorname{int}(A):=\{x\in A:\exists r>0,B_r(x)\subseteq A\}$ — largest open set inside $A$.
nodes.statement_tex: $\operatorname{int}(A):=\{x\in A:\exists r>0,B_r(x)\subseteq A\}$ — largest open set inside $A$.

nodes.name: Interior--Boundary--Exterior Decomposition
nodes.question: State the Interior-Boundary-Exterior Decomposition.
nodes.statement_display: $X=\operatorname{int}(A)\sqcup\partial A\sqcup\operatorname{ext}(A)$ — a disjoint partition.
nodes.statement_tex: $X=\operatorname{int}(A)\sqcup\partial A\sqcup\operatorname{ext}(A)$ — a disjoint partition.
nodes.hint: Every point has a ball entirely in $A$ (interior), entirely outside $A$ (exterior), or neither (boundary).

nodes.name: Open Set, Closed Set
nodes.question: Define open and closed sets in a metric space.
nodes.statement_display: $U$ is open if $\forall x\in U\,\exists r>0:B_r(x)\subseteq U$. $F$ is closed if $X\setminus F$ is open.
nodes.statement_tex: $U$ is open if $\forall x\in U\,\exists r>0:B_r(x)\subseteq U$. $F$ is closed if $X\setminus F$ is open.
nodes.hint: Open = every point has breathing room inside. Closed = complement is open.
nodes.negation_display: $[0,1)$ is neither open nor closed in $\mathbb{R}$.

nodes.name: Trivial Open and Closed Sets
nodes.question: State: $\varnothing$ and $X$ are both open and closed.
nodes.statement_display: In any metric space $\varnothing$ and $X$ are clopen: open because the condition is vacuously true; closed because their complements ($X$ and $\varnothing$) are open.
nodes.statement_tex: In any metric space $\varnothing$ and $X$ are clopen: open because the condition is vacuously true; closed because their complements ($X$ and $\varnothing$) are open.

nodes.name: Negation of Continuity at a Point
nodes.question: What is the negation of continuity at a point?
nodes.statement_display:
A function \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) is not continuous at \(x_0\in E\) if and only if there exists \(\varepsilon_0>0\) such that for every \(\delta>0\), there exists \(x\in E\) with
\[
|x-x_0|<\delta
\quad\text{and}\quad
|f(x)-f(x_0)|\ge \varepsilon_0.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
A function \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) is not continuous at \(x_0\in E\) if and only if there exists \(\varepsilon_0>0\) such that for every \(\delta>0\), there exists \(x\in E\) with
\[
|x-x_0|<\delta
\quad\text{and}\quad
|f(x)-f(x_0)|\ge \varepsilon_0.
\]
nodes.hint: Negate the quantifiers in order: \(\forall\exists\forall\) becomes \(\exists\forall\exists\), and the conclusion \(<\varepsilon\) becomes \(\ge \varepsilon\).
nodes.formal:
Let \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : E \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(x_0\in E\). Then
\[
f \text{ is not continuous at } x_0
\]
if and only if
\[
\exists \varepsilon_0>0\;\forall \delta>0\;\exists x\in E:
\bigl(|x-x_0|<\delta \ \wedge\ |f(x)-f(x_0)|\ge \varepsilon_0\bigr).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
Let \(E \subseteq \mathbb{R}\), let \(f : E \to \mathbb{R}\), and let \(x_0\in E\). Then
\[
f \text{ is not continuous at } x_0
\]
if and only if
\[
\exists \varepsilon_0>0\;\forall \delta>0\;\exists x\in E:
\bigl(|x-x_0|<\delta \ \wedge\ |f(x)-f(x_0)|\ge \varepsilon_0\bigr).
\]

nodes.name: $(1 + 1/x)^x \to e$
nodes.question: What is $\lim_{x\to\infty}(1+1/x)^x$?
nodes.hint: Write as $e^{x\ln(1+1/x)}$ and use $\ln(1+t)/t \to 1$.

nodes.name: Exponential Dominates Every Power
nodes.question: Compare $x^r$ and $e^x$ as $x \to \infty$.
nodes.statement_display: $e^x$ dominates: $x^r/e^x \to 0$ for every $r > 0$.
nodes.statement_tex: $e^x$ dominates: $x^r/e^x \to 0$ for every $r > 0$.
nodes.hint: One term of the series $e^x \geq x^n/n!$ is enough to dominate any power.

nodes.name: Fundamental Exponential Limit
nodes.question: State the fundamental exponential limit.
nodes.hint: $e^x \approx 1 + x$ near $x = 0$. This is the first-order Taylor approximation.
nodes.formal:
$\forall \varepsilon > 0\;\exists \delta > 0:\;
0 < |x| < \delta \Rightarrow |(e^x-1)/x - 1| < \varepsilon.$
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$\forall \varepsilon > 0\;\exists \delta > 0:\;
0 < |x| < \delta \Rightarrow |(e^x-1)/x - 1| < \varepsilon.$

nodes.name: Power Function
nodes.question: Define $x^r$ for $r \in \mathbb{R}$, $x > 0$.
nodes.statement_display: $x^r := e^{r\ln x}$.
nodes.statement_tex: $x^r := e^{r\ln x}$.
nodes.hint:
This unifies integer, rational, and irrational exponents into
one definition and makes differentiability immediate.
nodes.formal:
$\forall r \in \mathbb{R},\; \forall x > 0,\; x^r := e^{r\ln x}$.
For $r \in \mathbb{N}$ this reduces to $\underbrace{x \cdot x \cdots x}_r$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$\forall r \in \mathbb{R},\; \forall x > 0,\; x^r := e^{r\ln x}$.
For $r \in \mathbb{N}$ this reduces to $\underbrace{x \cdot x \cdots x}_r$.

nodes.name: The Number $e$
nodes.question: Define $e^x$ and state its two key properties.
nodes.statement_display:
$e^x = \sum_{n=0}^\infty x^n/n!$.
Properties: $e^{x+y} = e^xe^y$ and $(e^x)' = e^x$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$e^x = \sum_{n=0}^\infty x^n/n!$.
Properties: $e^{x+y} = e^xe^y$ and $(e^x)' = e^x$.
nodes.hint:
The functional equation converts addition to multiplication.
The self-derivative property is unique to base $e$.
nodes.negation_display:
$2^x$ is an exponential but $(2^x)' = 2^x \ln 2 \neq 2^x$.
Only $e^x$ is its own derivative.

nodes.name: Predicate Substitution under Equality
nodes.question: State the theorem: Predicate Substitution under Equality.
nodes.statement_display:
If $t_1 = t_2$ and $P$ is an $n$-ary predicate symbol, then
