$P(…,t_1,…) \;\Leftrightarrow\; P(…,t_2,…)$.
nodes.formal: $\forall t_1, t_2:\; t_1=t_2 \to \forall$ predicate symbol $P$: $P(…,t_1,…) \leftrightarrow P(…,t_2,…)$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $\forall t_1, t_2:\; t_1=t_2 \to \forall$ predicate symbol $P$: $P(…,t_1,…) \leftrightarrow P(…,t_2,…)$.

nodes.name: Soundness of First-Order Logic
nodes.question: State the theorem: Soundness of First-Order Logic.
nodes.statement_display:
The standard inference rules for first-order logic (UI, UG, EI, EG, and the
propositional rules) are sound: they preserve truth in all structures.

If $\Gamma \vdash \varphi$, then $\Gamma \models \varphi$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:fol-sound}
The standard inference rules for first-order logic (UI, UG, EI, EG, and the
propositional rules) are sound: they preserve truth in all structures.

If $\Gamma \vdash \varphi$, then $\Gamma \models \varphi$.
nodes.formal: $\forall \Gamma,\varphi:\; \Gamma\vdash\varphi \;\to\; \forall\mathcal{M},\forall s,\; (\forall\gamma\in\Gamma,\; \mathcal{M},s\models\gamma) \to \mathcal{M},s\models\varphi$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $\forall \Gamma,\varphi:\; \Gamma\vdash\varphi \;\to\; \forall\mathcal{M},\forall s,\; (\forall\gamma\in\Gamma,\; \mathcal{M},s\models\gamma) \to \mathcal{M},s\models\varphi$.

nodes.name: Symmetry of Equality
nodes.question: State the theorem: Symmetry of Equality.
nodes.statement_display: From $t_1 = t_2$, one may infer $t_2 = t_1$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:eq-sym}
From $t_1 = t_2$, one may infer $t_2 = t_1$.

nodes.name: Term Substitution under Equality
nodes.question: State the theorem: Term Substitution under Equality.
nodes.statement_display:
If $t_1 = t_2$, then for any function symbol $f$,
$f(…,t_1,…) = f(…,t_2,…)$.
nodes.formal: $\forall t_1, t_2:\; t_1=t_2 \to \forall$ function symbol $f$: $f(…,t_1,…) = f(…,t_2,…)$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $\forall t_1, t_2:\; t_1=t_2 \to \forall$ function symbol $f$: $f(…,t_1,…) = f(…,t_2,…)$.

nodes.name: Transitivity of Equality
nodes.question: State the theorem: Transitivity of Equality.
nodes.statement_display: From $t_1 = t_2$ and $t_2 = t_3$, one may infer $t_1 = t_3$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:eq-trans}
From $t_1 = t_2$ and $t_2 = t_3$, one may infer $t_1 = t_3$.
nodes.formal: $\forall t_1, t_2, t_3:\; t_1=t_2 \wedge t_2=t_3 \to t_1=t_3$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $\forall t_1, t_2, t_3:\; t_1=t_2 \wedge t_2=t_3 \to t_1=t_3$.

nodes.name: Negation of Bounded Quantifiers
nodes.question: State the theorem: Negation of Bounded Quantifiers.
nodes.formal:
$
eg( orall x\,(x\in A 	o \varphi)) \;\equiv\; \exists x\,(x\in A \wedge 
eg\varphi)$, and $
eg(\exists x\,(x\in A \wedge \varphi)) \;\equiv\;  orall x\,(x\in A 	o 
eg\varphi)$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$
eg( orall x\,(x\in A 	o \varphi)) \;\equiv\; \exists x\,(x\in A \wedge 
eg\varphi)$, and $
eg(\exists x\,(x\in A \wedge \varphi)) \;\equiv\;  orall x\,(x\in A 	o 
eg\varphi)$.

nodes.name: Quantifier Commutation
nodes.question: State the proposition: Quantifier Commutation.
nodes.statement_display: Quantifiers of the same type commute: \begin{align*} \forall x\,\forall y\,\varphi &\;\equiv\; \forall y\,\forall x\,\varphi, \\ \exists x\,\exists y\,\varphi &\;\equiv\; \exists y\,\exists x\,\varphi. \end{align*} However, quantifiers of different types do not commute in general: \[ \forall x\,\exists y\,\varphi \;\not\equiv\; \exists y\,\forall x\,\varphi. \]

nodes.name: Quantifier Distribution
nodes.question: State the proposition: Quantifier Distribution.

nodes.name: Quantifier Negation Laws
nodes.question: State the proposition: Quantifier Negation Laws.

nodes.name: Renaming Bound Variables
nodes.question: State the proposition: Renaming Bound Variables.

nodes.name: Vacuous Quantification
nodes.question: State the proposition: Vacuous Quantification.

nodes.name: Substitution Lemma for Formulas
nodes.question: State the lemma: Substitution Lemma for Formulas.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\varphi$ be a formula, $t$ a term free for $x$ in $\varphi$. Then for
any structure $\mathcal{M}$ and assignment $s$,
\[
\mathcal{M},s \models \varphi[t/x]
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\mathcal{M},\,s\bigl[x\mapsto \llbracket t \rrbracket_{\mathcal{M},s}\bigr] \models \varphi.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{lem:subst-formulas}
Let $\varphi$ be a formula, $t$ a term free for $x$ in $\varphi$. Then for
any structure $\mathcal{M}$ and assignment $s$,
\[
\mathcal{M},s \models \varphi[t/x]
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\mathcal{M},\,s\bigl[x\mapsto \llbracket t \rrbracket_{\mathcal{M},s}\bigr] \models \varphi.
\]

nodes.name: Substitution Lemma for Terms
nodes.question: State the lemma: Substitution Lemma for Terms.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\mathcal{M}$ be a structure, $s$ a variable assignment, $x$ a variable,
and $d \in D$. For any term $t$,
\[
\llbracket t \rrbracket_{\mathcal{M},\,s[x\mapsto d]}
=
\llbracket t[d/x] \rrbracket_{\mathcal{M},\,s}.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{lem:subst-terms}
Let $\mathcal{M}$ be a structure, $s$ a variable assignment, $x$ a variable,
and $d \in D$. For any term $t$,
\[
\llbracket t \rrbracket_{\mathcal{M},\,s[x\mapsto d]}
=
\llbracket t[d/x] \rrbracket_{\mathcal{M},\,s}.
\]

nodes.name: $\varepsilon$-characterisation of $\sup$
nodes.question: State the $\varepsilon$-characterisation of $\sup$ (proof-techniques version).
nodes.statement_display: If $s=\sup S$ then $\forall\varepsilon>0\,\exists x\in S: x>s-\varepsilon$.
nodes.statement_tex: If $s=\sup S$ then $\forall\varepsilon>0\,\exists x\in S: x>s-\varepsilon$.

nodes.name: Bolzano--Weierstrass via bisection
nodes.question: State Bolzano–Weierstrass.
nodes.statement_display: Every bounded sequence in $\mathbb{R}$ has a convergent subsequence.
nodes.statement_tex: Every bounded sequence in $\mathbb{R}$ has a convergent subsequence.
nodes.hint: Extract a monotone subsequence; bounded monotone sequences converge by MCT.

nodes.name: Convergent sequences are bounded
nodes.question: State: convergent sequences are bounded.
nodes.statement_display: If $(b_n)\to b$ then $\exists M>0$ with $|b_n|\le M$ for all $n$.
nodes.statement_tex: If $(b_n)\to b$ then $\exists M>0$ with $|b_n|\le M$ for all $n$.
nodes.hint: Take $\varepsilon=1$: beyond $N$ all terms lie in $(b-1,b+1)$; finitely many terms before $N$ give a finite bound.

nodes.name: Intermediate Value Theorem
nodes.question: State the Intermediate Value Theorem.
nodes.statement_display: If $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ is continuous and $f(a)<L<f(b)$ (or $f(a)>L>f(b)$), then $\exists c\in(a,b): f(c)=L$.
nodes.statement_tex: If $f:[a,b]\to\mathbb{R}$ is continuous and $f(a)<L<f(b)$ (or $f(a)>L>f(b)$), then $\exists c\in(a,b): f(c)=L$.
nodes.hint: Bisection: maintain $f(a_n)<L<f(b_n)$; the intersection point $c$ satisfies $f(c)=L$ by continuity.

nodes.name: Order arithmetic
nodes.question: State the order arithmetic properties of $\mathbb{R}$.
nodes.statement_display: (i) $a\le b,c\le d\Rightarrow a+c\le b+d$; (ii) $a\le b,c>0\Rightarrow ac\le bc$; (iii) $a\le b,c<0\Rightarrow ac\ge bc$.
nodes.statement_tex: (i) $a\le b,c\le d\Rightarrow a+c\le b+d$; (ii) $a\le b,c>0\Rightarrow ac\le bc$; (iii) $a\le b,c<0\Rightarrow ac\ge bc$.
nodes.hint: Multiplication by a negative flips the inequality.

nodes.name: Residue divergence criterion
nodes.question: State the Residue Divergence Criterion.
nodes.statement_display: If residue subsequences $(a_{kn+r})\to L$ and $(a_{kn+s})\to M$ with $L\neq M$, then $(a_n)$ diverges.
nodes.statement_tex: If residue subsequences $(a_{kn+r})\to L$ and $(a_{kn+s})\to M$ with $L\neq M$, then $(a_n)$ diverges.

nodes.name: The dichotomy
nodes.question: State the IO/EV dichotomy.
nodes.statement_display: For any predicate $P$: exactly one of ($P$ infinitely often) or ($\neg P$ eventually) holds.
nodes.statement_tex: For any predicate $P$: exactly one of ($P$ infinitely often) or ($\neg P$ eventually) holds.

nodes.name: prop:r-uncountable
nodes.question: State: $\mathbb{R}$ is uncountable.
nodes.statement_display: $\mathbb{R}$ is uncountable: any proposed enumeration can be contradicted by a nested-interval construction.
nodes.statement_tex: $\mathbb{R}$ is uncountable: any proposed enumeration can be contradicted by a nested-interval construction.

nodes.name: Compactness of Logical Consequence
nodes.question: State the corollary: Compactness of Logical Consequence.
nodes.statement_display: If $\Gamma \models \varphi$, then there exists a finite subset $\Gamma_0 \subseteq \Gamma$ with $\Gamma_0 \models \varphi$. Equivalently: logical consequence from an infinite premise set always depends on only finitely many premises.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{cor:consequence-compact}
If $\Gamma \models \varphi$, then there exists a \emph{finite} subset
$\Gamma_0 \subseteq \Gamma$ with $\Gamma_0 \models \varphi$.

Equivalently: logical consequence from an infinite premise set always depends
on only finitely many premises.

nodes.name: Biconditional Elimination
nodes.question: State the definition: Biconditional Elimination.

nodes.name: Biconditional Introduction
nodes.question: State the definition: Biconditional Introduction.

nodes.name: Conditional Introduction (Conditional Proof)
nodes.question: State the definition: Conditional Introduction (Conditional Proof).
nodes.statement_display:
If assuming $P$ in a subproof leads to $Q$, then discharge the assumption and
infer $P \rightarrow Q$:
\[
\frac{[P \vdash Q]}{P \rightarrow Q}
\]

nodes.name: Conjunction Elimination
nodes.question: State the definition: Conjunction Elimination.
nodes.statement_display:
From a conjunction, infer either conjunct:
\[
\frac{P \wedge Q}{P} \qquad \frac{P \wedge Q}{Q}
\]

nodes.name: Conjunction Introduction
nodes.question: State the definition: Conjunction Introduction.
nodes.statement_display:
From two formulas, infer their conjunction:
\[
\frac{P \qquad Q}{P \wedge Q}
\]

nodes.name: Constructive Dilemma
nodes.question: State the definition: Constructive Dilemma.

nodes.name: Destructive Dilemma
nodes.question: State the definition: Destructive Dilemma.
nodes.statement_display:
\[
 rac{P ightarrow R \qquad Q ightarrow S \qquad 
eg R \vee 
eg S}{
eg P \vee 
eg Q}
\]

nodes.name: Disjunction Elimination (Proof by Cases)
nodes.question: State the definition: Disjunction Elimination (Proof by Cases).
nodes.statement_display:
From a disjunction, if the same conclusion $R$ follows from each disjunct, infer $R$:
\[
\frac{P \vee Q \qquad [P \vdash R] \qquad [Q \vdash R]}{R}
\]

nodes.name: Disjunction Introduction
nodes.question: State the definition: Disjunction Introduction.
nodes.statement_display:
From a proposition, infer any disjunction containing it:
\[
\frac{P}{P \vee Q}
\]

nodes.name: Double Negation Elimination
nodes.question: State the definition: Double Negation Elimination.

nodes.name: Hypothetical Syllogism
nodes.question: State the definition: Hypothetical Syllogism.
nodes.statement_display:
From two conditionals chained, infer the composed conditional:
\[
\frac{P \rightarrow Q \qquad Q \rightarrow R}{P \rightarrow R}
\]

nodes.name: Modus Ponens
nodes.question: State the definition: Modus Ponens.
nodes.statement_display:
From a conditional and its antecedent, infer the consequent:
\[
\frac{P \rightarrow Q \qquad P}{Q}
\]

nodes.name: Modus Tollens
nodes.question: State the definition: Modus Tollens.
nodes.statement_display:
From a conditional and the negation of its consequent, infer the negation of
the antecedent:
\[
\frac{P \rightarrow Q \qquad \neg Q}{\neg P}
\]

nodes.name: Negation Elimination (Indirect Proof)
nodes.question: State the definition: Negation Elimination (Indirect Proof).
nodes.statement_display:
If assuming $\neg P$ leads to $\bot$, infer $P$:
\[
\frac{[\neg P \vdash \bot]}{P}
\]

nodes.name: Negation Introduction (Reductio ad Absurdum)
nodes.question: State the definition: Negation Introduction (Reductio ad Absurdum).
nodes.statement_display:
If assuming $P$ leads to contradiction $\bot$, infer $\neg P$:
\[
\frac{[P \vdash \bot]}{\neg P}
\]

nodes.name: Absorption
nodes.question: State the proposition: Absorption.
nodes.statement_display: $P \wedge (P \vee Q) \equiv P$;\qquad $P \vee (P \wedge Q) \equiv P$
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{eq:absorb}
$P \wedge (P \vee Q) \equiv P$;\qquad $P \vee (P \wedge Q) \equiv P$

nodes.name: Associativity
nodes.question: State the proposition: Associativity.
nodes.statement_display:
$(P \wedge Q) \wedge R \equiv P \wedge (Q \wedge R)$;\qquad
$(P \vee Q) \vee R \equiv P \vee (Q \vee R)$

nodes.name: Biconditional Expansion
nodes.question: State the proposition: Biconditional Expansion.
nodes.statement_display: $P \leftrightarrow Q \;\equiv\; (P \rightarrow Q) \wedge (Q \rightarrow P)$
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{eq:bicond-exp}
$P \leftrightarrow Q \;\equiv\; (P \rightarrow Q) \wedge (Q \rightarrow P)$

nodes.name: Commutativity
nodes.question: State the proposition: Commutativity.
nodes.statement_display: $P \wedge Q \equiv Q \wedge P$;\qquad $P \vee Q \equiv Q \vee P$
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{eq:comm}
$P \wedge Q \equiv Q \wedge P$;\qquad $P \vee Q \equiv Q \vee P$

nodes.name: Contraposition
nodes.question: State the proposition: Contraposition.
nodes.statement_display: $P \rightarrow Q \;\equiv\; \neg Q \rightarrow \neg P$
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{eq:contra}
$P \rightarrow Q \;\equiv\; \neg Q \rightarrow \neg P$

nodes.name: De Morgan's Laws
nodes.question: State the proposition: De Morgan's Laws.

nodes.name: Distributivity
nodes.question: State the proposition: Distributivity.
nodes.statement_display:
$P \wedge (Q \vee R) \equiv (P \wedge Q) \vee (P \wedge R)$;\qquad
$P \vee (Q \wedge R) \equiv (P \vee Q) \wedge (P \vee R)$

nodes.name: Domination Laws
nodes.question: State the proposition: Domination Laws.
nodes.statement_display: $P \vee \top \equiv \top$;\qquad $P \wedge \bot \equiv \bot$
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{eq:domination}
$P \vee \top \equiv \top$;\qquad $P \wedge \bot \equiv \bot$

nodes.name: Double Negation
nodes.question: State the proposition: Double Negation.
nodes.statement_display: $\neg\neg P \;\equiv\; P$
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{eq:double-neg}
$\neg\neg P \;\equiv\; P$

nodes.name: Every Formula Has a CNF
nodes.question: State the proposition: Every Formula Has a CNF.
nodes.statement_display: Every propositional formula is logically equivalent to a formula in CNF.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{prop:cnf-exists}
Every propositional formula is logically equivalent to a formula in CNF.
nodes.formal: $\forall\varphi:\; \exists\psi = \bigwedge_i \bigvee_j L_{ij}$ (conjunction of clauses, each literal),\; $\varphi \equiv \psi$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $\forall\varphi:\; \exists\psi = \bigwedge_i \bigvee_j L_{ij}$ (conjunction of clauses, each literal),\; $\varphi \equiv \psi$.

nodes.name: Every Formula Has a DNF
nodes.question: State the proposition: Every Formula Has a DNF.
nodes.statement_display: Every propositional formula is logically equivalent to a formula in DNF.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{prop:dnf-exists}
Every propositional formula is logically equivalent to a formula in DNF.
nodes.formal: $\forall\varphi:\; \exists\psi = \bigvee_i \bigwedge_j L_{ij}$ (disjunction of terms, each literal),\; $\varphi \equiv \psi$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $\forall\varphi:\; \exists\psi = \bigvee_i \bigwedge_j L_{ij}$ (disjunction of terms, each literal),\; $\varphi \equiv \psi$.

nodes.name: Every Formula Has an NNF
nodes.question: State the proposition: Every Formula Has an NNF.
nodes.statement_display: Every propositional formula is logically equivalent to a formula in NNF.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{prop:nnf-exists}
Every propositional formula is logically equivalent to a formula in NNF.
nodes.formal: $\forall\varphi:\; \exists\psi \text{ in NNF},\; \varphi \equiv \psi$ (same truth value under every assignment $v: \mathrm{Var}\to\{\mathsf{T},\mathsf{F}\}$).
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $\forall\varphi:\; \exists\psi \text{ in NNF},\; \varphi \equiv \psi$ (same truth value under every assignment $v: \mathrm{Var}\to\{\mathsf{T},\mathsf{F}\}$).

nodes.name: Exportation / Importation
nodes.question: State the proposition: Exportation / Importation.
nodes.statement_display: $(P \wedge Q) \rightarrow R \;\equiv\; P \rightarrow (Q \rightarrow R)$
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{eq:export}
$(P \wedge Q) \rightarrow R \;\equiv\; P \rightarrow (Q \rightarrow R)$

nodes.name: Idempotence
nodes.question: State the proposition: Idempotence.
nodes.statement_display: $P \wedge P \equiv P$;\qquad $P \vee P \equiv P$
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{eq:idemp}
$P \wedge P \equiv P$;\qquad $P \vee P \equiv P$

nodes.name: Identity Laws
nodes.question: State the proposition: Identity Laws.
nodes.statement_display: $P \wedge \top \equiv P$;\qquad $P \vee \bot \equiv P$
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{eq:identity}
$P \wedge \top \equiv P$;\qquad $P \vee \bot \equiv P$

nodes.name: Material Implication
nodes.question: State the proposition: Material Implication.
nodes.statement_display: $P \rightarrow Q \;\equiv\; \neg P \vee Q$
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{eq:mat-impl}
$P \rightarrow Q \;\equiv\; \neg P \vee Q$

nodes.name: Negation Laws
nodes.question: State the proposition: Negation Laws.
nodes.statement_display:
$P \vee \neg P \equiv \top$ \quad (Law of Excluded Middle);\qquad
$P \wedge \neg P \equiv \bot$ \quad (Law of Non-Contradiction)

nodes.name: Negation of Conditional
nodes.question: State the proposition: Negation of Conditional.
nodes.statement_display: $\neg(P \rightarrow Q) \;\equiv\; P \wedge \neg Q$
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{eq:neg-cond}
$\neg(P \rightarrow Q) \;\equiv\; P \wedge \neg Q$

nodes.name: Non-Associativity of NAND
nodes.question: State the proposition: Non-Associativity of NAND.
nodes.statement_display: NAND is not associative: $(P \uparrow Q) \uparrow R \not\equiv P \uparrow (Q \uparrow R)$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{prop:nand-complete}
NAND is \textbf{not associative}: $(P \uparrow Q) \uparrow R \not\equiv P \uparrow (Q \uparrow R)$.

nodes.name: Non-Associativity of NOR
nodes.question: State the proposition: Non-Associativity of NOR.
nodes.statement_display: NOR is not associative: $(P \downarrow Q) \downarrow R \not\equiv P \downarrow (Q \downarrow R)$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{prop:nor-complete}
NOR is \textbf{not associative}: $(P \downarrow Q) \downarrow R \not\equiv P \downarrow (Q \downarrow R)$.

nodes.name: Addition and multiplication
nodes.question: State the definition: Addition and multiplication.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $[(a,b)],[(c,d)]\in\mathbb{Q}$, where $(a,b),(c,d)\in W$.  Define
\[
[(a,b)] + [(c,d)] := [(ad+bc,bd)]
\]
and
\[
[(a,b)]\cdot[(c,d)] := [(ac,bd)].
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:rational-operations}

Let $[(a,b)],[(c,d)]\in\mathbb{Q}$, where $(a,b),(c,d)\in W$.  Define
\[
[(a,b)] + [(c,d)] := [(ad+bc,bd)]
\]
and
\[
[(a,b)]\cdot[(c,d)] := [(ac,bd)].
\]

nodes.name: Archimedean Property
nodes.question: State the definition: Archimedean Property.
nodes.statement_display: An ordered field $F$ is said to satisfy the Archimedean property if for every element $x \in F$ there exists a natural number $n$ such that \[ n > x. \]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:archimedean}

An ordered field $F$ is said to satisfy the \emph{Archimedean property}
if for every element $x \in F$ there exists a natural number $n$ such
that

\[
n > x.
\]

nodes.name: Archimedean property of $\mathbb{Q}$
nodes.question: State the theorem: Archimedean property of $\mathbb{Q}$.
nodes.statement_display:
For every $x\in\mathbb{Q}$ there exists $n\in\mathbb{N}$ such that
\[
n>x.
\]
Equivalently, for every $x>0$ there exists $n\in\mathbb{N}$ such that
\[
\frac{1}{n}<x.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:archimedean-q}
For every $x\in\mathbb{Q}$ there exists $n\in\mathbb{N}$ such that
\[
n>x.
\]
Equivalently, for every $x>0$ there exists $n\in\mathbb{N}$ such that
\[
\frac{1}{n}<x.
\]

nodes.name: Cauchy sequence in $\mathbb{Q}$
nodes.question: State the definition: Cauchy sequence in $\mathbb{Q}$.
nodes.statement_display: A sequence $(x_n)$ of rational numbers is called a Cauchy sequence if \[ \forall \varepsilon>0\ \exists N\in\mathbb{N}\ \forall m,n\ge N, \qquad |x_m-x_n|<\varepsilon. \]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:cauchy-rational}
A sequence $(x_n)$ of rational numbers is called a \emph{Cauchy sequence} if
\[
\forall \varepsilon>0\ \exists N\in\mathbb{N}\ \forall m,n\ge N,
\qquad
|x_m-x_n|<\varepsilon.
\]

nodes.name: Denominator growth on infinite Stern--Brocot paths
nodes.question: State the lemma: Denominator growth on infinite Stern--Brocot paths.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $a_0/b_0, a_1/b_1, a_2/b_2, …$ be the sequence of fractions
encountered along any infinite path in the Stern–Brocot tree (i.e.\ the
sequence of interval-endpoint fractions at successive levels). Then
\[
  b_0 < b_1 < b_2 < \cdots,
\]
and in particular $b_n \to \infty$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{lem:sb-denom-growth}
Let $a_0/b_0, a_1/b_1, a_2/b_2, \ldots$ be the sequence of fractions
encountered along any infinite path in the Stern--Brocot tree (i.e.\ the
sequence of interval-endpoint fractions at successive levels). Then
\[
  b_0 < b_1 < b_2 < \cdots,
\]
and in particular $b_n \to \infty$.
nodes.formal:
$\forall n \in \mathbb{N},\ b_n < b_{n+1}$.
In particular, $\forall M > 0\ \exists N \in \mathbb{N}\ \forall n \ge N,\ b_n > M$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$\forall n \in \mathbb{N},\ b_n < b_{n+1}$.
In particular, $\forall M > 0\ \exists N \in \mathbb{N}\ \forall n \ge N,\ b_n > M$.

nodes.name: Density of $\mathbb{Q}$ in $\mathbb{R}$
nodes.question: State the theorem: Density of $\mathbb{Q}$ in $\mathbb{R}$.
nodes.statement_display:
If $x,y\in\mathbb{R}$ and $x<y$, then there exists $q\in\mathbb{Q}$ such that
\[
x<q<y.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:q-dense-in-r}
If $x,y\in\mathbb{R}$ and $x<y$, then there exists $q\in\mathbb{Q}$ such that
\[
x<q<y.
\]

nodes.name: Density of $\mathbb{Q}$ within itself
nodes.question: State the corollary: Density of $\mathbb{Q}$ within itself.
nodes.statement_display:
If $r,s\in\mathbb{Q}$ with $r<s$, then there exists $t\in\mathbb{Q}$ such that
\[
r<t<s.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{cor:q-dense-in-q}
If $r,s\in\mathbb{Q}$ with $r<s$, then there exists $t\in\mathbb{Q}$ such that
\[
r<t<s.
\]

nodes.name: Dirichlet's Approximation Theorem
nodes.question: State the theorem: Dirichlet's Approximation Theorem.
nodes.statement_display:
For every real number $x$ there exist infinitely many rational numbers $p/q$ with $p\in\mathbb{Z}$ and
$q\in\mathbb{N}$ such that
\[
\left|x-\frac{p}{q}\right|<\frac{1}{q^2}.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:dirichlet-approx}
For every real number $x$ there exist infinitely many rational numbers $p/q$ with $p\in\mathbb{Z}$ and
$q\in\mathbb{N}$ such that
\[
\left|x-\frac{p}{q}\right|<\frac{1}{q^2}.
\]

nodes.name: Distinguished elements
nodes.question: State the definition: Distinguished elements.
nodes.statement_display:
Define
\[
0 := [(0,1)]
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
1 := [(1,1)].
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:rational-distinguished}

Define
\[
0 := [(0,1)]
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
1 := [(1,1)].
\]

nodes.name: Equality of fraction pairs
nodes.question: State the definition: Equality of fraction pairs.
nodes.statement_display:
For $(a,b),(c,d)\in W$, define
\[
(a,b) \sim (c,d)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
ad = bc.
\]

In fully quantified form,
\[
\forall (a,b),(c,d)\in W,
\qquad
(a,b)\sim(c,d)
\Longleftrightarrow
ad=bc.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:fraction-pair-equality}

For $(a,b),(c,d)\in W$, define
\[
(a,b) \sim (c,d)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
ad = bc.
\]

In fully quantified form,
\[
\forall (a,b),(c,d)\in W,
\qquad
(a,b)\sim(c,d)
\Longleftrightarrow
ad=bc.
\]

nodes.name: Every infinite Stern--Brocot path converges
nodes.question: State the corollary: Every infinite Stern--Brocot path converges.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $x > 0$ be a real number and let $(I_n)$ be the Stern–Brocot
refinement sequence targeting $x$.  Then the sequence of left endpoints
$(a_n/b_n)$ and the sequence of right endpoints $(c_n/d_n)$ both
converge in $\mathbb{R}$ to a common limit $\xi$, and $\xi = x$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{cor:sb-path-limit}
Let $x > 0$ be a real number and let $(I_n)$ be the Stern--Brocot
refinement sequence targeting $x$.  Then the sequence of left endpoints
$(a_n/b_n)$ and the sequence of right endpoints $(c_n/d_n)$ both
converge in $\mathbb{R}$ to a common limit $\xi$, and $\xi = x$.
nodes.formal:
$\displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{a_n}{b_n}
 = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{c_n}{d_n} = x$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$\displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{a_n}{b_n}
 = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{c_n}{d_n} = x$.

nodes.name: Farey neighbor determinant condition
nodes.question: State the theorem: Farey neighbor determinant condition.
nodes.statement_display:
Suppose
\[
\frac{a}{b}<\frac{c}{d}
\qquad\text{with}\qquad
\gcd(a,b)=\gcd(c,d)=1,
\quad b,d>0.
\]
If
\[
bc-ad=1,
\]
then the two fractions are neighbors in a Farey sequence, and their mediant is already in reduced
form:
\[
\frac{a+c}{b+d}.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:farey-neighbor}
Suppose
\[
\frac{a}{b}<\frac{c}{d}
\qquad\text{with}\qquad
\gcd(a,b)=\gcd(c,d)=1,
\quad b,d>0.
\]
If
\[
bc-ad=1,
\]
then the two fractions are neighbors in a Farey sequence, and their mediant is already in reduced
form:
\[
\frac{a+c}{b+d}.
\]

nodes.name: Fraction pairs
nodes.question: State the definition: Fraction pairs.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\mathbb{Z}$ denote the set of integers and define
\[
W = \mathbb{Z} \times (\mathbb{Z}\setminus\{0\}).
\]
Thus
\[
W = \{(a,b) : a,b\in\mathbb{Z},\ b\neq 0\}.
\]

Each ordered pair $(a,b)$ is intended to represent the formal fraction $a/b$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:fraction-pairs}

Let $\mathbb{Z}$ denote the set of integers and define
\[
W = \mathbb{Z} \times (\mathbb{Z}\setminus\{0\}).
\]
Thus
\[
W = \{(a,b) : a,b\in\mathbb{Z},\ b\neq 0\}.
\]

Each ordered pair $(a,b)$ is intended to represent the formal fraction $a/b$.

nodes.name: Gap Between Farey Neighbors
nodes.question: State the theorem: Gap Between Farey Neighbors.
nodes.statement_display:
Let

\[
\frac{a}{b} < \frac{c}{d}
\]

be consecutive fractions in a Farey sequence.

Then

\[
\frac{c}{d} - \frac{a}{b}
=
\frac{1}{bd}.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:farey-gap}

Let

\[
\frac{a}{b} < \frac{c}{d}
\]

be consecutive fractions in a Farey sequence.

Then

\[
\frac{c}{d} - \frac{a}{b}
=
\frac{1}{bd}.
\]

nodes.name: Infinite Stern--Brocot paths converge to irrationals
nodes.question: State the corollary: Infinite Stern--Brocot paths converge to irrationals.
nodes.statement_display:
A positive real number $x$ corresponds to a finite path in the
Stern–Brocot tree if and only if $x$ is rational.  Equivalently,
$x$ corresponds to an infinite path if and only if $x$ is irrational.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{cor:sb-limit-irrational}
A positive real number $x$ corresponds to a finite path in the
Stern--Brocot tree if and only if $x$ is rational.  Equivalently,
$x$ corresponds to an infinite path if and only if $x$ is irrational.
nodes.formal:
$x \in \mathbb{Q}_{>0}\ \Longleftrightarrow\ $ the Stern–Brocot refinement
process for $x$ terminates in finitely many steps.
Equivalently:
$x \notin \mathbb{Q}\ \Longleftrightarrow\ $ the refinement process
runs for infinitely many steps.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$x \in \mathbb{Q}_{>0}\ \Longleftrightarrow\ $ the Stern–Brocot refinement
process for $x$ terminates in finitely many steps.
Equivalently:
$x \notin \mathbb{Q}\ \Longleftrightarrow\ $ the refinement process
runs for infinitely many steps.

nodes.name: Mediant
nodes.question: State the definition: Mediant.
nodes.statement_display: Let \[ \frac{a}{b},\frac{c}{d}\in\mathbb{Q}, \qquad b,d>0. \] The mediant of these two fractions is \[ \frac{a+c}{b+d}. \]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:mediant}
Let
\[
\frac{a}{b},\frac{c}{d}\in\mathbb{Q},
\qquad b,d>0.
\]
The \emph{mediant} of these two fractions is
\[
\frac{a+c}{b+d}.
\]

nodes.name: Mediant Gap Lemma
nodes.question: State the lemma: Mediant Gap Lemma.
nodes.statement_display:
If

\[
\frac{a}{b} < \frac{c}{d},
\]

then the mediant

\[
\frac{a+c}{b+d}
\]

lies between them, and the gaps satisfy

\[
\frac{a+c}{b+d}-\frac{a}{b}
=
\frac{1}{b(b+d)}
\]

and

\[
\frac{c}{d}-\frac{a+c}{b+d}
=
\frac{1}{d(b+d)}.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{lem:mediant-gap}

If

\[
\frac{a}{b} < \frac{c}{d},
\]

then the mediant

\[
\frac{a+c}{b+d}
\]

lies between them, and the gaps satisfy

\[
\frac{a+c}{b+d}-\frac{a}{b}
=
\frac{1}{b(b+d)}
\]

and

\[
\frac{c}{d}-\frac{a+c}{b+d}
=
\frac{1}{d(b+d)}.
\]

nodes.name: Mediant inequality
nodes.question: State the theorem: Mediant inequality.
nodes.statement_display:
If
\[
\frac{a}{b}<\frac{c}{d}
\qquad\text{with}\qquad b,d>0,
\]
then
\[
\frac{a}{b}<\frac{a+c}{b+d}<\frac{c}{d}.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:mediant-inequality}
If
\[
\frac{a}{b}<\frac{c}{d}
\qquad\text{with}\qquad b,d>0,
\]
then
\[
\frac{a}{b}<\frac{a+c}{b+d}<\frac{c}{d}.
\]

nodes.name: Nested Stern--Brocot intervals contain a unique limit point
nodes.question: State the proposition: Nested Stern--Brocot intervals contain a unique limit point.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(I_n)_{n \ge 0}$ be the nested sequence of closed rational intervals
generated by the Stern–Brocot refinement process targeting a real number
$x > 0$, with $I_n = [a_n/b_n,\; c_n/d_n]$.  Then there exists a unique
real number $\xi \in \bigcap_{n=0}^{\infty} I_n$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{prop:sb-unique-point}
Let $(I_n)_{n \ge 0}$ be the nested sequence of closed rational intervals
generated by the Stern--Brocot refinement process targeting a real number
$x > 0$, with $I_n = [a_n/b_n,\; c_n/d_n]$.  Then there exists a unique
real number $\xi \in \bigcap_{n=0}^{\infty} I_n$.
nodes.formal:
$\exists!\, \xi \in \mathbb{R}$ such that
$\forall n \in \mathbb{N},\ a_n/b_n \le \xi \le c_n/d_n$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$\exists!\, \xi \in \mathbb{R}$ such that
$\forall n \in \mathbb{N},\ a_n/b_n \le \xi \le c_n/d_n$.

nodes.name: Positive cone
nodes.question: State the definition: Positive cone.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $F$ be a field. A subset $P\subset F$ is called a positive cone if it satisfies:
  1. if $a,b\in P$, then $a+b\in P$;
  2. if $a,b\in P$, then $ab\in P$;
  3. for every $a\in F$, exactly one of the following holds: \[ a\in P, \qquad a=0, \qquad -a\in P. \]
Define the order relation by \[ a
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:positive-cone}
Let $F$ be a field.  A subset $P\subset F$ is called a \emph{positive cone} if it satisfies:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item if $a,b\in P$, then $a+b\in P$;
  \item if $a,b\in P$, then $ab\in P$;
  \item for every $a\in F$, exactly one of the following holds:
  \[
  a\in P,
  \qquad
  a=0,
  \qquad
  -a\in P.
  \]
\end{enumerate}
Define the order relation by
\[
a<b \iff b-a\in P.
\]

nodes.name: Rational approximation
nodes.question: State the theorem: Rational approximation.
nodes.statement_display:
For every real number $x$ and every $\varepsilon>0$, there exists $r\in\mathbb{Q}$ such that
\[
|x-r|<\varepsilon.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:rational-approximation}
For every real number $x$ and every $\varepsilon>0$, there exists $r\in\mathbb{Q}$ such that
\[
|x-r|<\varepsilon.
\]

nodes.name: Rational numbers
nodes.question: State the definition: Rational numbers.
nodes.statement_display: The set of rational numbers is the quotient \[ \mathbb{Q} = W/\sim. \] If $(a,b)\in W$, its equivalence class is denoted by \[ [(a,b)]. \] We interpret $[(a,b)]$ as the rational number represented informally by $a/b$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:rational-numbers}

The set of \emph{rational numbers} is the quotient
\[
\mathbb{Q} = W/\sim.
\]
If $(a,b)\in W$, its equivalence class is denoted by
\[
[(a,b)].
\]
We interpret $[(a,b)]$ as the rational number represented informally by $a/b$.

nodes.name: Sequence of rational numbers
nodes.question: State the definition: Sequence of rational numbers.
nodes.statement_display: A sequence of rational numbers is a function \[ x:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{Q}. \] It is usually written $(x_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ or simply $x_1,x_2,x_3,…$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:rational-sequence}
A \emph{sequence of rational numbers} is a function
\[
x:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{Q}.
\]
It is usually written $(x_n)_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$ or simply $x_1,x_2,x_3,\dots$.

nodes.name: Stern--Brocot enumeration
nodes.question: State the theorem: Stern--Brocot enumeration.
nodes.statement_display: Every positive rational number appears exactly once in reduced form in the Stern–Brocot tree.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:stern-brocot-enumeration}
Every positive rational number appears exactly once in reduced form in the Stern--Brocot tree.

nodes.name: Stern--Brocot interval lengths shrink to zero
nodes.question: State the lemma: Stern--Brocot interval lengths shrink to zero.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(I_n)_{n \ge 0}$ be the sequence of nested rational intervals generated
by the Stern–Brocot refinement process targeting a real number $x > 0$,
where $I_n = [a_n/b_n,\; c_n/d_n]$.  Write $\ell_n = c_n/d_n - a_n/b_n$
for the length of $I_n$.  Then $\ell_n \to 0$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{lem:sb-length-zero}
Let $(I_n)_{n \ge 0}$ be the sequence of nested rational intervals generated
by the Stern--Brocot refinement process targeting a real number $x > 0$,
where $I_n = [a_n/b_n,\; c_n/d_n]$.  Write $\ell_n = c_n/d_n - a_n/b_n$
for the length of $I_n$.  Then $\ell_n \to 0$.
nodes.formal: $\forall \varepsilon > 0\ \exists N \in \mathbb{N}\ \forall n \ge N,\ \ell_n < \varepsilon$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $\forall \varepsilon > 0\ \exists N \in \mathbb{N}\ \forall n \ge N,\ \ell_n < \varepsilon$.

nodes.name: Stern--Brocot interval refinement
nodes.question: State the theorem: Stern--Brocot interval refinement.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $x>0$ be a real number.  Suppose one begins with two rational
numbers

\[
\frac{a}{b} < x < \frac{c}{d},
\qquad b,d>0,
\]

and repeatedly inserts the mediant

\[
\frac{a+c}{b+d}.
\]

At each step, replace one endpoint by the mediant so that the new
interval still contains $x$.  Then this process generates a nested
sequence of rational intervals containing $x$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:stern-brocot-refinement}

Let $x>0$ be a real number.  Suppose one begins with two rational
numbers

\[
\frac{a}{b} < x < \frac{c}{d},
\qquad b,d>0,
\]

and repeatedly inserts the mediant

\[
\frac{a+c}{b+d}.
\]

At each step, replace one endpoint by the mediant so that the new
interval still contains $x$.  Then this process generates a nested
sequence of rational intervals containing $x$.

nodes.name: cor:infinitely-many-rationals-between
nodes.question: State the corollary: cor:infinitely-many-rationals-between.
nodes.statement_display: Between any two distinct real numbers there are infinitely many rational numbers.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{cor:infinitely-many-rationals-between}
Between any two distinct real numbers there are infinitely many rational numbers.

nodes.name: cor:n-positive
nodes.question: State the corollary: cor:n-positive.
nodes.statement_display:
For every natural number $n$,
\[
n>0.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{cor:n-positive}
For every natural number $n$,
\[
n>0.
\]

nodes.name: thm:cauchy-not-convergent-q
nodes.question: State the theorem: thm:cauchy-not-convergent-q.
nodes.statement_display: There exist Cauchy sequences in $\mathbb{Q}$ that do not converge to a rational number.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:cauchy-not-convergent-q}
There exist Cauchy sequences in $\mathbb{Q}$ that do not converge to a rational number.

nodes.name: thm:convergent-implies-cauchy-q
nodes.question: State the theorem: thm:convergent-implies-cauchy-q.
nodes.statement_display: Every convergent sequence in $\mathbb{Q}$ is a Cauchy sequence.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:convergent-implies-cauchy-q}
Every convergent sequence in $\mathbb{Q}$ is a Cauchy sequence.

nodes.name: thm:fraction-pair-equivalence
nodes.question: State the theorem: thm:fraction-pair-equivalence.
nodes.statement_display: The relation $\sim$ is an equivalence relation on $W$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:fraction-pair-equivalence}
The relation $\sim$ is an equivalence relation on $W$.

nodes.name: thm:inverse-positive
nodes.question: State the theorem: thm:inverse-positive.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:inverse-positive}
If $a>0$, then $a^{-1}>0$.

nodes.name: thm:multiply-negative
nodes.question: State the theorem: thm:multiply-negative.
nodes.statement_display:
If $a<b$ and $c<0$, then
\[
ac>bc.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:multiply-negative}
If $a<b$ and $c<0$, then
\[
ac>bc.
\]

nodes.name: thm:multiply-positive
nodes.question: State the theorem: thm:multiply-positive.
nodes.statement_display:
If $a<b$ and $c>0$, then
\[
ac<bc.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:multiply-positive}
If $a<b$ and $c>0$, then
\[
ac<bc.
\]

nodes.name: thm:one-positive
nodes.question: State the theorem: thm:one-positive.
nodes.statement_display:
In an ordered field,
\[
1>0.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:one-positive}
In an ordered field,
\[
1>0.
\]

nodes.name: thm:sqrt2-irrational
nodes.question: State the theorem: thm:sqrt2-irrational.
nodes.statement_display: There is no rational number whose square is $2$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:sqrt2-irrational}
There is no rational number whose square is $2$.

nodes.name: thm:squares-nonnegative
nodes.question: State the theorem: thm:squares-nonnegative.
nodes.statement_display:
For every $a\in F$,
\[
a^2\ge 0.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:squares-nonnegative}
For every $a\in F$,
\[
a^2\ge 0.
\]

nodes.name: Arithmetic Operations on Real-Valued Functions
nodes.question: Define the pointwise sum of two real-valued functions $f,g : X \to \mathbb{R}$.
nodes.statement_display: $(f+g)(x) := f(x) + g(x)$ for all $x \in X$. The operations $-, \cdot, /, \max, \min, c\cdot$ are defined analogously.
nodes.statement_tex: $(f+g)(x) := f(x) + g(x)$ for all $x \in X$. The operations $-, \cdot, /, \max, \min, c\cdot$ are defined analogously.
nodes.hint: Pointwise = apply the operation value-by-value. No limits involved.
nodes.formal: Each operation is pointwise: the value of the combined function at $x$ depends only on $f(x)$ and $g(x)$, not on nearby values. Formally, $f + g$ is the unique function $h : X \to \mathbb{R}$ such that $h(x) = f(x) + g(x)$ for all $x \in X$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: Each operation is pointwise: the value of the combined function at $x$ depends only on $f(x)$ and $g(x)$, not on nearby values. Formally, $f + g$ is the unique function $h : X \to \mathbb{R}$ such that $h(x) = f(x) + g(x)$ for all $x \in X$.
nodes.negation_display: $fg$ is not composition: $(fg)(x) = f(x)g(x)$ but $(f \circ g)(x) = f(g(x))$. These agree only in special cases.

nodes.name: Mediant inequality
nodes.question: State the proposition: Mediant inequality.
nodes.statement_display:
If

\[
\frac{a}{b} < \frac{c}{d},
\]

then

\[
\frac{a}{b}
<
\frac{a+c}{b+d}
<
\frac{c}{d}.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{prop:mediant-inequality}

If

\[
\frac{a}{b} < \frac{c}{d},
\]

then

\[
\frac{a}{b}
<
\frac{a+c}{b+d}
<
\frac{c}{d}.
\]

nodes.name: Stern–Brocot Limit Theorem
nodes.question: State the theorem: Stern–Brocot Limit Theorem.
nodes.statement_display:
Let

\[
\left(\frac{a_n}{b_n}\right),\quad
\left(\frac{c_n}{d_n}\right)
\]

be sequences of rationals obtained by successive Stern–Brocot
refinement so that

\[
\frac{a_n}{b_n}
<
\frac{c_n}{d_n}
\]

and

\[
\left[\frac{a_{n+1}}{b_{n+1}},\frac{c_{n+1}}{d_{n+1}}\right]
\subseteq
\left[\frac{a_n}{b_n},\frac{c_n}{d_n}\right].
\]

Then there exists a unique real number

\[
x
=
\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{a_n}{b_n}
=
\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{c_n}{d_n}.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:stern-brocot-limit}

Let

\[
\left(\frac{a_n}{b_n}\right),\quad
\left(\frac{c_n}{d_n}\right)
\]

be sequences of rationals obtained by successive Stern–Brocot
refinement so that

\[
\frac{a_n}{b_n}
<
\frac{c_n}{d_n}
\]

and

\[
\left[\frac{a_{n+1}}{b_{n+1}},\frac{c_{n+1}}{d_{n+1}}\right]
\subseteq
\left[\frac{a_n}{b_n},\frac{c_n}{d_n}\right].
\]

Then there exists a unique real number

\[
x
=
\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{a_n}{b_n}
=
\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{c_n}{d_n}.
\]

nodes.name: Stern–Brocot enumeration
nodes.question: State the theorem: Stern–Brocot enumeration.
nodes.statement_display:
Every positive rational number appears exactly once in the
Stern–Brocot tree obtained by repeated mediant insertion.
nodes.formal:
\[
\forall q\in\mathbb{Q}_{>0},
\quad
\exists ! \text{ finite path in the Stern–Brocot tree reaching } q.
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
\[
\forall q\in\mathbb{Q}_{>0},
\quad
\exists ! \text{ finite path in the Stern–Brocot tree reaching } q.
\]

nodes.name: Cantor's Intersection Theorem
nodes.question: State the theorem: Cantor's Intersection Theorem.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(X, d)$ be a metric space and let
\[
  A_1 \supseteq A_2 \supseteq A_3 \supseteq \cdots
\]
be a nested sequence of nonempty compact subsets of $X$. Then
\[
  \bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n
\]
is compact and nonempty.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:cantors-intersection-theorem}
Let $(X, d)$ be a metric space and let
\[
  A_1 \supseteq A_2 \supseteq A_3 \supseteq \cdots
\]
be a nested sequence of nonempty compact subsets of $X$. Then
\[
  \bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n
\]
is compact and nonempty.
nodes.formal:
$\forall (X,d)$ metric space,
$\forall (A_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ with each $A_n \subseteq X$ compact, $A_n \neq \emptyset$,
and $A_{n+1} \subseteq A_n$:
\[
  \bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n \neq \emptyset
  \quad\text{and}\quad
  \bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n \text{ is compact.}
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$\forall (X,d)$ metric space,
$\forall (A_n)_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ with each $A_n \subseteq X$ compact, $A_n \neq \emptyset$,
and $A_{n+1} \subseteq A_n$:
\[
  \bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n \neq \emptyset
  \quad\text{and}\quad
  \bigcap_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n \text{ is compact.}
\]

nodes.name: Cancellation in Integral Domains
nodes.question: State cancellation in an integral domain.
nodes.statement_display: In integral domain $R$: $a \neq 0$ and $ab = ac \Rightarrow b = c$.
nodes.statement_tex: In integral domain $R$: $a \neq 0$ and $ab = ac \Rightarrow b = c$.
nodes.hint: $ab = ac \Rightarrow a(b-c) = 0$; since $a \neq 0$ and no zero divisors, $b - c = 0$.
nodes.negation_display: Fails in $\mathbb{Z}/6\mathbb{Z}$: $2 \cdot 1 = 2 \cdot 4 = 2$ but $1 \neq 4$.

nodes.name: Multiplication by Additive Inverse
nodes.question: In a ring, what is $a \cdot (-b)$?
nodes.statement_display: $a(-b) = -(ab)$ and $(-a)b = -(ab)$; in a ring with unity $(-1)a = -a$.
nodes.statement_tex: $a(-b) = -(ab)$ and $(-a)b = -(ab)$; in a ring with unity $(-1)a = -a$.
nodes.hint: Show $a(-b)$ satisfies the defining property of $-(ab)$, then invoke uniqueness of additive inverse.

nodes.name: Multiplication by Zero
nodes.question: State: multiplication by zero in a ring.
nodes.statement_display: In any ring $R$: $a\cdot 0=0\cdot a=0$ for all $a\in R$.
nodes.statement_tex: In any ring $R$: $a\cdot 0=0\cdot a=0$ for all $a\in R$.
nodes.hint: Distributivity: $a\cdot 0=a(0+0)=a\cdot 0+a\cdot 0$; cancel by additive inverse.

nodes.name: A Sequence Is Determined by Its Values
nodes.question: When are two sequences equal?
nodes.statement_display: Two sequences $x, y : \mathbb{N} \to X$ are equal iff $x_n = y_n$ for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$.
nodes.statement_tex: Two sequences $x, y : \mathbb{N} \to X$ are equal iff $x_n = y_n$ for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$.
nodes.hint: Sequences are functions; function equality is pointwise equality.
nodes.proof_sketch: Direct appeal to the definition of function equality. No quantifier beyond pointwise universality is needed.
nodes.uses_text: Definition of function equality; definition of a sequence as a function $\mathbb{N} \to X$.

nodes.name: Algebra of limits
nodes.question: State the Algebra of Limits.
nodes.statement_display: If $x_n\to x$ and $y_n\to y$: $x_n+y_n\to x+y$; $x_n-y_n\to x-y$; $cx_n\to cx$; $x_ny_n\to xy$; if $y\neq 0$ then $x_n/y_n\to x/y$.
nodes.statement_tex: If $x_n\to x$ and $y_n\to y$: $x_n+y_n\to x+y$; $x_n-y_n\to x-y$; $cx_n\to cx$; $x_ny_n\to xy$; if $y\neq 0$ then $x_n/y_n\to x/y$.
nodes.hint: Each rule follows from the $\varepsilon$-$N$ definition; products use the rectangle decomposition.
nodes.proof_sketch: Cases 1–3 are direct $\varepsilon/2$ splits. Case 4 uses add-and-subtract ($x_n y_n - xy = x_n(y_n - y) + y(x_n - x)$) together with the boundedness of $(x_n)$. Case 5 first establishes $|y_n| > |y|/2$ to bound the denominator away from zero, then estimates $|1/y_n - 1/y|$.
nodes.uses_text: $\varepsilon$-definition of convergence; triangle inequality; Every Convergent Sequence Is Bounded.

nodes.name: Bolzano--Weierstrass
nodes.question: State Bolzano–Weierstrass.
nodes.statement_display: Every bounded sequence in $\mathbb{R}$ has a convergent subsequence.
nodes.statement_tex: Every bounded sequence in $\mathbb{R}$ has a convergent subsequence.
nodes.proof_sketch: Three-step chain: every sequence has a monotone subsequence (Monotone Subsequence Theorem); a monotone subsequence of a bounded sequence is bounded and monotone; every bounded monotone sequence converges (MCT).
nodes.uses_text: Monotone Subsequence Theorem; Boundedness Is Inherited; Monotone Convergence Theorem.

nodes.name: Bolzano--Weierstrass
nodes.question: State Bolzano–Weierstrass.
nodes.statement_display: Every bounded sequence in $\mathbb{R}$ has a convergent subsequence.
nodes.statement_tex: Every bounded sequence in $\mathbb{R}$ has a convergent subsequence.
nodes.hint: Extract a monotone subsequence (Monotone Subsequence Theorem); bounded monotone sequences converge (MCT).

nodes.name: Bounded Does Not Imply Convergent
nodes.question: Is every bounded sequence convergent?
nodes.statement_display: No. Bounded does not imply convergent.
nodes.statement_tex: No. Bounded does not imply convergent.
nodes.hint: Counterexample: $(-1)^n$ is bounded by $1$ but oscillates between $\pm 1$, never settling.
nodes.proof_sketch: Counterexample proof. $(-1)^n$ oscillates between $1$ and $-1$ and cannot settle near any single value: any two consecutive terms are distance $2$ apart, so $\varepsilon = 1$ forces a contradiction.
nodes.uses_text: $\varepsilon$-definition of convergence; triangle inequality; the sequence $(-1)^n$ as a standard example.

nodes.name: Boundedness Is Inherited
nodes.question: State: boundedness is inherited.
nodes.statement_display: If $(a_n)$ is bounded then every subsequence is bounded by the same bound.
nodes.statement_tex: If $(a_n)$ is bounded then every subsequence is bounded by the same bound.
nodes.proof_sketch: Subsequence terms are a subset of the original terms; the original bound applies directly.
nodes.uses_text: Definition of bounded sequence; definition of subsequence.

nodes.name: Convergence Is Inherited
nodes.question: State: convergence is inherited by subsequences.
nodes.statement_display: If $(a_n)\to L$ then every subsequence $(a_{n_k})\to L$.
nodes.statement_tex: If $(a_n)\to L$ then every subsequence $(a_{n_k})\to L$.
nodes.hint: For $\varepsilon>0$ pick $N$; since $n_k\ge k$, $k\ge N$ forces $n_k\ge N$.
nodes.proof_sketch: Direct restatement of Subsequences Inherit Limits.

nodes.name: Convergence Is a Tail Property
nodes.question: State: convergence is a tail property.
nodes.statement_display: $(x_n)\to L$ iff every $k$-tail $(x_{n+k})_{n\ge 1}\to L$.
nodes.statement_tex: $(x_n)\to L$ iff every $k$-tail $(x_{n+k})_{n\ge 1}\to L$.
nodes.hint: Adding or removing finitely many terms does not affect convergence or its limit.
nodes.proof_sketch: The forward direction re-indexes: any $N$ that works for the full sequence works for any tail. The backward direction is immediate since the full sequence is its own $0$-tail.

nodes.name: Convergence of Absolute Values
nodes.question: State the corollary: Convergence of Absolute Values.
nodes.statement_display:
If $(x_n)$ converges, then $(|x_n|)$ converges and
\[
|x_n| \to |x|.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{cor:conv-abs-values}
If $(x_n)$ converges, then $(|x_n|)$ converges and
\[
|x_n| \to |x|.
\]
nodes.proof_sketch: One line: the reverse triangle inequality $\bigl||a| - |b|\bigr| \le |a - b|$ directly transfers convergence of $(x_n)$ to convergence of $(|x_n|)$.

nodes.name: Convergence via Even and Odd Subsequences
nodes.question: State convergence via even and odd subsequences.
nodes.statement_display: $(a_n)\to L$ iff $(a_{2n})\to L$ and $(a_{2n+1})\to L$.
nodes.statement_tex: $(a_n)\to L$ iff $(a_{2n})\to L$ and $(a_{2n+1})\to L$.
nodes.proof_sketch: Choose a single threshold $N$ large enough that both even and odd index subsequences are within $\varepsilon$ of $L$; then every $n \ge N$ falls into one of the two cases.
nodes.uses_text: $\varepsilon$-definition of convergence; parity of natural numbers.

nodes.name: Convergent Times Bounded Is Bounded
nodes.question: State: convergent times bounded is bounded.
nodes.statement_display: If $(x_n)\to x$ and $(y_n)$ is bounded then $(x_ny_n)$ is bounded.
nodes.statement_tex: If $(x_n)\to x$ and $(y_n)$ is bounded then $(x_ny_n)$ is bounded.
nodes.hint: $(x_n)$ is bounded (convergent $\Rightarrow$ bounded); product of bounded sequences is bounded.
nodes.proof_sketch: Both sequences are bounded; their product is bounded by the product of their bounds.
nodes.uses_text: Every Convergent Sequence Is Bounded; multiplicativity of absolute value.

nodes.name: Dense Subsequence Criterion
nodes.question: State the Dense Subsequence Criterion.
nodes.statement_display: If every neighbourhood of $L$ contains infinitely many terms of $(a_n)$, then $(a_n)$ has a subsequence converging to $L$.
nodes.statement_tex: If every neighbourhood of $L$ contains infinitely many terms of $(a_n)$, then $(a_n)$ has a subsequence converging to $L$.
nodes.hint: From $B_{1/k}(L)$ containing infinitely many terms, pick $n_k>n_{k-1}$ with $|a_{n_k}-L|<1/k$.

nodes.name: Equivalence: LUB $\Longleftrightarrow$ MCT
nodes.question: State LUB $\Leftrightarrow$ MCT.
nodes.statement_display: TFAE: (i) LUB property; (ii) bounded monotone sequences converge.
nodes.statement_tex: TFAE: (i) LUB property; (ii) bounded monotone sequences converge.

nodes.name: Even-Odd Convergence
nodes.question: State Even-Odd Convergence.
nodes.statement_display: If $(a_{2n})\to L$ and $(a_{2n+1})\to L$ then $(a_n)\to L$.
nodes.statement_tex: If $(a_{2n})\to L$ and $(a_{2n+1})\to L$ then $(a_n)\to L$.
nodes.hint: Special case of Residue-Class Convergence with $k=2$.
nodes.proof_sketch: Special case of Residue-Class Convergence with $k = 2$.

nodes.name: Every convergent sequence is bounded
nodes.question: State: convergent sequences are bounded.
nodes.statement_display: If $(x_n)$ converges then $(x_n)$ is bounded.
nodes.statement_tex: If $(x_n)$ converges then $(x_n)$ is bounded.
nodes.hint: Take $\varepsilon=1$; terms beyond $N$ lie within 1 of the limit; finitely many terms before $N$ are bounded.
nodes.proof_sketch: Split at the threshold $N$ from convergence with $\varepsilon = 1$: the tail is controlled by $1 + |L|$; the finitely many head terms are bounded by their own maximum. Combine via a single max.
nodes.uses_text: $\varepsilon$-definition of convergence; triangle inequality for $|\cdot|$; finiteness of $\{x_1, …, x_{N-1}\}$.

nodes.name: Existence of Subsequential Limits
nodes.question: State: every bounded sequence has a subsequential limit.
nodes.statement_display: If $(a_n)$ is bounded then $\mathcal{L}(a_n)\neq\varnothing$.
nodes.statement_tex: If $(a_n)$ is bounded then $\mathcal{L}(a_n)\neq\varnothing$.
nodes.hint: By BW, extract a convergent subsequence; its limit is a subsequential limit.
nodes.proof_sketch: Immediate from Bolzano–Weierstrass: the limit of the convergent subsequence is a subsequential limit.

nodes.name: Finite Modification Theorem
nodes.question: State the Finite Modification Theorem.
nodes.statement_display: If $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ differ in only finitely many terms: either both converge to the same limit or neither converges.
nodes.statement_tex: If $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$ differ in only finitely many terms: either both converge to the same limit or neither converges.
nodes.proof_sketch: Past the threshold $K$ the sequences agree, so any $N$ that works for $(x_n)$ works for $(y_n)$ once raised to $\max\{N_1, K\}$. Symmetry gives the other direction; uniqueness of limits ties down the common value.
implies_titles: Tails of a Convergent Sequence Converge

nodes.name: Finite Partition Convergence Principle
nodes.question: State the Finite Partition Convergence Principle.
nodes.statement_display: If $\mathbb{N}=E_1\cup\cdots\cup E_k$ (finite partition, each $E_i$ infinite) and $(a_n)_{n\in E_i}\to L$ for each $i$, then $a_n\to L$.
nodes.statement_tex: If $\mathbb{N}=E_1\cup\cdots\cup E_k$ (finite partition, each $E_i$ infinite) and $(a_n)_{n\in E_i}\to L$ for each $i$, then $a_n\to L$.
nodes.hint: Take the maximum of the finitely many thresholds $N_i$.
nodes.proof_sketch: Take $N$ to be the maximum of the individual thresholds $N_i$. Every $n \ge N$ belongs to exactly one partition class $E_i$, which supplies the bound $|a_n - L| < \varepsilon$.
nodes.uses_text: $\varepsilon$-definition of convergence; finiteness of the partition (guaranteeing $\max\{N_1, …, N_k\}$ is well-defined).

nodes.name: Index Growth
nodes.question: State the Index Growth lemma.
nodes.statement_display: If $(n_k)$ is strictly increasing in $\mathbb{N}$ then $n_k\ge k$ for all $k$.
nodes.statement_tex: If $(n_k)$ is strictly increasing in $\mathbb{N}$ then $n_k\ge k$ for all $k$.
nodes.hint: Induction: $n_1\ge 1$; $n_{k+1}\ge n_k+1\ge k+1$.
nodes.proof_sketch: Induction. The base case is vacuous; the inductive step uses strict inequality and the discreteness of $\mathbb{N}$.
nodes.uses_text: Mathematical induction; strict monotonicity of the index sequence; the natural numbers are discrete ($m > k \Rightarrow m \ge k+1$ for $m, k \in \mathbb{N}$).

nodes.name: Limit Respects Upper Bounds
nodes.question: State: limits respect upper bounds.
nodes.statement_display: If $x_n\le b$ for all $n$ and $x_n\to x$, then $x\le b$.
nodes.statement_tex: If $x_n\le b$ for all $n$ and $x_n\to x$, then $x\le b$.
nodes.hint: Apply OLT with $y_n=b$.
nodes.proof_sketch: Reduce to the Order Limit Theorem by pairing $(x_n)$ with the constant sequence $(b)$.
nodes.uses_text: Order Limit Theorem; convergence of constant sequences.

nodes.name: Limit of a Polynomial of a Sequence
nodes.question: If $x_n \to x$ and $p$ is a polynomial, does $p(x_n) \to p(x)$?
nodes.statement_display: Yes. $x_n \to x \Rightarrow p(x_n) \to p(x)$ for any polynomial $p$.
nodes.statement_tex: Yes. $x_n \to x \Rightarrow p(x_n) \to p(x)$ for any polynomial $p$.
nodes.hint: Induction on degree using the Algebra of Limits: sum and product rules handle each term.
nodes.proof_sketch: Two nested inductions: one on degree, one on exponent (for the power rule). Both use only the sum, scalar, and product rules of the Algebra of Limits.
nodes.uses_text: Algebra of Limits (sum, scalar, and product rules); convergence of constant sequences.

nodes.name: Monotone Approximation of Bounds
nodes.question: State Monotone Approximation of Bounds.
nodes.statement_display: For nonempty bounded $S$: there exist monotone sequences $(x_n),(y_n)\subseteq S$ with $x_n\to\sup S$, $y_n\to\inf S$.
nodes.statement_tex: For nonempty bounded $S$: there exist monotone sequences $(x_n),(y_n)\subseteq S$ with $x_n\to\sup S$, $y_n\to\inf S$.
nodes.proof_sketch: Approximate from inside using the $\varepsilon$-witness property of sup/inf, then monotonize by taking running max (resp.\ min). Squeeze Theorem closes the gap.
nodes.uses_text: Least Upper Bound Property of $\mathbb{R}$; $\varepsilon$-witness property of $\sup$ and $\inf$; Squeeze Theorem.

nodes.name: Monotone Convergence Theorem
nodes.question: State the Monotone Convergence Theorem (MCT).
nodes.statement_display: Every bounded monotone sequence converges: an increasing bounded sequence converges to $\sup\{x_n\}$; decreasing bounded below converges to $\inf\{x_n\}$.
nodes.statement_tex: Every bounded monotone sequence converges: an increasing bounded sequence converges to $\sup\{x_n\}$; decreasing bounded below converges to $\inf\{x_n\}$.
nodes.hint: Let $L=\sup\{x_n\}$; given $\varepsilon$, the $\varepsilon$-characterisation gives $N$ with $x_N>L-\varepsilon$; monotonicity propagates.
nodes.negation_display: Fails without boundedness: $(n)$ is monotone and diverges.
nodes.proof_sketch: The key pivot is the $\varepsilon$-witness property of supremum: for any $\varepsilon > 0$, some $x_N$ lies within $\varepsilon$ of $\sup S$. Monotonicity then propagates this to the entire tail.
nodes.uses_text: Least Upper Bound Property of $\mathbb{R}$; $\varepsilon$-witness property of $\sup$; monotonicity to propagate the bound to the tail.

nodes.name: Monotone Convergence Theorem (MCT)
nodes.question: State the Monotone Convergence Theorem.
nodes.statement_display: Every bounded monotone sequence converges.
nodes.statement_tex: Every bounded monotone sequence converges.
nodes.hint: Increasing bounded: converges to $\sup\{x_n\}$. Decreasing bounded below: converges to $\inf\{x_n\}$.

nodes.name: Monotone Subsequence Theorem
nodes.question: State the Monotone Subsequence Theorem.
nodes.statement_display: Every sequence in $\mathbb{R}$ has a monotone subsequence.
nodes.statement_tex: Every sequence in $\mathbb{R}$ has a monotone subsequence.
nodes.hint: Peak argument: if infinitely many peak terms, they form a decreasing subsequence; if finitely many, extract an increasing one from beyond the last peak.
nodes.proof_sketch: Peak-index dichotomy. If infinitely many peaks exist, they form a decreasing subsequence. If only finitely many exist, the failure of the peak condition beyond index $N$ allows greedy construction of an increasing subsequence.
nodes.uses_text: Definition of subsequence; discreteness of $\mathbb{N}$ (to choose successively larger indices in Case 2).

nodes.name: Monotone Unbounded Sequences Diverge to $\pm\infty$
nodes.question: Does an unbounded monotone sequence converge?
nodes.statement_display: No. Every monotone unbounded sequence diverges (to $\pm\infty$).
nodes.statement_tex: No. Every monotone unbounded sequence diverges (to $\pm\infty$).
nodes.hint: Contrapositive of MCT: convergent bounded. An unbounded monotone sequence surpasses every $M$.
nodes.proof_sketch: Direct from definitions. Unboundedness supplies one index $N$ beyond the threshold $K$; monotonicity propagates it to the entire tail.
nodes.uses_text: Definition of divergence to $\pm\infty$; definition of increasing sequence; definition of unbounded above.

nodes.name: Monotonicity Is Inherited
nodes.question: State: monotonicity is inherited.
nodes.statement_display: If $(a_n)$ is monotone then every subsequence is monotone in the same direction.
nodes.statement_tex: If $(a_n)$ is monotone then every subsequence is monotone in the same direction.
nodes.proof_sketch: The index sequence is strictly increasing, so consecutive subsequence indices satisfy $n_k < n_{k+1}$; monotonicity of $(a_n)$ then forces $a_{n_k} \le a_{n_{k+1}}$.
nodes.uses_text: Definition of monotone sequence; definition of strictly increasing index sequence.

nodes.name: Order Limit Theorem
nodes.question: State the Order Limit Theorem.
nodes.statement_display: If $x_n\le y_n$ for all $n$ and $x_n\to x$, $y_n\to y$, then $x\le y$.
nodes.statement_tex: If $x_n\le y_n$ for all $n$ and $x_n\to x$, $y_n\to y$, then $x\le y$.
nodes.hint: If $x>y$, take $\varepsilon=(x-y)/2$; eventually $x_n>y_n$, contradiction.
nodes.negation_display: Strict inequality $x_n<y_n$ does NOT force $x<y$: $(0)\to 0$ and $(1/n)\to 0$.
nodes.proof_sketch: Contradiction: if $x > y$, the gap $(x-y)/2$ separates the eventual ranges of $(x_n)$ and $(y_n)$, producing $y_n < x_n$ for large $n$.

nodes.name: Residue-Class Convergence
nodes.question: State Residue-Class Convergence.
nodes.statement_display: If $(a_{kn+r})\to L$ for each $r\in\{0,…,k-1\}$, then $(a_n)\to L$.
nodes.statement_tex: If $(a_{kn+r})\to L$ for each $r\in\{0,…,k-1\}$, then $(a_n)\to L$.
nodes.hint: The residue classes form a finite partition of $\mathbb{N}$; apply Finite Partition Convergence.
nodes.proof_sketch: Residue classes mod $k$ give a canonical finite partition of $\mathbb{N}$; apply the Finite Partition Convergence Principle directly.
nodes.uses_text: Finite Partition Convergence Principle; division algorithm for $\mathbb{N}$.

nodes.name: Sequential Compactness of Closed Intervals
nodes.question: State sequential compactness of closed intervals.
nodes.statement_display: Every sequence in $[a,b]$ has a convergent subsequence whose limit lies in $[a,b]$.
nodes.statement_tex: Every sequence in $[a,b]$ has a convergent subsequence whose limit lies in $[a,b]$.
nodes.hint: BW gives a convergent subsequence; the limit is in $[a,b]$ since $[a,b]$ is closed.
nodes.proof_sketch: BW supplies the convergent subsequence; the Order Limit Theorem (via Limit Respects Upper Bounds) keeps the limit inside the closed interval.

nodes.name: Sequential compactness of closed intervals
nodes.question: State the corollary: Sequential compactness of closed intervals.
nodes.statement_display:
Every sequence in a closed bounded interval $[a,b] \subset \mathbb{R}$ has a
convergent subsequence whose limit lies in $[a,b]$.

nodes.name: Squeeze Theorem
nodes.question: State the Squeeze Theorem.
nodes.statement_display: If $x_n\le y_n\le z_n$ for all $n$ and $x_n\to L$, $z_n\to L$, then $y_n\to L$.
nodes.statement_tex: If $x_n\le y_n\le z_n$ for all $n$ and $x_n\to L$, $z_n\to L$, then $y_n\to L$.
nodes.hint: For $\varepsilon>0$: eventually $L-\varepsilon<x_n\le y_n\le z_n<L+\varepsilon$.
nodes.proof_sketch: Both outer sequences enter the $\varepsilon$-band around $L$ after their respective thresholds; past the maximum threshold, the sandwich forces the middle sequence into the same band.

nodes.name: Subsequences Inherit Limits
nodes.question: State: subsequences inherit limits.
nodes.statement_display: If $a_n\to L$ then $a_{n_k}\to L$ for every subsequence $(a_{n_k})$.
nodes.statement_tex: If $a_n\to L$ then $a_{n_k}\to L$ for every subsequence $(a_{n_k})$.
nodes.proof_sketch: The key fact is $n_k \ge k$: since the index sequence grows at least as fast as the natural numbers, any tail threshold $N$ for the full sequence is eventually passed by $(n_k)$.
nodes.uses_text: $\varepsilon$-definition of convergence; Index Growth ($n_k \ge k$ for strictly increasing index sequences).

nodes.name: T6.1 — Every convergent sequence is Cauchy
nodes.question: State: convergent implies Cauchy.
nodes.statement_display: If $(a_n)$ converges then $(a_n)$ is Cauchy.
nodes.statement_tex: If $(a_n)$ converges then $(a_n)$ is Cauchy.

nodes.name: T6.10 — Every contractive sequence converges
nodes.question: Does every contractive sequence converge?
nodes.statement_display: Every contractive sequence (where $|x_{n+1} - x_n| \leq c|x_n - x_{n-1}|$ for some $c < 1$) converges.
nodes.statement_tex: Every contractive sequence (where $|x_{n+1} - x_n| \leq c|x_n - x_{n-1}|$ for some $c < 1$) converges.
nodes.hint: Contractive sequences are rapidly Cauchy (after rescaling); apply the rapidly Cauchy $\Rightarrow$ Cauchy $\Rightarrow$ convergent chain.

nodes.name: T6.1c — Every convergent series has Cauchy partial sums
nodes.question: State: convergent series have Cauchy partial sums.
nodes.statement_display: If $\sum a_n$ converges and $s_n=\sum_{k=1}^n a_k$, then $(s_n)$ is Cauchy.
nodes.statement_tex: If $\sum a_n$ converges and $s_n=\sum_{k=1}^n a_k$, then $(s_n)$ is Cauchy.

nodes.name: T6.2 — Cauchy sequences are bounded
nodes.question: State: Cauchy sequences are bounded.
nodes.statement_display: Every Cauchy sequence is bounded.
nodes.statement_tex: Every Cauchy sequence is bounded.
nodes.proof_sketch: Identical structure to the proof that convergent sequences are bounded, with the Cauchy condition (at $\varepsilon = 1$) replacing the convergence condition. Anchor the tail at $a_N$; handle the finite head by a max.
nodes.uses_text: Definition of Cauchy sequence; triangle inequality; finiteness of $\{a_1, …, a_{N-1}\}$.

nodes.name: T6.4 — Algebra of Cauchy sequences
nodes.question: State the Algebra of Cauchy Sequences.
nodes.statement_display: If $(a_n),(b_n)$ are Cauchy and $c\in\mathbb{R}$: $(a_n+b_n)$, $(a_n-b_n)$, $(ca_n)$, $(a_nb_n)$ are all Cauchy.
nodes.statement_tex: If $(a_n),(b_n)$ are Cauchy and $c\in\mathbb{R}$: $(a_n+b_n)$, $(a_n-b_n)$, $(ca_n)$, $(a_nb_n)$ are all Cauchy.
nodes.proof_sketch: Cases 1, 2, 5 are direct triangle-inequality arguments. Case 3 uses the add-and-subtract decomposition $a_m b_m - a_n b_n = a_m(b_m-b_n) + b_n(a_m-a_n)$ together with the boundedness of Cauchy sequences (T6.2). Case 4 uses the uniform lower bound $|b_n| \ge \delta$ to control the denominator. No limits are named anywhere: this is an intrinsic, internal argument.
nodes.uses_text: Definition of Cauchy sequence; triangle inequality; Cauchy Sequences Are Bounded (for Case 3).

nodes.name: T6.5 — Cauchy subsequence limit
nodes.question: State: Cauchy with convergent subsequence converges.
nodes.statement_display: If $(a_n)$ Cauchy and $(a_{n_k})\to L$ then $a_n\to L$.
nodes.statement_tex: If $(a_n)$ Cauchy and $(a_{n_k})\to L$ then $a_n\to L$.

nodes.name: T6.6 — Every rapidly Cauchy sequence is Cauchy
nodes.question: State the theorem: T6.6 — Every rapidly Cauchy sequence is Cauchy.
nodes.statement_display: If $(f_k)$ is rapidly Cauchy, then $(f_k)$ is Cauchy.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:rapidly-cauchy}
If $(f_k)$ is rapidly Cauchy, then $(f_k)$ is Cauchy.

nodes.name: T6.7 — Cauchy Divergence Criterion
nodes.question: State the Cauchy Divergence Criterion.
nodes.statement_display: $(a_n)$ diverges if $\exists\varepsilon_0>0$ such that $\forall N\,\exists m,n\ge N:|a_m-a_n|\ge\varepsilon_0$.
nodes.statement_tex: $(a_n)$ diverges if $\exists\varepsilon_0>0$ such that $\forall N\,\exists m,n\ge N:|a_m-a_n|\ge\varepsilon_0$.
nodes.hint: Negation of the Cauchy condition; useful to prove divergence.

nodes.name: T6.8 — Linear combinations of Cauchy sequences are Cauchy
nodes.question: State: linear combinations of Cauchy sequences are Cauchy.
nodes.statement_display: If $(a_n),(b_n)$ are Cauchy and $\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}$ then $(\alpha a_n+\beta b_n)$ is Cauchy.
nodes.statement_tex: If $(a_n),(b_n)$ are Cauchy and $\alpha,\beta\in\mathbb{R}$ then $(\alpha a_n+\beta b_n)$ is Cauchy.
nodes.proof_sketch: Immediate two-step composition of the scalar and sum cases of the Algebra of Cauchy Sequences.

nodes.name: Tails of a Convergent Sequence Converge
nodes.question: Do tails of a convergent sequence converge?
nodes.statement_display: If $(x_n) \to L$, then every $k$-tail converges to $L$.
nodes.statement_tex: If $(x_n) \to L$, then every $k$-tail converges to $L$.
nodes.hint: Direct consequence of convergence being a tail property.
nodes.proof_sketch: Immediate forward direction of the Convergence Is a Tail Property equivalence.

nodes.name: Uniqueness of Limits
nodes.question: State the theorem: Uniqueness of Limits.
nodes.statement_display:
If a sequence $(x_n)$ converges to a real number $L$ and also converges to a real
number $M$, then $L = M$.
nodes.proof_sketch: Contradiction via the triangle inequality. The adversarial choice $\varepsilon = |L - M|/2$ ensures the two $\varepsilon$-balls around $L$ and $M$ are disjoint, yet both must contain $x_n$ for large $n$.

nodes.name: $\mathbb{Q}$ is countable
nodes.question: State the theorem: $\mathbb{Q}$ is countable.
nodes.statement_display: The set $\mathbb{Q}$ of rational numbers is countable.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:Q-countable}
The set $\mathbb{Q}$ of rational numbers is countable.

nodes.name: $\mathbb{R}$ is uncountable
nodes.question: State the theorem: $\mathbb{R}$ is uncountable.
nodes.statement_display: The set $\mathbb{R}$ of real numbers is not countable.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:R-uncountable}
The set $\mathbb{R}$ of real numbers is not countable.

nodes.name: Cantor's Theorem
nodes.question: State the theorem: Cantor's Theorem.
nodes.statement_display:
For any set $A$, there is no surjection $A \to \mathcal{P}(A)$. In
particular, $|A| < |\mathcal{P}(A)|$.
nodes.formal: $\forall A:\; \neg\exists f: A\to\mathcal{P}(A),\; \forall S\in\mathcal{P}(A),\; \exists a\in A,\; f(a)=S$. Equivalently: $\forall f: A\to\mathcal{P}(A),\; \exists D\in\mathcal{P}(A),\; \forall a\in A,\; f(a)\neq D$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $\forall A:\; \neg\exists f: A\to\mathcal{P}(A),\; \forall S\in\mathcal{P}(A),\; \exists a\in A,\; f(a)=S$. Equivalently: $\forall f: A\to\mathcal{P}(A),\; \exists D\in\mathcal{P}(A),\; \forall a\in A,\; f(a)\neq D$.

nodes.name: Countable union of countable sets is countable
nodes.question: State the theorem: Countable union of countable sets is countable.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\{A_n\}_{n \in \mathbb{N}}$ be a countable family of countable sets.
Then $\bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n$ is countable.

nodes.name: Schröder--Bernstein Theorem
nodes.question: State the theorem: Schröder--Bernstein Theorem.
nodes.statement_display:
If $|A| \leq |B|$ and $|B| \leq |A|$, then $A \sim B$.

Equivalently: if there exist injections $f : A \hookrightarrow B$ and
$g : B \hookrightarrow A$, then there exists a bijection $h : A \to B$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:schroder-bernstein}
If $|A| \leq |B|$ and $|B| \leq |A|$, then $A \sim B$.

Equivalently: if there exist injections $f : A \hookrightarrow B$ and
$g : B \hookrightarrow A$, then there exists a bijection $h : A \to B$.
nodes.formal: $\bigl(\exists f: A\hookrightarrow B\bigr) \wedge \bigl(\exists g: B\hookrightarrow A\bigr) \;\to\; \exists h: A\to B,\; h$ bijective.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $\bigl(\exists f: A\hookrightarrow B\bigr) \wedge \bigl(\exists g: B\hookrightarrow A\bigr) \;\to\; \exists h: A\to B,\; h$ bijective.

nodes.name: Equivalence Relations and Partitions
nodes.question: State the theorem: Equivalence Relations and Partitions.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A$ be a set.
  1. If $R$ is an equivalence relation on $A$, then $A/R$ is a partition of $A$.
  2. If $\mathcal{P}$ is a partition of $A$, then the relation $R_{\mathcal{P}}$ defined by \[ (a,b) \in R_{\mathcal{P}} \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \exists P \in \mathcal{P} \text{ with } a \in P \text{ and } b \in P \] is an equivalence relation on $A$.
  3. These constructions are inverse: $R_{A/R} = R$ and $A/R_{\mathcal{P}} = \mathcal{P}$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:equiv-partition}
Let $A$ be a set.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item If $R$ is an equivalence relation on $A$, then $A/R$ is a partition of $A$.
\item If $\mathcal{P}$ is a partition of $A$, then the relation
$R_{\mathcal{P}}$ defined by
\[
(a,b) \in R_{\mathcal{P}} \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
\exists P \in \mathcal{P} \text{ with } a \in P \text{ and } b \in P
\]
is an equivalence relation on $A$.
\item These constructions are inverse: $R_{A/R} = R$ and $A/R_{\mathcal{P}} = \mathcal{P}$.
\end{enumerate}
nodes.formal:
(i) $\forall a \in A,\; [a]_R \neq \varnothing$; $\forall a,b \in A,\; [a]_R \neq [b]_R \to [a]_R \cap [b]_R = \varnothing$; $\bigcup_{a \in A}[a]_R = A$.\\
(ii) $\forall a,b \in A:\; (a,b) \in R_{\mathcal{P}} \;\leftrightarrow\; \exists P \in \mathcal{P},\; a \in P \wedge b \in P$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
(i) $\forall a \in A,\; [a]_R \neq \varnothing$; $\forall a,b \in A,\; [a]_R \neq [b]_R \to [a]_R \cap [b]_R = \varnothing$; $\bigcup_{a \in A}[a]_R = A$.\\
(ii) $\forall a,b \in A:\; (a,b) \in R_{\mathcal{P}} \;\leftrightarrow\; \exists P \in \mathcal{P},\; a \in P \wedge b \in P$.

nodes.name: Index of an Equivalence Relation
nodes.question: State the definition: Index of an Equivalence Relation.
nodes.statement_display: The index of $R$ on $A$ is the cardinality $|A/R|$, i.e.\ the number of equivalence classes.
nodes.formal: $\mathrm{index}(R) = |A/R| = |\{[a]_R \mid a \in A\}|$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $\mathrm{index}(R) = |A/R| = |\{[a]_R \mid a \in A\}|$.

nodes.name: Representative Independence Lemma
nodes.question: State the lemma: Representative Independence Lemma.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $R$ be an equivalence relation on $A$. For any $a,b \in A$,
\[
[a] = [b]
\;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\;
(a,b) \in R.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{lem:rep-independence}
Let $R$ be an equivalence relation on $A$. For any $a,b \in A$,
\[
[a] = [b]
\;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\;
(a,b) \in R.
\]
nodes.formal: $\forall a, b \in A:\; [a]_R = [b]_R \;\leftrightarrow\; (a,b)\in R$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $\forall a, b \in A:\; [a]_R = [b]_R \;\leftrightarrow\; (a,b)\in R$.

nodes.name: Universal Property of the Quotient Map
nodes.question: State the theorem: Universal Property of the Quotient Map.
nodes.statement_display: Let $R$ be an equivalence relation on $A$, let $\pi : A \to A/R$ be the canonical surjection, and let $f : A \to B$ be any function. Then $f$ is constant on equivalence classes — meaning $(a,b) \in R$ implies $f(a) = f(b)$ — if and only if there exists a unique function $\bar{f} : A/R \to B$ such that \[ f = \bar{f} \circ \pi. \] When it exists, $\bar{f}$ is defined by $\bar{f}([a]) = f(a)$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:quotient-universal}
Let $R$ be an equivalence relation on $A$, let $\pi : A \to A/R$ be the
canonical surjection, and let $f : A \to B$ be any function. Then $f$ is
\emph{constant on equivalence classes} — meaning $(a,b) \in R$ implies
$f(a) = f(b)$ — if and only if there exists a unique function
$\bar{f} : A/R \to B$ such that
\[
f = \bar{f} \circ \pi.
\]
When it exists, $\bar{f}$ is defined by $\bar{f}([a]) = f(a)$.
nodes.formal: $\bigl(\forall a,b \in A,\; (a,b)\in R \to f(a) = f(b)\bigr) \;\leftrightarrow\; \bigl(\exists!\, \bar{f} : A/R \to B,\; \forall a \in A,\; \bar{f}([a]) = f(a)\bigr)$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $\bigl(\forall a,b \in A,\; (a,b)\in R \to f(a) = f(b)\bigr) \;\leftrightarrow\; \bigl(\exists!\, \bar{f} : A/R \to B,\; \forall a \in A,\; \bar{f}([a]) = f(a)\bigr)$.

nodes.name: Cover
nodes.question: State the definition: Cover.
nodes.statement_display: A collection $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathcal{P}(A)$ is a cover of $A$ if \[ \bigcup_{C \in \mathcal{C}} C = A. \]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:set-cover}
A collection $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \mathcal{P}(A)$ is a \emph{cover} of $A$ if
\[
\bigcup_{C \in \mathcal{C}} C = A.
\]
nodes.formal: $\mathcal{C}$ covers $A$ $\;\leftrightarrow\; \forall x \in A,\; \exists C \in \mathcal{C},\; x \in C$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $\mathcal{C}$ covers $A$ $\;\leftrightarrow\; \forall x \in A,\; \exists C \in \mathcal{C},\; x \in C$.

nodes.name: FIP--Cover Duality
nodes.question: State the proposition: FIP--Cover Duality.
nodes.statement_display: Let $X$ be a set, $A \subseteq X$, and $\{U_\alpha\}_{\alpha \in I}$ a family of subsets of $X$. Let $F_\alpha = U_\alpha^c = X \setminus U_\alpha$. Then: \[ \{U_\alpha\} \text{ covers } A \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \{F_\alpha \cap A\}_{\alpha \in I} \text{ does not have the FIP}. \] More precisely: the family $\{U_\alpha\}$ does not cover $A$ if and only if the family of closed complements $\{F_\alpha\}$ has the FIP relative to $A$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{prop:fip-duality}
Let $X$ be a set, $A \subseteq X$, and $\{U_\alpha\}_{\alpha \in I}$ a
family of subsets of $X$. Let $F_\alpha = U_\alpha^c = X \setminus U_\alpha$.
Then:
\[
\{U_\alpha\} \text{ covers } A
\;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\;
\{F_\alpha \cap A\}_{\alpha \in I} \text{ does \emph{not} have the FIP}.
\]
More precisely: the family $\{U_\alpha\}$ does \emph{not} cover $A$ if and
only if the family of closed complements $\{F_\alpha\}$ has the FIP relative
to $A$.
nodes.formal: $A \subseteq \bigcup_{\alpha \in I} U_\alpha \;\leftrightarrow\; \neg(\forall\text{ finite }I_0\subseteq I,\; \bigcap_{\alpha\in I_0} F_\alpha \cap A \neq \varnothing)$. Reading pointwise: $\forall x\in A,\;\exists\alpha\in I,\; x\in U_\alpha \;\leftrightarrow\; \{F_\alpha\cap A\}$ fails the FIP.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $A \subseteq \bigcup_{\alpha \in I} U_\alpha \;\leftrightarrow\; \neg(\forall\text{ finite }I_0\subseteq I,\; \bigcap_{\alpha\in I_0} F_\alpha \cap A \neq \varnothing)$. Reading pointwise: $\forall x\in A,\;\exists\alpha\in I,\; x\in U_\alpha \;\leftrightarrow\; \{F_\alpha\cap A\}$ fails the FIP.

nodes.name: Open Cover
nodes.question: State the definition: Open Cover.
nodes.statement_display: In a topological space $(X, \tau)$, an open cover of a subset $A \subseteq X$ is a cover $\mathcal{C}$ of $A$ all of whose members are open sets: $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \tau$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:open-cover}
In a topological space $(X, \tau)$, an \emph{open cover} of a subset $A
\subseteq X$ is a cover $\mathcal{C}$ of $A$ all of whose members are open
sets: $\mathcal{C} \subseteq \tau$.
nodes.formal: $\mathcal{C}$ is an open cover of $A$ $\;\leftrightarrow\;$ $(\forall C \in \mathcal{C},\; C \in \tau) \;\wedge\; (\forall x \in A,\; \exists C \in \mathcal{C},\; x \in C)$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $\mathcal{C}$ is an open cover of $A$ $\;\leftrightarrow\;$ $(\forall C \in \mathcal{C},\; C \in \tau) \;\wedge\; (\forall x \in A,\; \exists C \in \mathcal{C},\; x \in C)$.

nodes.name: Associativity of Composition
nodes.question: State the theorem: Associativity of Composition.
nodes.statement_display:
For $f : A \to B$, $g : B \to C$, $h : C \to D$:
\[
h \circ (g \circ f) = (h \circ g) \circ f.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:comp-assoc}
For $f : A \to B$, $g : B \to C$, $h : C \to D$:
\[
h \circ (g \circ f) = (h \circ g) \circ f.
\]
nodes.formal: $\forall a \in A:\; h(g(f(a))) = h(g(f(a)))$, i.e.\ $\forall f\!:\!A\!\to\!B,\; g\!:\!B\!\to\!C,\; h\!:\!C\!\to\!D,\; \forall a \in A:\; [h \circ (g \circ f)](a) = [(h \circ g) \circ f](a)$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $\forall a \in A:\; h(g(f(a))) = h(g(f(a)))$, i.e.\ $\forall f\!:\!A\!\to\!B,\; g\!:\!B\!\to\!C,\; h\!:\!C\!\to\!D,\; \forall a \in A:\; [h \circ (g \circ f)](a) = [(h \circ g) \circ f](a)$.

nodes.name: Characterization of Inverse Functions
nodes.question: State the theorem: Characterization of Inverse Functions.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to B$ be bijective. Then
\[
f^{-1} \circ f = \id_A
\quad\text{and}\quad
f \circ f^{-1} = \id_B.
\]
Conversely, a function admits an inverse iff it is bijective.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:inverse-char}
Let $f : A \to B$ be bijective. Then
\[
f^{-1} \circ f = \id_A
\quad\text{and}\quad
f \circ f^{-1} = \id_B.
\]
Conversely, a function admits an inverse iff it is bijective.
nodes.formal:
$\forall a \in A:\; f^{-1}(f(a)) = a$ and $\forall b \in B:\; f(f^{-1}(b)) = b$.\\
Converse: $(\exists g: B\to A,\; g\circ f = \id_A \wedge f\circ g = \id_B) \;\leftrightarrow\; f$ is bijective.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$\forall a \in A:\; f^{-1}(f(a)) = a$ and $\forall b \in B:\; f(f^{-1}(b)) = b$.\\
Converse: $(\exists g: B\to A,\; g\circ f = \id_A \wedge f\circ g = \id_B) \;\leftrightarrow\; f$ is bijective.

nodes.name: Constant Function
nodes.question: State the definition: Constant Function.
nodes.statement_display: $f : A \to B$ is constant if there exists $b_0 \in B$ with $f(a) = b_0$ for all $a \in A$.
nodes.formal: $f$ is constant $\;\leftrightarrow\; \exists b_0 \in B,\; \forall a \in A,\; f(a) = b_0$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $f$ is constant $\;\leftrightarrow\; \exists b_0 \in B,\; \forall a \in A,\; f(a) = b_0$.

nodes.name: Identity and Composition
nodes.question: State the theorem: Identity and Composition.
nodes.statement_display:
For any $f : A \to B$:
\[
f \circ \id_A = f
\quad\text{and}\quad
\id_B \circ f = f.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:comp-id}
For any $f : A \to B$:
\[
f \circ \id_A = f
\quad\text{and}\quad
