\id_B \circ f = f.
\]
nodes.formal: $\forall f : A \to B,\; \forall a \in A:\; (f \circ \id_A)(a) = f(a)$ and $(\id_B \circ f)(a) = f(a)$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $\forall f : A \to B,\; \forall a \in A:\; (f \circ \id_A)(a) = f(a)$ and $(\id_B \circ f)(a) = f(a)$.

nodes.name: Images and Set Operations
nodes.question: State the theorem: Images and Set Operations.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to B$ and $S, T \subseteq A$. Then
  1. $f(S \cup T) = f(S) \cup f(T)$,
  2. $f(S \cap T) \subseteq f(S) \cap f(T)$,
  3. $f(S \setminus T) \supseteq f(S) \setminus f(T)$.
Equality holds in (ii) and (iii) for all $S,T$ iff $f$ is injective.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:image-ops}
Let $f : A \to B$ and $S, T \subseteq A$. Then
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item $f(S \cup T) = f(S) \cup f(T)$,
\item $f(S \cap T) \subseteq f(S) \cap f(T)$,
\item $f(S \setminus T) \supseteq f(S) \setminus f(T)$.
\end{enumerate}
Equality holds in (ii) and (iii) for all $S,T$ iff $f$ is injective.
nodes.formal:
(i) $\forall b\in B:\; b \in f(S\cup T) \leftrightarrow (\exists a\in S,\; f(a)=b) \vee (\exists a\in T,\; f(a)=b)$.\\
(ii) $\forall b\in B:\; (\exists a\in S\cap T,\; f(a)=b) \to (\exists a\in S,\; f(a)=b) \wedge (\exists a\in T,\; f(a)=b)$; equality iff $f$ injective.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
(i) $\forall b\in B:\; b \in f(S\cup T) \leftrightarrow (\exists a\in S,\; f(a)=b) \vee (\exists a\in T,\; f(a)=b)$.\\
(ii) $\forall b\in B:\; (\exists a\in S\cap T,\; f(a)=b) \to (\exists a\in S,\; f(a)=b) \wedge (\exists a\in T,\; f(a)=b)$; equality iff $f$ injective.

nodes.name: Injectivity and Surjectivity Under Composition
nodes.question: State the theorem: Injectivity and Surjectivity Under Composition.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to B$ and $g : B \to C$.
  1. If $f$ and $g$ are injective, then $g \circ f$ is injective.
  2. If $f$ and $g$ are surjective, then $g \circ f$ is surjective.
  3. If $g \circ f$ is injective, then $f$ is injective.
  4. If $g \circ f$ is surjective, then $g$ is surjective.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:comp-inj-surj}
Let $f : A \to B$ and $g : B \to C$.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item If $f$ and $g$ are injective, then $g \circ f$ is injective.
\item If $f$ and $g$ are surjective, then $g \circ f$ is surjective.
\item If $g \circ f$ is injective, then $f$ is injective.
\item If $g \circ f$ is surjective, then $g$ is surjective.
\end{enumerate}
nodes.formal:
(i) $(\forall a_1,a_2 \in A,\; f(a_1)=f(a_2)\to a_1=a_2) \wedge (\forall b_1,b_2\in B,\; g(b_1)=g(b_2)\to b_1=b_2) \;\to\; \forall a_1,a_2\in A,\; g(f(a_1))=g(f(a_2))\to a_1=a_2$.\\
(iii) $(\forall a_1,a_2\in A,\; g(f(a_1))=g(f(a_2))\to a_1=a_2) \;\to\; \forall a_1,a_2\in A,\; f(a_1)=f(a_2)\to a_1=a_2$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
(i) $(\forall a_1,a_2 \in A,\; f(a_1)=f(a_2)\to a_1=a_2) \wedge (\forall b_1,b_2\in B,\; g(b_1)=g(b_2)\to b_1=b_2) \;\to\; \forall a_1,a_2\in A,\; g(f(a_1))=g(f(a_2))\to a_1=a_2$.\\
(iii) $(\forall a_1,a_2\in A,\; g(f(a_1))=g(f(a_2))\to a_1=a_2) \;\to\; \forall a_1,a_2\in A,\; f(a_1)=f(a_2)\to a_1=a_2$.

nodes.name: Inverse of a Composition
nodes.question: State the theorem: Inverse of a Composition.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to B$ and $g : B \to C$ be bijective. Then $g \circ f$ is
bijective and
\[
(g \circ f)^{-1} = f^{-1} \circ g^{-1}.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:inverse-comp}
Let $f : A \to B$ and $g : B \to C$ be bijective. Then $g \circ f$ is
bijective and
\[
(g \circ f)^{-1} = f^{-1} \circ g^{-1}.
\]

nodes.name: One-Sided Inverses and Function Properties
nodes.question: State the theorem: One-Sided Inverses and Function Properties.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to B$.
  1. $f$ has a left inverse $\iff$ $f$ is injective (and $A \neq \varnothing$ or $A=B=\varnothing$).
  2. $f$ has a right inverse $\iff$ $f$ is surjective.
  3. If $f$ has both a left inverse $g$ and a right inverse $h$, then $g = h$ and $f$ is bijective.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:one-sided}
Let $f : A \to B$.
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item $f$ has a left inverse $\iff$ $f$ is injective (and $A \neq \varnothing$ or $A=B=\varnothing$).
\item $f$ has a right inverse $\iff$ $f$ is surjective.
\item If $f$ has both a left inverse $g$ and a right inverse $h$, then $g = h$
and $f$ is bijective.
\end{enumerate}
nodes.formal:
(i) $(\exists g: B\to A,\; \forall a\in A,\; g(f(a))=a) \;\leftrightarrow\; \forall a_1,a_2\in A,\; f(a_1)=f(a_2)\to a_1=a_2$.\\
(ii) $(\exists h: B\to A,\; \forall b\in B,\; f(h(b))=b) \;\leftrightarrow\; \forall b\in B,\; \exists a\in A,\; f(a)=b$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
(i) $(\exists g: B\to A,\; \forall a\in A,\; g(f(a))=a) \;\leftrightarrow\; \forall a_1,a_2\in A,\; f(a_1)=f(a_2)\to a_1=a_2$.\\
(ii) $(\exists h: B\to A,\; \forall b\in B,\; f(h(b))=b) \;\leftrightarrow\; \forall b\in B,\; \exists a\in A,\; f(a)=b$.

nodes.name: Preimages Preserve Set Operations
nodes.question: State the theorem: Preimages Preserve Set Operations.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $f : A \to B$ and $S, T \subseteq B$. Then
  1. $f^{-1}(S \cup T) = f^{-1}(S) \cup f^{-1}(T)$,
  2. $f^{-1}(S \cap T) = f^{-1}(S) \cap f^{-1}(T)$,
  3. $f^{-1}(S \setminus T) = f^{-1}(S) \setminus f^{-1}(T)$,
  4. $f^{-1}(S^c) = (f^{-1}(S))^c$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:preimage-ops}
Let $f : A \to B$ and $S, T \subseteq B$. Then
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item $f^{-1}(S \cup T) = f^{-1}(S) \cup f^{-1}(T)$,
\item $f^{-1}(S \cap T) = f^{-1}(S) \cap f^{-1}(T)$,
\item $f^{-1}(S \setminus T) = f^{-1}(S) \setminus f^{-1}(T)$,
\item $f^{-1}(S^c) = (f^{-1}(S))^c$.
\end{enumerate}
nodes.formal:
(i) $\forall a \in A:\; a \in f^{-1}(S\cup T) \leftrightarrow (f(a)\in S \vee f(a)\in T)$.\\
(ii) $\forall a \in A:\; a \in f^{-1}(S\cap T) \leftrightarrow (f(a)\in S \wedge f(a)\in T)$.\\
(iv) $\forall a \in A:\; a \in f^{-1}(S^c) \leftrightarrow f(a)\notin S$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
(i) $\forall a \in A:\; a \in f^{-1}(S\cup T) \leftrightarrow (f(a)\in S \vee f(a)\in T)$.\\
(ii) $\forall a \in A:\; a \in f^{-1}(S\cap T) \leftrightarrow (f(a)\in S \wedge f(a)\in T)$.\\
(iv) $\forall a \in A:\; a \in f^{-1}(S^c) \leftrightarrow f(a)\notin S$.

nodes.name: $\leq_f$ is a partial order iff $f$ is injective
nodes.question: State the proposition: $\leq_f$ is a partial order iff $f$ is injective.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(B, \leq')$ be a partially ordered set and $f : A \to B$.
The induced order $\leq_f$ is a partial order on $A$ if and only if
$f$ is injective.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{prop:induced-poset}
Let $(B, \leq')$ be a partially ordered set and $f : A \to B$.
The induced order $\leq_f$ is a partial order on $A$ if and only if
$f$ is injective.
nodes.formal: $\leq_f$ is a partial order $\;\leftrightarrow\; \forall x,y \in A,\; f(x)\leq' f(y) \wedge f(y)\leq' f(x) \to x=y$ $\;\leftrightarrow\; \forall x,y \in A,\; f(x)=f(y) \to x=y$ (injectivity of $f$, since $\leq'$ is antisymmetric).
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $\leq_f$ is a partial order $\;\leftrightarrow\; \forall x,y \in A,\; f(x)\leq' f(y) \wedge f(y)\leq' f(x) \to x=y$ $\;\leftrightarrow\; \forall x,y \in A,\; f(x)=f(y) \to x=y$ (injectivity of $f$, since $\leq'$ is antisymmetric).

nodes.name: $\leq_f$ is always a preorder
nodes.question: State the proposition: $\leq_f$ is always a preorder.
nodes.statement_display:
For any function $f : A \to B$ and partial order $(B, \leq')$, the relation
$\leq_f$ is a preorder on $A$: it is reflexive and transitive.
nodes.formal: $\forall x \in A:\; f(x)\leq' f(x)$ (reflexivity); $\forall x,y,z \in A:\; f(x)\leq' f(y) \wedge f(y)\leq' f(z) \to f(x)\leq' f(z)$ (transitivity).
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $\forall x \in A:\; f(x)\leq' f(x)$ (reflexivity); $\forall x,y,z \in A:\; f(x)\leq' f(y) \wedge f(y)\leq' f(z) \to f(x)\leq' f(z)$ (transitivity).

nodes.name: Chain and Antichain
nodes.question: State the definition: Chain and Antichain.
nodes.statement_display: A subset $C \subseteq A$ of a poset $(A,\leq)$ is a chain if every pair of elements in $C$ is comparable. It is an antichain if no two distinct elements of $C$ are comparable.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:chain}
A subset $C \subseteq A$ of a poset $(A,\leq)$ is a \emph{chain} if every
pair of elements in $C$ is comparable. It is an \emph{antichain} if no two
distinct elements of $C$ are comparable.
nodes.formal:
$C$ is a chain $\;\leftrightarrow\;  orall a,b \in C,\; a \leq b \vee b \leq a$.\
$C$ is an antichain $\;\leftrightarrow\;  orall a,b \in C,\; a 
eq b 	o 
eg(a \leq b) \wedge 
eg(b \leq a)$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$C$ is a chain $\;\leftrightarrow\;  orall a,b \in C,\; a \leq b \vee b \leq a$.\
$C$ is an antichain $\;\leftrightarrow\;  orall a,b \in C,\; a 
eq b 	o 
eg(a \leq b) \wedge 
eg(b \leq a)$.

nodes.name: Characterisation of Supremum
nodes.question: State the proposition: Characterisation of Supremum.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(A, \leq)$ be a poset, $S \subseteq A$, and $u^* \in A$. Then $u^* = \sup S$ if and only if:
  1. $u^*$ is an upper bound of $S$; and
  2. for every $v \in A$ with $v < u^*$, there exists $s \in S$ with $v < s$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{prop:sup-char}
Let $(A, \leq)$ be a poset, $S \subseteq A$, and $u^* \in A$.  Then
$u^* = \sup S$ if and only if:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
  \item $u^*$ is an upper bound of $S$; and
  \item for every $v \in A$ with $v < u^*$, there exists $s \in S$ with
        $v < s$.
\end{enumerate}
nodes.formal:
$u^* = \sup S \;\leftrightarrow\; (\forall s \in S,\; s \leq u^*) \;\wedge\;
(\forall v \in A,\; v < u^* \;\rightarrow\; \exists s \in S,\; v < s)$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$u^* = \sup S \;\leftrightarrow\; (\forall s \in S,\; s \leq u^*) \;\wedge\;
(\forall v \in A,\; v < u^* \;\rightarrow\; \exists s \in S,\; v < s)$.

nodes.name: Comparable and Incomparable Elements
nodes.question: State the definition: Comparable and Incomparable Elements.
nodes.statement_display: In an ordered set $(A,\leq)$, elements $a,b \in A$ are comparable if $a \leq b$ or $b \leq a$; they are incomparable if neither holds.
nodes.formal:
$a,b$ comparable $\;\leftrightarrow\; a \leq b \vee b \leq a$. \quad
$a,b$ incomparable $\;\leftrightarrow\; \neg(a \leq b) \wedge \neg(b \leq a)$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$a,b$ comparable $\;\leftrightarrow\; a \leq b \vee b \leq a$. \quad
$a,b$ incomparable $\;\leftrightarrow\; \neg(a \leq b) \wedge \neg(b \leq a)$.

nodes.name: Duality Principle for Posets
nodes.question: State the proposition: Duality Principle for Posets.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\Phi$ be any first-order statement about a poset $(A, \leq)$ expressed
using only the relation $\leq$.  Let $\Phi^*$ be the statement obtained from
$\Phi$ by replacing every occurrence of $\leq$ with $\geq$ (equivalently,
working in the dual poset).  Then:
\[
  (A, \leq) \models \Phi
  \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\;
  (A, \geq) \models \Phi.
\]
In particular, if $\Phi$ is a theorem about all posets, then so is $\Phi^*$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{prop:order-duality}
Let $\Phi$ be any first-order statement about a poset $(A, \leq)$ expressed
using only the relation $\leq$.  Let $\Phi^*$ be the statement obtained from
$\Phi$ by replacing every occurrence of $\leq$ with $\geq$ (equivalently,
working in the dual poset).  Then:
\[
  (A, \leq) \models \Phi
  \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\;
  (A, \geq) \models \Phi.
\]
In particular, if $\Phi$ is a theorem about all posets, then so is $\Phi^*$.
nodes.formal:
$\forall$ posets $(A,\leq)$, $\forall$ first-order sentences $\Phi$ in the
language of $\leq$: $(A,\leq)\models\Phi \;\leftrightarrow\; (A,\geq)\models\Phi^*$,
where $\Phi^*$ substitutes $\geq$ for every $\leq$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$\forall$ posets $(A,\leq)$, $\forall$ first-order sentences $\Phi$ in the
language of $\leq$: $(A,\leq)\models\Phi \;\leftrightarrow\; (A,\geq)\models\Phi^*$,
where $\Phi^*$ substitutes $\geq$ for every $\leq$.

nodes.name: Duality of Supremum and Infimum
nodes.question: State the proposition: Duality of Supremum and Infimum.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(A, \leq)$ be a poset and $S \subseteq A$.  Then
$\inf_{(A,\leq)} S = \sup_{(A,\geq)} S$,
where $(A,\geq)$ denotes the dual poset.

In particular: if every nonempty bounded-above subset of $(A,\leq)$ has a
supremum, then every nonempty bounded-below subset of $(A,\leq)$ has an
infimum.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{prop:sup-inf-duality}
Let $(A, \leq)$ be a poset and $S \subseteq A$.  Then
$\inf_{(A,\leq)} S = \sup_{(A,\geq)} S$,
where $(A,\geq)$ denotes the dual poset.

In particular: if every nonempty bounded-above subset of $(A,\leq)$ has a
supremum, then every nonempty bounded-below subset of $(A,\leq)$ has an
infimum.
nodes.formal:
$\forall S \subseteq A$: $\ell^*$ is the infimum of $S$ in $(A,\leq)$
$\;\leftrightarrow\;$ $\ell^*$ is the supremum of $S$ in $(A,\geq)$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$\forall S \subseteq A$: $\ell^*$ is the infimum of $S$ in $(A,\leq)$
$\;\leftrightarrow\;$ $\ell^*$ is the supremum of $S$ in $(A,\geq)$.

nodes.name: Initial Segment
nodes.question: State the definition: Initial Segment.
nodes.statement_display: A subset $I \subseteq A$ is an initial segment of $(A,\leq)$ if \[ a \in I \text{ and } b \leq a \;\Rightarrow\; b \in I. \]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:initial-segment}
A subset $I \subseteq A$ is an \emph{initial segment} of $(A,\leq)$ if
\[
a \in I \text{ and } b \leq a \;\Rightarrow\; b \in I.
\]
nodes.formal: $I$ is an initial segment $\;\leftrightarrow\; \forall a \in I,\; \forall b \in A,\; b \leq a \to b \in I$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $I$ is an initial segment $\;\leftrightarrow\; \forall a \in I,\; \forall b \in A,\; b \leq a \to b \in I$.

nodes.name: Order embedding is isomorphism onto image
nodes.question: State the proposition: Order embedding is isomorphism onto image.
nodes.statement_display:
If $f : (A, \leq) \to (B, \leq')$ is an order embedding, then $f$ is an
order isomorphism from $(A, \leq)$ to the suborder $(f(A), \leq'_{f(A)})$.
nodes.formal: $\forall x,y \in A:\; x \leq y \;\leftrightarrow\; f(x) \leq'_{f(A)} f(y)$, i.e.\ $f$ is an order isomorphism $(A,\leq)\cong(f(A),\leq')$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $\forall x,y \in A:\; x \leq y \;\leftrightarrow\; f(x) \leq'_{f(A)} f(y)$, i.e.\ $f$ is an order isomorphism $(A,\leq)\cong(f(A),\leq')$.

nodes.name: Order embeddings are injective
nodes.question: State the proposition: Order embeddings are injective.
nodes.statement_display: Every order embedding $f : (A, \leq) \to (B, \leq')$ is injective.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{prop:embedding-injective}
Every order embedding $f : (A, \leq) \to (B, \leq')$ is injective.
nodes.formal: $\forall x,y \in A:\; f(x) = f(y) \to x = y$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $\forall x,y \in A:\; f(x) = f(y) \to x = y$.

nodes.name: Antisymmetric Relation
nodes.question: State the definition: Antisymmetric Relation.
nodes.statement_display: $R$ is antisymmetric if mutual relation implies equality: \[ \forall a,b \in A, \; \bigl( (a,b) \in R \land (b,a) \in R \bigr) \rightarrow a = b. \]

nodes.name: Asymmetric Relation
nodes.question: State the definition: Asymmetric Relation.
nodes.statement_display: $R$ is asymmetric if related pairs never commute: \[ \forall a,b \in A, \; (a,b) \in R \rightarrow (b,a) \notin R. \]

nodes.name: Irreflexive Relation
nodes.question: State the definition: Irreflexive Relation.
nodes.statement_display: $R$ is irreflexive if no element is related to itself: \[ \forall a \in A, \; (a,a) \notin R. \]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:irreflexive}
$R$ is \emph{irreflexive} if no element is related to itself:
\[
\forall a \in A, \; (a,a) \notin R.
\]

nodes.name: Reflexive Relation
nodes.question: State the definition: Reflexive Relation.
nodes.statement_display: A relation $R$ on $A$ is reflexive if every element is related to itself: \[ \forall a \in A, \; (a,a) \in R. \]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:reflexive}
A relation $R$ on $A$ is \emph{reflexive} if every element is related to itself:
\[
\forall a \in A, \; (a,a) \in R.
\]

nodes.name: Symmetric Relation
nodes.question: State the definition: Symmetric Relation.
nodes.statement_display: $R$ is symmetric if related pairs commute: \[ \forall a,b \in A, \; (a,b) \in R \rightarrow (b,a) \in R. \]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:symmetric}
$R$ is \emph{symmetric} if related pairs commute:
\[
\forall a,b \in A, \; (a,b) \in R \rightarrow (b,a) \in R.
\]

nodes.name: Total (Connex) Relation
nodes.question: State the definition: Total (Connex) Relation.
nodes.statement_display: $R$ is total (or connex) if every pair is comparable: \[ \forall a,b \in A, \; (a,b) \in R \lor (b,a) \in R. \]
nodes.formal:
$R$ is a preorder on $A$ iff:
\[
(\forall a \in A,\; (a,a)\in R)\;\wedge\;
(\forall a,b,c \in A,\; (a,b)\in R \wedge (b,c)\in R \to (a,c)\in R).
\]
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$R$ is a preorder on $A$ iff:
\[
(\forall a \in A,\; (a,a)\in R)\;\wedge\;
(\forall a,b,c \in A,\; (a,b)\in R \wedge (b,c)\in R \to (a,c)\in R).
\]

nodes.name: Transitive Relation
nodes.question: State the definition: Transitive Relation.
nodes.statement_display: $R$ is transitive if it extends along chains: \[ \forall a,b,c \in A, \; \bigl( (a,b) \in R \land (b,c) \in R \bigr) \rightarrow (a,c) \in R. \]

nodes.name: Uniqueness of Ordered Pairs
nodes.question: State the theorem: Uniqueness of Ordered Pairs.
nodes.statement_display:
For any sets $a,b,c,d$,
\[
(a,b) = (c,d)
\;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\;
(a = c \land b = d).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:ordered-pair-unique}
For any sets $a,b,c,d$,
\[
(a,b) = (c,d)
\;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\;
(a = c \land b = d).
\]
nodes.formal: $\forall a, b, c, d:\; (a,b) = (c,d) \;\leftrightarrow\; (a = c \wedge b = d)$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $\forall a, b, c, d:\; (a,b) = (c,d) \;\leftrightarrow\; (a = c \wedge b = d)$.

nodes.name: Intersection of $\sigma$-algebras is a $\sigma$-algebra
nodes.question: State the theorem: Intersection of $\sigma$-algebras is a $\sigma$-algebra.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $\{\mathcal{M}_\alpha\}_{\alpha \in I}$ be any family of $\sigma$-algebras
on $X$ (indexed by any set $I$). Then
\[
\mathcal{M} \;:=\; \bigcap_{\alpha \in I} \mathcal{M}_\alpha
\]
is a $\sigma$-algebra on $X$.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:sigma-intersection}
Let $\{\mathcal{M}_\alpha\}_{\alpha \in I}$ be any family of $\sigma$-algebras
on $X$ (indexed by any set $I$). Then
\[
\mathcal{M} \;:=\; \bigcap_{\alpha \in I} \mathcal{M}_\alpha
\]
is a $\sigma$-algebra on $X$.
nodes.formal:
$\bigl(\forall\alpha\in I,\; \mathcal{M}_\alpha \text{ is a $\sigma$-algebra on }X\bigr) \;\to\; \bigcap_{\alpha\in I}\mathcal{M}_\alpha \text{ is a $\sigma$-algebra on }X$.\\
Explicitly: $X\in\bigcap\mathcal{M}_\alpha$; $A\in\bigcap\mathcal{M}_\alpha \to A^c\in\bigcap\mathcal{M}_\alpha$; $\forall n,\; A_n\in\bigcap\mathcal{M}_\alpha \to \bigcup_n A_n\in\bigcap\mathcal{M}_\alpha$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$\bigl(\forall\alpha\in I,\; \mathcal{M}_\alpha \text{ is a $\sigma$-algebra on }X\bigr) \;\to\; \bigcap_{\alpha\in I}\mathcal{M}_\alpha \text{ is a $\sigma$-algebra on }X$.\\
Explicitly: $X\in\bigcap\mathcal{M}_\alpha$; $A\in\bigcap\mathcal{M}_\alpha \to A^c\in\bigcap\mathcal{M}_\alpha$; $\forall n,\; A_n\in\bigcap\mathcal{M}_\alpha \to \bigcup_n A_n\in\bigcap\mathcal{M}_\alpha$.

nodes.name: Associativity of Union and Intersection
nodes.question: State the theorem: Associativity of Union and Intersection.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A,B,C$ be sets. Then
\[
(A \cup B) \cup C = A \cup (B \cup C)
\quad\text{and}\quad
(A \cap B) \cap C = A \cap (B \cap C).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:associativity}
Let $A,B,C$ be sets. Then
\[
(A \cup B) \cup C = A \cup (B \cup C)
\quad\text{and}\quad
(A \cap B) \cap C = A \cap (B \cap C).
\]
nodes.formal: $\forall A, B, C:\; \forall x\,\bigl((x \in (A \cup B) \cup C) \leftrightarrow (x \in A \cup (B \cup C))\bigr)$, and analogously for $\cap$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $\forall A, B, C:\; \forall x\,\bigl((x \in (A \cup B) \cup C) \leftrightarrow (x \in A \cup (B \cup C))\bigr)$, and analogously for $\cap$.

nodes.name: Commutativity of Union and Intersection
nodes.question: State the theorem: Commutativity of Union and Intersection.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A,B$ be sets. Then
\[
A \cup B = B \cup A
\quad\text{and}\quad
A \cap B = B \cap A.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:commutativity}
Let $A,B$ be sets. Then
\[
A \cup B = B \cup A
\quad\text{and}\quad
A \cap B = B \cap A.
\]
nodes.formal: $\forall A, B:\; \forall x\,(x \in A \cup B \leftrightarrow x \in B \cup A)$ and $\forall x\,(x \in A \cap B \leftrightarrow x \in B \cap A)$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $\forall A, B:\; \forall x\,(x \in A \cup B \leftrightarrow x \in B \cup A)$ and $\forall x\,(x \in A \cap B \leftrightarrow x \in B \cap A)$.

nodes.name: Distributive Laws
nodes.question: State the theorem: Distributive Laws.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A,B,C$ be sets. Then
\[
A \cap (B \cup C) = (A \cap B) \cup (A \cap C),
\]
\[
A \cup (B \cap C) = (A \cup B) \cap (A \cup C).
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:distributivity}
Let $A,B,C$ be sets. Then
\[
A \cap (B \cup C) = (A \cap B) \cup (A \cap C),
\]
\[
A \cup (B \cap C) = (A \cup B) \cap (A \cup C).
\]
nodes.formal: $\forall A, B, C:\; \forall x\,\bigl(x \in A \cap (B \cup C) \leftrightarrow x \in (A \cap B) \cup (A \cap C)\bigr)$, and dually for $\cup$ over $\cap$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $\forall A, B, C:\; \forall x\,\bigl(x \in A \cap (B \cup C) \leftrightarrow x \in (A \cap B) \cup (A \cap C)\bigr)$, and dually for $\cup$ over $\cap$.

nodes.name: Identity and Absorption Laws
nodes.question: State the theorem: Identity and Absorption Laws.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $A,B$ be sets and $U$ a universe with $A \subseteq U$. Then
\[
A \cup \varnothing = A,
\qquad
A \cap U = A,
\]
\[
A \cup (A \cap B) = A,
\qquad
A \cap (A \cup B) = A.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:identity-absorption}
Let $A,B$ be sets and $U$ a universe with $A \subseteq U$. Then
\[
A \cup \varnothing = A,
\qquad
A \cap U = A,
\]
\[
A \cup (A \cap B) = A,
\qquad
A \cap (A \cup B) = A.
\]
nodes.formal:
$\forall A \subseteq U:\; \forall x\,(x \in A \cup \varnothing \leftrightarrow x \in A)$ and $\forall x\,(x \in A \cap U \leftrightarrow x \in A)$.
Absorption: $\forall A, B:\; \forall x\,(x \in A \cup (A \cap B) \leftrightarrow x \in A)$, and dually.
nodes.fully_quantified_form:
$\forall A \subseteq U:\; \forall x\,(x \in A \cup \varnothing \leftrightarrow x \in A)$ and $\forall x\,(x \in A \cap U \leftrightarrow x \in A)$.
Absorption: $\forall A, B:\; \forall x\,(x \in A \cup (A \cap B) \leftrightarrow x \in A)$, and dually.

nodes.name: Involution of Complement
nodes.question: State the theorem: Involution of Complement.
nodes.statement_display:
For any $A \subseteq U$,
\[
(A^c)^c = A.
\]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:involution}
For any $A \subseteq U$,
\[
(A^c)^c = A.
\]
nodes.formal: $\forall A \subseteq U:\; \forall x\,(x \in (A^c)^c \leftrightarrow x \in A)$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $\forall A \subseteq U:\; \forall x\,(x \in (A^c)^c \leftrightarrow x \in A)$.

nodes.name: Principle of Set Duality
nodes.question: State the corollary: Principle of Set Duality.
nodes.statement_display:
Any identity involving $\cup$, $\cap$, $\varnothing$, and $U$ that holds for
all subsets of a universe remains valid when each operation and constant is
replaced by its dual.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{cor:set-duality}
Any identity involving $\cup$, $\cap$, $\varnothing$, and $U$ that holds for
all subsets of a universe remains valid when each operation and constant is
replaced by its dual.
nodes.formal: For any identity $\forall A_1,…,A_n \subseteq U:\; E(A_i,\cup,\cap,\varnothing,U) = E'(A_i,\cup,\cap,\varnothing,U)$, the dual identity $\forall A_1,…,A_n \subseteq U:\; E^d = E'^d$ holds, where $E^d$ replaces $\cup\leftrightarrow\cap$ and $\varnothing\leftrightarrow U$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: For any identity $\forall A_1,…,A_n \subseteq U:\; E(A_i,\cup,\cap,\varnothing,U) = E'(A_i,\cup,\cap,\varnothing,U)$, the dual identity $\forall A_1,…,A_n \subseteq U:\; E^d = E'^d$ holds, where $E^d$ replaces $\cup\leftrightarrow\cap$ and $\varnothing\leftrightarrow U$.

nodes.name: Set Duality
nodes.question: State the definition: Set Duality.
nodes.statement_display: Two set-theoretic expressions over a fixed universe $U$ are dual if one is obtained from the other by simultaneously replacing $\cup \leftrightarrow \cap$ and $\varnothing \leftrightarrow U$, with complements unchanged.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:set-duality}
Two set-theoretic expressions over a fixed universe $U$ are \emph{dual} if one
is obtained from the other by simultaneously replacing
$\cup \leftrightarrow \cap$ and $\varnothing \leftrightarrow U$, with
complements unchanged.
nodes.formal: $\forall$ expressions $E, E'$ over $U$: $E$ and $E'$ are dual iff $E'$ is obtained from $E$ by the substitution $\cup \mapsto \cap,\; \cap \mapsto \cup,\; \varnothing \mapsto U,\; U \mapsto \varnothing$.
nodes.fully_quantified_form: $\forall$ expressions $E, E'$ over $U$: $E$ and $E'$ are dual iff $E'$ is obtained from $E$ by the substitution $\cup \mapsto \cap,\; \cap \mapsto \cup,\; \varnothing \mapsto U,\; U \mapsto \varnothing$.

nodes.name:
%
    Closed Iff Sequence Limits Stay In Set
nodes.question: State the sequential characterisation of closed sets.
nodes.statement_display:
$X$ is closed iff every convergent sequence in $X$ has
  its limit in $X$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$X$ is closed iff every convergent sequence in $X$ has
  its limit in $X$.
nodes.negation_display:
$(0,1)$ not closed: $1/n \in (0,1)$ but
  $1/n \to 0 \notin (0,1)$.
nodes.proof_sketch: $(\Rightarrow)$: $a_n\to x$ in $X$ gives $x\in\overline{X}$ by 9.1.14; $X$ closed gives $x\in X$. $(\Leftarrow)$: let $x\in\overline{X}$; 9.1.14 gives sequence in $X$ converging to $x$; hypothesis gives $x\in X$; $x$ arbitrary so $\overline{X}\subseteq X$.

nodes.name: Bounded Subset of $\mathbb{R}$
nodes.question: Define a bounded subset of $\mathbb{R}$.
nodes.statement_display:
$X$ is bounded if $X \subseteq [-M,M]$ for some $M > 0$,
  i.e.\ $\exists M > 0, \forall x\in X, |x|\leq M$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$X$ is bounded if $X \subseteq [-M,M]$ for some $M > 0$,
  i.e.\ $\exists M > 0, \forall x\in X, |x|\leq M$.
nodes.hint: Bounded $=$ fits inside some finite symmetric interval.
nodes.negation_display:
$\mathbb{N}$, $\mathbb{Z}$, $\mathbb{Q}$, $\mathbb{R}$
  are all unbounded.

nodes.name: Closed Subset of $\mathbb{R}$
nodes.question: Define a closed subset of $\mathbb{R}$.
nodes.statement_display:
$E \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ is closed if $\overline{E} = E$:
  every adherent point of $E$ lies in $E$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$E \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ is closed if $\overline{E} = E$:
  every adherent point of $E$ lies in $E$.
nodes.hint:
Closed $=$ contains all its limit points $=$ sequentially
  closed.
nodes.negation_display:
$(a,b)$ is not closed: $a$ and $b$ are adherent to
  $(a,b)$ but $a,b \notin (a,b)$.
implies_titles:
%
    Closed Iff Sequence Limits Stay In Set

nodes.name: Closure of a Subset of $\mathbb{R}$
nodes.question:
Define the closure $\overline{X}$ of
  $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$.
nodes.statement_display:
$\overline{X} := \{x \in \mathbb{R} :
  \forall \varepsilon > 0\;\exists y \in X: |x-y|\leq\varepsilon\}$
  — the set of all adherent points of $X$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$\overline{X} := \{x \in \mathbb{R} :
  \forall \varepsilon > 0\;\exists y \in X: |x-y|\leq\varepsilon\}$
  — the set of all adherent points of $X$.
nodes.hint:
$\overline{(a,b)} = [a,b]$;\quad
  $\overline{\mathbb{Q}} = \mathbb{R}$;\quad
  $\overline{\mathbb{Z}} = \mathbb{Z}$.
nodes.negation_display:
$\overline{\mathbb{Q}} \neq \mathbb{Q}$: every real is
  adherent to $\mathbb{Q}$ by density of $\mathbb{Q}$ in
  $\mathbb{R}$.

nodes.name:
Elementary Properties of
  Closures
nodes.question:
State the four elementary properties of closures
  (Tao Lemma 9.1.11).
nodes.statement_display:
(i) $X \subseteq \overline{X}$;\quad
  (ii) $\overline{X\cup Y}=\overline{X}\cup\overline{Y}$;\quad
  (iii) $\overline{X\cap Y}\subseteq
    \overline{X}\cap\overline{Y}$;\quad
  (iv) $X\subseteq Y\Rightarrow\overline{X}\subseteq\overline{Y}$.
nodes.statement_tex:
(i) $X \subseteq \overline{X}$;\quad
  (ii) $\overline{X\cup Y}=\overline{X}\cup\overline{Y}$;\quad
  (iii) $\overline{X\cap Y}\subseteq
    \overline{X}\cap\overline{Y}$;\quad
  (iv) $X\subseteq Y\Rightarrow\overline{X}\subseteq\overline{Y}$.
nodes.hint: (ii) is equality; (iii) is only $\subseteq$.
nodes.negation_display:
$\overline{X\cap Y} \neq \overline{X}\cap\overline{Y}$
  in general: $X=(0,1)$, $Y=(1,2)$ gives
  $\overline{X\cap Y}=\varnothing$ but
  $\overline{X}\cap\overline{Y}=\{1\}$.

nodes.name: Every Point of an Interval is a Limit Point
nodes.question: State the corollary: Every Point of an Interval is a Limit Point.

nodes.name: Heine--Borel Theorem for $\mathbb{R}$
nodes.question: Define: property $P$ is true near $x_0$.
nodes.statement_display:
There exists $\delta > 0$ such that $P(f(x), …)$
  holds for all $x \in I$ with $0 < |x - x_0| < \delta$.
  Written $P(f(x), …)\ (x \approx x_0)$.
nodes.statement_tex:
There exists $\delta > 0$ such that $P(f(x), …)$
  holds for all $x \in I$ with $0 < |x - x_0| < \delta$.
  Written $P(f(x), …)\ (x \approx x_0)$.
nodes.hint:
The $0 <$ excludes $x_0$ itself — the property need
  only hold on the punctured neighbourhood $\dot{B}_\delta(x_0)$.
  The limit definition is the special case $P \equiv
  |f(x) - y_0| < \varepsilon$.
nodes.proof_sketch: $(a)\Rightarrow(b)$: BW gives convergent subseq; closure lands limit in $X$. $(b)\Rightarrow(a)$: not-closed gives seq escaping $X$, subseq limit must be in $X$ (contradiction). Not-bounded gives $|a_n|>n$, subseq converges hence bounded (contradiction).
nodes.uses_text: def:closed-set-R, def:bounded-set-R, thm:bolzano-weierstrass, cor:closed-iff-seq-limits, prop:convergent-bounded, thm:subseq-inherit-limits

nodes.name: Sequential Characterization of Cluster Points
nodes.question:
What is an adherent point (closure point) of
  $X \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, and how does it differ from a cluster
  point?
nodes.statement_display:
An adherent point $x$ satisfies: every neighbourhood of $x$
  meets $X$ (witness $y = x$ permitted).
  A cluster point requires a \emph{distinct} witness $y \neq x$.
  Every cluster point is adherent; an isolated point is adherent
  but not a cluster point.
nodes.statement_tex:
An adherent point $x$ satisfies: every neighbourhood of $x$
  meets $X$ (witness $y = x$ permitted).
  A cluster point requires a \emph{distinct} witness $y \neq x$.
  Every cluster point is adherent; an isolated point is adherent
  but not a cluster point.
nodes.hint:
Adherent $=$ cluster $\cup$ isolated.
  The closure $\overline{X}$ is the set of all adherent points.
nodes.negation_display:
$\{2\}$ is adherent to $\{2\}$ (take $y = x = 2$) but
  $2$ is not a cluster point of $\{2\}$: no distinct witness
  exists.

nodes.name: Baire Category Theorem
nodes.question: State the theorem: Baire Category Theorem.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(X, d)$ be a complete metric space.
Then $X$ is not meager in itself: $X$ cannot be written as a countable
union of nowhere-dense sets.

Equivalently, if $X = \bigcup_{n=1}^\infty E_n$ then at least one $E_n$
has non-empty interior.
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{thm:baire-preview}
Let $(X, d)$ be a complete metric space.
Then $X$ is not meager in itself: $X$ cannot be written as a countable
union of nowhere-dense sets.

Equivalently, if $X = \bigcup_{n=1}^\infty E_n$ then at least one $E_n$
has non-empty interior.

nodes.name: Classical Heine-Borel
nodes.question: State the corollary: Classical Heine-Borel.
nodes.statement_display:
A subset $K \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$ is compact if and only if
$K$ is closed and bounded.

nodes.name: Complement characterization of nowhere-dense
nodes.question: State the proposition: Complement characterization of nowhere-dense.
nodes.statement_display:
$E \subseteq X$ is nowhere-dense if and only if $X \setminus \overline{E}$
is dense in $X$.
nodes.proof_sketch: Both conditions say the same thing about open sets: translate ``empty interior of $\overline{E}$” into ``every open set meets the complement,” which is the density criterion.
nodes.uses_text:
• Definition~nowhere dense: $\operatorname{int}(\overline{E}) = \varnothing$.
  • Proposition~dense equiv(iii): dense iff meets every open set.
  • Definition of interior: largest open subset of $\overline{E}$.

nodes.name: Generalized Heine-Borel
nodes.question: State the theorem: Generalized Heine-Borel.
nodes.statement_display:
Let $(X,d)$ be a complete metric space and $A \subseteq X$.
Then $A$ is compact if and only if $A$ is closed and totally bounded.

nodes.name: Meager Set
nodes.question: State the definition: Meager Set.
nodes.statement_display: A subset $A \subseteq X$ is meager (or of first category) if it can be written as a countable union of nowhere-dense sets: \[ A = \bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty} E_n, \quad \text{where each } E_n \text{ is nowhere-dense.} \] A set that is not meager is called non-meager (or of second category).

nodes.name: Nowhere-Dense
nodes.question: State the definition: Nowhere-Dense.
nodes.statement_display: Let $(X,d)$ be a metric space and $E \subseteq X$. We say $E$ is nowhere-dense in $X$ if the interior of its closure is empty: \[ \operatorname{int}(\overline{E}) = \varnothing. \]
nodes.statement_tex:
\label{def:nowhere-dense}
Let $(X,d)$ be a metric space and $E \subseteq X$.
We say $E$ is \emph{nowhere-dense in $X$} if the interior of its closure
is empty:
\[
\operatorname{int}(\overline{E}) = \varnothing.
\]

nodes.name: Addition Formulas
nodes.question: State the addition formula for $\sin(x+y)$.
nodes.statement_display: $\sin(x+y) = \sin x \cos y + \cos x \sin y$.
nodes.statement_tex: $\sin(x+y) = \sin x \cos y + \cos x \sin y$.
nodes.hint:
``Both same, both swap'' — $\sin\cos + \cos\sin$ for sine;
$\cos\cos - \sin\sin$ for cosine.

nodes.name: Inverse Trigonometric Functions
nodes.question: State the fundamental trigonometric limit and its proof method.
nodes.statement_display:
$\lim_{x\to 0}\sin x/x = 1$.
Proof: geometric squeeze $\cos x \leq \sin x / x \leq 1$, then
$\cos x \to 1$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$\lim_{x\to 0}\sin x/x = 1$.
Proof: geometric squeeze $\cos x \leq \sin x / x \leq 1$, then
$\cos x \to 1$.
nodes.hint:
$\sin x \approx x$ near $0$. This is the first-order Taylor
approximation.
nodes.negation_display:
$\lim_{x\to 0}\sin(ax)/x = a$, not $1$ — the limit of
$\sin(ax)/(ax) = 1$ but $\sin(ax)/x = a \cdot \sin(ax)/(ax) \to a$.

nodes.name: Pythagorean Identity
nodes.question: State and sketch-prove the Pythagorean identity.
nodes.statement_display:
$\sin^2 x + \cos^2 x = 1$. Proof: the derivative is $0$ and
the value at $0$ is $1$, so it is constantly $1$.
nodes.statement_tex:
$\sin^2 x + \cos^2 x = 1$. Proof: the derivative is $0$ and
the value at $0$ is $1$, so it is constantly $1$.

nodes.name: Second Fundamental Trigonometric Limit
nodes.question: What is $\lim_{x\to 0}(1-\cos x)/x^2$?
nodes.hint: Write $1-\cos x = 2\sin^2(x/2)$ and apply $\sin t/t \to 1$.

nodes.name: Tangent, Cotangent, Secant, Cosecant
nodes.question: Define $\tan x$ and state its Pythagorean identity.
nodes.statement_display: $\tan x = \sin x / \cos x$. Identity: $1 + \tan^2 x = \sec^2 x$.
nodes.statement_tex: $\tan x = \sin x / \cos x$. Identity: $1 + \tan^2 x = \sec^2 x$.
nodes.hint: Divide $\sin^2 x + \cos^2 x = 1$ through by $\cos^2 x$.

nodes.name: $\mathbb{F}^S$ --- the function space over a set
nodes.question: Define $\mathbb{F}^S$.
nodes.statement_display: The vector space of all functions $f:S\to\mathbb{F}$ under pointwise addition and scalar multiplication.
nodes.statement_tex: The vector space of all functions $f:S\to\mathbb{F}$ under pointwise addition and scalar multiplication.
nodes.formal: \[ \mathbb{F}^S := \{f:S\to\mathbb{F}\}\text{ with pointwise ops}. \]
nodes.fully_quantified_form: \[ \mathbb{F}^S := \{f:S\to\mathbb{F}\}\text{ with pointwise ops}. \]

nodes.name: $\mathbb{F}^{\infty}$ --- the space of sequences
nodes.question: Define $\mathbb{F}^\infty$.
nodes.statement_display: The vector space of all infinite sequences $(x_1,x_2,…)$ with $x_i\in\mathbb{F}$.
nodes.statement_tex: The vector space of all infinite sequences $(x_1,x_2,…)$ with $x_i\in\mathbb{F}$.
nodes.formal: \[ \mathbb{F}^\infty := \{(x_1,x_2,…): x_i\in\mathbb{F}\}. \]
nodes.fully_quantified_form: \[ \mathbb{F}^\infty := \{(x_1,x_2,…): x_i\in\mathbb{F}\}. \]

nodes.name: $n$-tuple
nodes.question: Define an $n$-tuple.
nodes.statement_display: An ordered list $(x_1,…,x_n)$ with $x_i\in X_i$.
nodes.statement_tex: An ordered list $(x_1,…,x_n)$ with $x_i\in X_i$.
nodes.formal: \[ (x_1,…,x_n)\in X_1\times\cdots\times X_n. \]
nodes.fully_quantified_form: \[ (x_1,…,x_n)\in X_1\times\cdots\times X_n. \]

nodes.name: Abelian group
nodes.question: Define an abelian group.
nodes.statement_display: A set $G$ with $+$ satisfying closure, associativity, identity, inverses, and commutativity.
nodes.statement_tex: A set $G$ with $+$ satisfying closure, associativity, identity, inverses, and commutativity.
nodes.formal: \[ (G,+)\text{ abelian group} \iff \text{closure, assoc, identity, inverses, commutativity}. \]
nodes.fully_quantified_form: \[ (G,+)\text{ abelian group} \iff \text{closure, assoc, identity, inverses, commutativity}. \]

nodes.name: Complex Numbers
nodes.question: Define the complex numbers.
nodes.statement_display: $\mathbb{C}=\{a+bi:a,b\in\mathbb{R}\}$ where $i^2=-1$.
nodes.statement_tex: $\mathbb{C}=\{a+bi:a,b\in\mathbb{R}\}$ where $i^2=-1$.
nodes.formal: \[ \mathbb{C} := \{a+bi : a,b\in\mathbb{R}\},\; i^2=-1. \]
nodes.fully_quantified_form: \[ \mathbb{C} := \{a+bi : a,b\in\mathbb{R}\},\; i^2=-1. \]

nodes.name: Coordinate
nodes.question: Define a coordinate.
nodes.statement_display: The $i$-th entry $x_i$ of an $n$-tuple $(x_1,…,x_n)$.
nodes.statement_tex: The $i$-th entry $x_i$ of an $n$-tuple $(x_1,…,x_n)$.
nodes.formal: \[ \text{The }i\text{-th coordinate of }(x_1,…,x_n)\text{ is }x_i. \]
nodes.fully_quantified_form: \[ \text{The }i\text{-th coordinate of }(x_1,…,x_n)\text{ is }x_i. \]

nodes.name: Coordinate space as a function space
nodes.question: How is $\mathbb{F}^n$ a function space?
nodes.statement_display: $\mathbb{F}^n$ is identified with functions $\{1,…,n\}\to\mathbb{F}$; the $i$-th coordinate is $f(i)$.
nodes.statement_tex: $\mathbb{F}^n$ is identified with functions $\{1,…,n\}\to\mathbb{F}$; the $i$-th coordinate is $f(i)$.
nodes.formal: \[ \mathbb{F}^n \cong \mathbb{F}^{\{1,…,n\}}. \]
nodes.fully_quantified_form: \[ \mathbb{F}^n \cong \mathbb{F}^{\{1,…,n\}}. \]

nodes.name: Field --- structural form
nodes.question: Define a field (structural form).
nodes.statement_display: A field $(F,+,\cdot)$ is an abelian group under $+$ and $\cdot$ (on $F^*$) with distributivity.
nodes.statement_tex: A field $(F,+,\cdot)$ is an abelian group under $+$ and $\cdot$ (on $F^*$) with distributivity.
nodes.formal: \[ (F,+,\cdot)\text{ field} \iff (F,+)\text{ abelian group},\; (F^*,\cdot)\text{ abelian group},\; \text{distributivity}. \]
nodes.fully_quantified_form: \[ (F,+,\cdot)\text{ field} \iff (F,+)\text{ abelian group},\; (F^*,\cdot)\text{ abelian group},\; \text{distributivity}. \]

nodes.name: Finite list
nodes.question: Define a finite list.
nodes.statement_display: A finite ordered sequence of elements from a set $X$.
nodes.statement_tex: A finite ordered sequence of elements from a set $X$.
nodes.formal: \[ \text{A finite list of length }n\text{ is an element of }X^n. \]
nodes.fully_quantified_form: \[ \text{A finite list of length }n\text{ is an element of }X^n. \]

nodes.name: Scalar Multiplication by Zero and Negation
nodes.question: State scalar multiplication by zero and negation.
nodes.statement_display: $0v=\mathbf{0}$, $(-1)v=-v$, and $\lambda\mathbf{0}=\mathbf{0}$ for all $v\in V$, $\lambda\in\mathbb{F}$.
nodes.statement_tex: $0v=\mathbf{0}$, $(-1)v=-v$, and $\lambda\mathbf{0}=\mathbf{0}$ for all $v\in V$, $\lambda\in\mathbb{F}$.
nodes.formal: \[ 0\cdot v=\mathbf{0},\quad (-1)\cdot v=-v,\quad \lambda\cdot\mathbf{0}=\mathbf{0}. \]
nodes.fully_quantified_form: \[ 0\cdot v=\mathbf{0},\quad (-1)\cdot v=-v,\quad \lambda\cdot\mathbf{0}=\mathbf{0}. \]

nodes.name: Set
nodes.question: Define a set (linear algebra context).
nodes.statement_display: An unordered collection of distinct elements; order and repetition do not matter.
nodes.statement_tex: An unordered collection of distinct elements; order and repetition do not matter.
nodes.formal: \[ \text{An unordered collection of distinct elements.} \]
nodes.fully_quantified_form: \[ \text{An unordered collection of distinct elements.} \]

nodes.name: Set Exponentiation
nodes.question: Define set exponentiation $X^S$.
nodes.statement_display: $X^S$ is the set of all functions from $S$ to $X$.
nodes.statement_tex: $X^S$ is the set of all functions from $S$ to $X$.
nodes.formal: \[ X^S := \{f : S\to X\},\; |X^S|=|X|^{|S|}\text{ (finite case)}. \]
nodes.fully_quantified_form: \[ X^S := \{f : S\to X\},\; |X^S|=|X|^{|S|}\text{ (finite case)}. \]

nodes.name: Uniqueness of the Additive Identity
nodes.question: State uniqueness of the additive identity.
nodes.statement_display: A vector space has a unique additive identity $\mathbf{0}$.
nodes.statement_tex: A vector space has a unique additive identity $\mathbf{0}$.
nodes.formal: \[ \forall V\text{ vector space}: \exists!\,\mathbf{0}\in V,\;\forall v,\;v+\mathbf{0}=v. \]
nodes.fully_quantified_form: \[ \forall V\text{ vector space}: \exists!\,\mathbf{0}\in V,\;\forall v,\;v+\mathbf{0}=v. \]

nodes.name: Uniqueness of the Additive Inverse
nodes.question: State uniqueness of the additive inverse.
nodes.statement_display: Every vector $v$ has a unique additive inverse $-v$.
nodes.statement_tex: Every vector $v$ has a unique additive inverse $-v$.
nodes.formal: \[ \forall v\in V,\;\exists!\,(-v)\in V:\;v+(-v)=\mathbf{0}. \]
nodes.fully_quantified_form: \[ \forall v\in V,\;\exists!\,(-v)\in V:\;v+(-v)=\mathbf{0}. \]

nodes.name: Vector Space
nodes.question: State the definition of a vector space.
nodes.statement_display: A set $V$ over field $\mathbb{F}$ with $+$ and scalar mult satisfying the 8 vector space axioms.
nodes.statement_tex: A set $V$ over field $\mathbb{F}$ with $+$ and scalar mult satisfying the 8 vector space axioms.
nodes.formal: \[ V\text{ vector space over }\mathbb{F} \iff \text{8 axioms: 4 additive, 4 scalar mult}. \]
nodes.fully_quantified_form: \[ V\text{ vector space over }\mathbb{F} \iff \text{8 axioms: 4 additive, 4 scalar mult}. \]

nodes.name: Vector Space --- informal description
nodes.question: Describe a vector space informally.
nodes.statement_display: A set of objects (vectors) closed under addition and scalar multiplication over a field.
nodes.statement_tex: A set of objects (vectors) closed under addition and scalar multiplication over a field.
nodes.formal: \[ \text{A set closed under scalar multiplication and vector addition.} \]
nodes.fully_quantified_form: \[ \text{A set closed under scalar multiplication and vector addition.} \]

nodes.name: Zero vector
nodes.question: Define the zero vector.
nodes.statement_display: The unique element $\mathbf{0}\in V$ with $v+\mathbf{0}=v$ for all $v\in V$.
nodes.statement_tex: The unique element $\mathbf{0}\in V$ with $v+\mathbf{0}=v$ for all $v\in V$.
nodes.formal: \[ \exists!\, \mathbf{0}\in V: \forall v\in V,\; v+\mathbf{0}=v. \]
nodes.fully_quantified_form: \[ \exists!\, \mathbf{0}\in V: \forall v\in V,\; v+\mathbf{0}=v. \]
